drafts.body_md:
# Custard

**Cream and milk, warmed. Yolks and sugar, whisked pale. Temper. Back to the pan. Stir constantly to 82°C — not 83, not 85. Strain. Ice bath. This is the base recipe every serious dessert on a menu leans on, and the failure window is two degrees wide.**

Custard is the most fundamental, least forgiving preparation in the Western dessert kitchen. Done well, a crème anglaise is a silk sauce that glazes a spoon and tastes like the essence of dairy-and-vanilla; the same preparation five degrees hotter is scrambled eggs in sweet milk. This version sets the technique bulletproof — ratios, temperatures, the stir, the strain, the stop — and shows the three branches it supports: crème anglaise (stirred, pouring), pastry cream (stabilised with starch, pipeable), and the sous-vide method that removes the anxiety entirely.

---

## AT A GLANCE

| Yield | Active Time | Total Time | Difficulty |
|-------|-------------|------------|------------|
| 750 ml crème anglaise / 8 servings | 25 min | 45 min + chill | Medium — the window is narrow |

**Source:** Harold McGee (*On Food and Cooking*, egg coagulation science), Jacques Pépin (*La Technique*, classical method), J. Kenji López-Alt (*The Food Lab*, temperature targets), Paula Figoni (*How Baking Works*, starch stabilisation), Thomas Keller (*The French Laundry Cookbook*, ratios).

**Key technique:** Temper-and-cook a 1:4 yolk-to-dairy custard to **82°C (180°F)** internal, hold briefly at nappe stage, strain immediately, shock in an ice bath. The target window for stirred custard is 80-84°C — below and it pours like milk, above and it curdles.

---

## INGREDIENTS

*Organized by mise en place groups — prep in this order*

### GROUP A: Crème Anglaise Base

*The stirred custard. The backbone of the technique.*

| Ingredient | Weight | Volume | Notes |
|------------|--------|--------|-------|
| Whole milk | 250 ml | 1 cup | Full fat — skimmed thins the texture |
| Heavy cream (35%+) | 250 ml | 1 cup | UHT acceptable at sea; fresh better |
| Large egg yolks | 6 | ~110 g | No whites — whites coagulate at 63°C and wreck the custard |
| Granulated sugar | 100 g | ½ cup | Caster if you have it; dissolves faster |
| Fine sea salt | 1 g | ⅛ tsp | Yes, salt in custard |
| Vanilla bean | 1 pod | — | Seeds + pod, or 10 g paste |

> **Why whole milk + cream and not all cream?** All-cream custard tastes flat — too much butterfat mutes the vanilla and the dairy sweetness. The 50:50 split is classical for a reason: enough fat for texture and mouthfeel, enough milk solids (lactose and whey) to carry flavour.

### GROUP B: Pastry Cream Variation

*Same custard, stabilised with starch. Holds shape, pipes, tolerates being folded into cream for a Chiboust or diplomat.*

| Ingredient | Weight | Volume | Notes |
|------------|--------|--------|-------|
| Whole milk | 500 ml | 2 cups | Usually all-milk, or 80:20 milk:cream |
| Large egg yolks | 4 | ~75 g | Fewer yolks than anglaise — starch adds body |
| Granulated sugar | 100 g | ½ cup | |
| Cornstarch | 40 g | ⅓ cup | OR 50 g flour — cornstarch is cleaner |
| Fine sea salt | 1 g | ⅛ tsp | |
| Vanilla bean or paste | 1 pod / 10 g | — | |
| Unsalted butter | 30 g | 2 tbsp | **Off heat**, stirred in — richness and sheen |

### GROUP C: Flavour Variations

*Applied to crème anglaise base (double for pastry cream)*

| Variation | How | Notes |
|-----------|-----|-------|
| **Coffee** | Infuse 20 g freshly ground coffee in warm dairy 15 min, strain | Espresso grind; skip if bitter |
| **Chocolate** | Pour hot finished anglaise over 150 g chopped 70% chocolate, stand 1 min, whisk | Makes chocolate sauce / ganache-anglaise |
| **Praliné / gianduja** | 100 g paste whisked in while warm | Classical pair with poached pear |
| **Cardamom / tea** | Infuse 8 crushed pods or 2 tbsp tea in dairy, strain | Chai, Earl Grey, hojicha all work |
| **Citrus** | Zest of 2 oranges or lemons in dairy, infuse, strain | Never add citrus juice — it curdles the custard |
| **Rum / Cognac / Armagnac** | 20-30 ml stirred in cold, at service | Raw alcohol; do not cook off |
| **Saffron** | 10 threads bloomed in 15 ml hot water, whisked in | A pinch goes far |
| **Brown butter** | Replace 100 ml cream with equal brown butter | Keller's technique — deep nutty backbone |

### GROUP D: Sous-Vide Method (the bulletproof version)

*Same ingredients as Group A. No stovetop. No curdle risk. Use when the galley is chaotic or when a junior hand is cooking.*

See Elevation Tier 2 for method.

### EQUIPMENT NEEDED

- Heavy-bottomed saucepan, 3 L — **light-coloured interior** (you need to read the colour change)
- Silicone spatula OR wooden spoon
- **Instant-read thermometer** — non-negotiable for stirred custard
- Fine-mesh strainer / chinois
- Bowl set over ice bath, large enough for the pot to nest
- Whisk (for the yolks, not the custard in the pan)
- Digital scale
- **For sous-vide:** immersion circulator, vacuum bags or silicone zip bags with water displacement

---

## METHOD — CRÈME ANGLAISE (classical stovetop)

### PHASE 1: Infuse the Dairy
*10 minutes — unattended once on heat*

**1.** Split the vanilla pod lengthwise. Scrape seeds into a heavy saucepan. Drop the pod in too — the pod carries most of the aroma.

**2.** Add milk and cream. Bring to a bare simmer over medium heat — **steaming, small bubbles at the edge, not rolling**. Pull off, cover, steep **10 minutes**. For coffee, tea, citrus zest, cardamom, saffron — infuse now in the warm dairy, strain before proceeding.

> **Why steep, not boil?** Vanilla aroma compounds are heat-sensitive — a rolling boil drives off the high notes. A gentle steep extracts maximum flavour without damage. The same rule applies to coffee (bitter above 90°C), tea (tannin extraction accelerates and turns astringent), and citrus zest (volatile oils boil off fast).

### PHASE 2: Build the Base
*5 minutes*

**3.** In a separate bowl, whisk egg yolks with sugar and salt until the mixture is pale yellow and ribbons off the whisk — **2-3 minutes vigorously**. This is not cosmetic. Whisking incorporates the sugar into the yolks, dissolving the granules and — more importantly — **coating the proteins in sugar molecules that raise their coagulation temperature by 10-15°C**. An under-whisked yolk is a pre-scrambled yolk.

**4.** Re-warm the infused dairy to a steam (80-85°C). Pour a third of it into the yolk mixture in a thin stream, **whisking constantly**. This is tempering — raising the temperature of the yolks gradually so they don't hit a thermal shock and coagulate on contact.

**5.** Pour the tempered yolks back into the pan with the remaining dairy. Whisk once to combine, then switch to a silicone spatula.

### PHASE 3: The Stir
*5-8 minutes — the only phase where you cannot look away*

**6.** Return the pan to medium-low heat. From this point, you **stir continuously** in a figure-eight pattern that sweeps the bottom and corners of the pan. Do not whisk — whisking aerates and prevents you from reading the texture.

> **Why figure-eight?** The coagulation danger zone is the pan bottom and corners, where direct heat concentrates. A straight back-and-forth stir leaves dead zones. The figure-eight with corner sweeps means every square centimetre of the pan is scraped every two or three seconds. This is the move that separates a home custard from a restaurant one.

**7.** As the custard approaches **75°C**, it will visibly thicken — the spatula leaves a clean trail on the pan bottom, and the surface loses its froth and becomes glossy. From here, two to three more minutes, still stirring.

**8.** The target is **82°C (180°F)**. Check with a thermometer held in the liquid, not touching the pan. The "nappe" test — coat the back of the spatula with custard, draw a line with your fingertip, the line should hold — happens at 82°C.

> **The window:** 80°C is thin and just-set. 82°C is the sweet spot. 84°C is the edge — you will feel the custard begin to grain. Above 85°C, the yolk proteins have contracted, squeezed out their water, and coagulated into visible curds. There is no recovery from a fully curdled custard. Sieve it and move on.

### PHASE 4: The Stop
*30 seconds — critical*

**9.** The moment the thermometer reads 82°C, **remove the pan from the heat immediately** and **pour the custard through the strainer into a bowl set over an ice bath.** Do not stop at the heat — the residual pan temperature will carry the custard over 84°C in seconds.

> **Why strain?** Even a perfect custard has a small amount of coagulated albumen (from the chalazae — the twisted white cords attached to the yolk) and tiny fragments from the bottom of the pan. Straining removes these. It is also the single best insurance against a slightly-overcooked batch — minor graininess passes through a chinois.

**10.** Stir the custard over the ice bath for **1-2 minutes** to drop the temperature below 30°C and stop all further cooking. Once cool, refrigerate.

### PHASE 5: Rest
*2 hours minimum*

**11.** Chill **at least 2 hours**, ideally overnight. The flavour develops significantly in the cold rest — vanilla deepens, the custard settles, and the texture firms from "pourable warm" to "glazes a spoon cold." This is not optional for ice cream base; for immediate use as a sauce, a 30-minute chill is acceptable.

**12.** Before serving, whisk briefly to restore the uniform texture, and thin with a splash of milk or cream if it has set too firm.

---

## METHOD — PASTRY CREAM (crème pâtissière)

*Same spine, starch added, different target.*

**1.** Infuse milk with vanilla as in Phase 1.

**2.** Whisk yolks, sugar, salt, **and cornstarch** together. The starch is whisked in now, raw.

**3.** Temper with one-third of hot milk, return to pan.

**4.** Over medium heat, **whisk constantly** (now you can whisk — starch prevents the yolks from seizing). The mixture thickens abruptly around 82-85°C as starch granules gelatinise. **Continue whisking and bring to a bare boil for 90 seconds** — this is critical: it deactivates an enzyme in the yolk (alpha-amylase) that would otherwise break down the starch and turn your pastry cream runny over 24 hours.

> **Why boil pastry cream but not anglaise?** Starch raises the coagulation ceiling of the egg proteins — the starch granules physically separate the protein chains so they cannot aggregate into curds. You can boil pastry cream; you cannot boil anglaise. The 90-second boil is the amylase kill step; without it, pastry cream held overnight turns into soup.

**5.** Off heat, whisk in cold butter cubes — richness and sheen.

**6.** Strain through a chinois, press plastic wrap **directly onto the surface** to prevent a skin, chill.

**7.** Before use, whisk hard for 30 seconds to restore smoothness — pastry cream stiffens dramatically in the fridge and needs re-homogenising.

---

## THE SCIENCE

*Why this recipe works*

### Egg Yolk Coagulation = A 15°C Window

Egg yolk proteins begin denaturing at 65°C and fully coagulate by 70°C in pure water. Custard protein coagulation is delayed because the proteins are diluted in dairy and shielded by sugar and fat — the practical window is 75-85°C. At 75°C, the custard is thin. At 82°C, the proteins have just cross-linked enough to thicken without aggregating into visible curds. At 85°C and above, the cross-links tighten and water is squeezed out, producing the grainy "sweet scrambled eggs" failure mode.

**What's happening:** Yolk proteins (primarily livetins and low-density lipoproteins) unfold and form a weak three-dimensional network that traps water. Sugar interferes with protein-protein bonding, widening the window. Starch, in pastry cream, extends the window further by physical separation.

**Why it matters:** Custard is one of the few preparations where a two-degree error is visible on the plate. The thermometer is not optional. Once you've made a hundred, you can read the texture — until then, verify.

*Source: Harold McGee, On Food and Cooking, 2004, pp. 85-88, 97-98.*

### Tempering = Thermal Shock Prevention

Pouring hot dairy directly into cold yolks produces instant scrambled eggs on contact. Tempering — streaming a portion of hot dairy into the whisked yolks while whisking — raises the yolk temperature gradually, giving the proteins time to unfold rather than seize.

**What's happening:** Protein denaturation is irreversible once the tertiary structure collapses. Gradual heating gives proteins time to unfold and re-form into a weak matrix; sudden heating collapses them into aggregates.

**Why it matters:** Every curdled custard in the world is either an under-whisked yolk, an un-tempered addition, or a too-hot pan. Two of the three are fixed by tempering.

*Source: Jacques Pépin, La Technique, 1976; confirmed by Kenji López-Alt, "The Food Lab: How to Make the Best Custard," Serious Eats, 2015.*

### Sugar = Protein Protection

Sugar is not only sweetener — it is a coagulation delayer. Sucrose molecules bind to protein surfaces via hydrogen bonding and raise the temperature at which proteins can aggregate. A custard with 100 g sugar coagulates at 82°C; the same custard with 50 g coagulates at 78°C; sugar-free custard coagulates at 72°C.

**What's happening:** Sucrose hydrates the protein surface, competing with protein-protein bonds. More sugar = wider safety margin.

**Why it matters:** The ratio of sugar to yolk is structural, not stylistic. A reduced-sugar custard needs a lower target temperature. Do not pull sugar below 80 g per 6 yolks unless you move to sous-vide.

*Source: Harold McGee, On Food and Cooking, 2004, pp. 97-98; Paula Figoni, How Baking Works, 2010, Chapter 10.*

### Starch = Permission to Boil (Pastry Cream Only)

In pastry cream, cornstarch or flour physically separates the egg proteins from each other, raising the effective coagulation ceiling above 100°C. This lets you bring pastry cream to a full boil — which is not just tolerated but required, because egg yolks contain alpha-amylase, an enzyme that slowly breaks down starch and would liquefy your pastry cream over 24 hours if not destroyed.

**What's happening:** Alpha-amylase is heat-stable up to ~82°C; it is destroyed by sustained boiling. Unboiled pastry cream (many home recipes stop at "thickened" around 82°C) is amylase-active and liquefies in the fridge.

**Why it matters:** If you have ever made pastry cream that was perfect in the evening and slumped into soup by morning, you stopped cooking too early. 90 seconds at a full boil, whisking constantly, is the kill step.

*Source: Paula Figoni, How Baking Works, 2010, Chapter 10; Harold McGee, On Food and Cooking, p. 101.*

### Ice Bath Stop = Arresting the Carryover

A hot pan holds significant thermal mass. A custard pulled at 82°C and left in the pan will climb to 85-86°C from residual heat alone within 30 seconds. The ice bath plus the cold strainer mass absorb this residual energy and hold the custard at its target.

**What's happening:** Carryover cooking is thermal diffusion from the pan + liquid mass into the custard. Separating the custard from the hot pan and cooling it immediately stops the climb.

**Why it matters:** Skip the ice bath on a hot galley, and your perfectly-pulled custard overcooks in the bowl. The strainer and the ice bath together are the difference between 82°C final and 86°C final.

*Source: J. Kenji López-Alt, Serious Eats, 2015.*

---

## ELEVATION

*How to make this recipe unforgettable*

### TIER 1: Simple Upgrades (No Extra Time)

| Modification | What It Does | How |
|--------------|--------------|-----|
| **Tahitian vanilla** | Floral, cherry-cola notes Bourbon doesn't carry | Use a Tahitian pod instead of Bourbon |
| **Brown sugar swap** | Molasses-caramel depth | Replace 30 g of the sugar with light brown |
| **Pinch of citric acid** | Brightness without curdling | ⅛ tsp dissolved in cold milk before tempering |
| **Add a second yolk** | Richer, thicker, ice-cream-ready | 7 yolks instead of 6 |
| **Malt powder** | Horlicks-style register | 10 g barley malt syrup whisked in cold |

### TIER 2: Worth the Extra Effort

| Modification | What It Does | Extra Time | How |
|--------------|--------------|------------|-----|
| **Sous-vide method** | Bulletproof, no curdle risk | +60 min | See sub-recipe below |
| **Caramelised cream** | Toffee-register crème anglaise | +10 min | Caramelise 40 g sugar dry, deglaze with cream off heat, incorporate |
| **Brown-butter base** | Deep nutty backbone | +8 min | Replace 100 ml cream with brown butter (made, cooled to warm) |
| **Infused duck-egg yolks** | Richer yellow, more viscous | +0 min | Substitute 4 duck yolks for 6 hen yolks, 1:1.5 by weight |
| **Make crème brûlée** | Baked-custard direction from same base | +45 min | Fill ramekins, bain-marie, 150°C 30-40 min, brûlée top at service |

### TIER 3: Restaurant-Level

| Modification | What It Does | Extra Time | Notes |
|--------------|--------------|------------|-------|
| **Frozen custard / ice cream** | Churn the anglaise | +4 hr | 500-750 ml anglaise → ice cream in 20-30 min churn + 4 hr harden |
| **Pot de crème** | Baked custard en cocotte, espresso-cup format | +45 min | Same base, ramekins, bain-marie 150°C 25-35 min; jiggle test |
| **Custard foam (espuma)** | iSi-charged light sauce | +30 min | Fine-strained anglaise in iSi, 1 N₂O charge, refrigerate 1 hr |
| **Ile flottante** | Classical | +30 min | Anglaise + poached sweetened meringue quenelles + caramel |
| **Dulce-de-leche custard** | Reverse-direction caramel | +3 hr (condensed milk confit) | Replace sugar with 150 g dulce de leche |
| **Sabayon direction** | Fortified-wine warm custard | +10 min | Marsala or Moscato replaces 100 ml cream, whisked over bain-marie |

### SUB-RECIPE: Sous-Vide Crème Anglaise (Tier 2 — the unfailable method)

| Ingredient | Same as Group A |
|------------|-----------------|

**Method:** Set immersion circulator to **82°C (180°F)**. Whisk yolks, sugar, salt in a bowl. Whisk in cold dairy and vanilla seeds (drop the pod in too). Transfer to a vacuum bag or silicone zip bag (water-displacement seal). Cook at 82°C for **45 minutes**, gently agitating or massaging the bag every 10-15 minutes to prevent settling. Pour out through a chinois, chill in an ice bath. Done.

**Why it works:** 82°C water holds the custard at exactly 82°C — no pan gradient, no carryover, no chance of curdle. The 45-minute hold is long enough to fully coagulate the yolks and pasteurise them without aggregating. The texture is slightly less silky than a perfectly-pulled stovetop version but the failure rate is zero. On a busy charter this is the right call.

*Source: ChefSteps, "The Best Crème Anglaise," 2016; Modernist Cuisine, 2011, Vol. 4.*

### SUB-RECIPE: Brown-Butter Vanilla Ice Cream (from this custard)

**To the base recipe, add:**

| Ingredient | Weight |
|------------|--------|
| Unsalted butter (browned dark) | 60 g |
| Additional yolks | 1 (total 7) |

**Method:** Brown 60 g butter deep and dark (8-10 min), scrape every brown bit into the cream during Phase 1. Use 7 yolks instead of 6. Proceed as base recipe. Chill overnight minimum. Churn per manufacturer. Hardens in 4 hours. The single best vanilla ice cream you will make.

---

## MISE EN PLACE & PREP-AHEAD

*For professional service and galley-scale production*

### WHAT CAN BE PREPPED AHEAD

| Component | How Far Ahead | Storage | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|---------|-------|
| Infused dairy | 24 hr | Refrigerated | Re-warm before tempering |
| Whisked yolks + sugar | 30 min | Room temp | Longer and the sugar burns the yolks |
| Finished crème anglaise | 3 days fridge | Airtight, plastic wrap on surface | Flavour peaks at 24 hr |
| Finished pastry cream | 3 days fridge | Plastic wrap directly on surface | Whisk before use |
| Sous-vide anglaise (still sealed) | 1 week fridge | In original bag | Never frozen — ice crystals break the emulsion |
| Pot de crème (baked, in ramekins) | 3 days fridge | Sealed with film | Serve chilled or room temp |
| Custard ice cream base (churned and hardened) | 2 months freezer | Airtight | Temper 5-10 min before scooping |

### THE GALLEY HACK: The Morning Batch + Three Desserts

**For charter service:**

1. Morning: make 1 L of sous-vide crème anglaise (double the base recipe).
2. 250 ml → sauce for that night's plated dessert, held chilled.
3. 500 ml → churned into ice cream mid-afternoon, hardened for service.
4. 250 ml → into a blast-chilled pot de crème ramekin set for lunch day 2.

One technique, three desserts, one pot of ice cream, zero curdle risk.

### BATCH SCALING

| Component | ×1 (750 ml) | ×2 (1.5 L) | ×4 (charter scale) | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|------------|---------------------|-------|
| Dairy total | 500 ml | 1 L | 2 L | Ratio holds |
| Yolks | 6 | 12 | 24 | |
| Sugar | 100 g | 200 g | 400 g | |
| Stovetop stir time | 5-8 min | 10-14 min | Two pots — do not go above 1.5 L per pot | Bigger pots = dead zones at corners |
| Sous-vide time | 45 min | 60 min | 75 min | Scales with bag thickness |
| Target temp | 82°C | 82°C | 82°C | Never changes |

### FREEZER STRATEGY

**Ice cream (from base):**
- Chill fully churned and hardened in a lidded container
- Up to 2 months
- Temper 5-10 min before scooping
- A splash of vodka in the base (20 ml per litre) prevents over-hardening without flavouring

**Custard sauce itself — do not freeze:**
- Anglaise freezes into a grainy, broken mess. The water crystallises out of the emulsion.
- Churn it into ice cream if you want long storage.

**Pastry cream — do not freeze:**
- Starch retrogrades in the freezer. Texture becomes powdery and weeping. 3 days fridge, maximum.

### SERVICE FORMATS

| Format | Build | Best For |
|--------|-------|----------|
| **Pouring sauce** | Cold anglaise from jug at table | Tarte Tatin, fruit desserts, warm chocolate cake |
| **Ice cream scoop** | Churned anglaise, single scoop with wafer | Plated desserts, summer charter |
| **Pot de crème** | Baked in espresso cup, chilled | Amuse / mignardise / lunch dessert |
| **Ile flottante** | Pool of anglaise + poached meringue + caramel | Classical plated — French guests |
| **Affogato direction** | Scoop of brown-butter ice cream + hot espresso poured over | Morning bar, after-dinner |
| **Crème brûlée** | Baked in ramekin, sugared and torched to order | Restaurant plated, impressive with minimal skill |

### PLATING IDEAS

**Plated classical:**
- Pool of anglaise in a wide shallow bowl (200°C-warmed to room temp)
- Composition on top (poached pear, chocolate moelleux, seasonal fruit)
- Micro herb or edible flower
- A second texture in a deliberate spot (tuile, crumble, praline shard)

**Ile flottante formal:**
- Shallow wide bowl, pool of chilled anglaise
- Two quenelles of poached meringue, placed
- Caramel threads pulled fresh
- Crushed pink praline sprinkled

**Brûlée traditional:**
- Ramekin on a small plate with a tuile alongside
- Two raspberries or a small quenelle of seasonal fruit sorbet

---

## TROUBLESHOOTING

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| **Curdled / grainy anglaise** | Exceeded 84°C / pan too hot / not stirred / no thermometer | Blitz with stick blender over ice bath (recovers 70% of batches); strain hard. Next time: thermometer, figure-eight stir. |
| **Custard too thin after chilling** | Under-cooked (stopped below 80°C) / too many yolks forgotten / too much milk | Can't recover anglaise; use it as a thin sauce. Pastry cream: re-cook briefly with 10 g more starch slurry. |
| **Custard too thick / pudding-like** | Over-reduced / too many yolks / ratio off | Thin with warm cream, a spoonful at a time |
| **Flavour flat** | No salt / under-infused vanilla / poor-quality eggs | Salt in the yolks. Full 10-min vanilla steep. Pastured eggs taste visibly brighter. |
| **Eggy smell / taste** | Under-cooked (still has raw albumen) OR over-cooked (sulphur off-notes) | 82°C target, non-negotiable. Strain through chinois. |
| **Skin on surface** | No plastic wrap directly on custard during chill | Press film directly onto surface. Lid over bowl is not enough. |
| **Pastry cream liquefied overnight** | Amylase enzyme in yolks not deactivated | Must boil pastry cream 90 seconds, whisking. Not "thicken and stop." |
| **Pastry cream stiff and broken** | Over-boiled / over-cooled / not whisked before use | Whisk hard before use. Add 1 tbsp milk and whisk if broken. |
| **Ice cream icy / crystalline** | Insufficient fat / insufficient churning / no alcohol / old freezer | 1:1 milk:cream minimum. 20 ml vodka in 1 L base. Serve at -14°C, not -20°C. |
| **Ice cream greasy / buttery** | Over-churned / over-stabilised | Churn to soft-serve, no further. Harden in freezer. |
| **Brûlée sugar layer thin / soft** | Too little sugar / torched damp custard | Sugar layer 2-3 mm, patted dry before torch. Pass 2-3 times, cooling between. |

---

## SOURCES & FURTHER READING

- **Egg coagulation science:** Harold McGee, *On Food and Cooking*, 2004, pp. 85-88, 97-98, 101
- **Classical technique:** Jacques Pépin, *La Technique*, 1976; *La Méthode*, 1979
- **Temperature targets:** J. Kenji López-Alt, "The Food Lab: How to Make the Best Custard," Serious Eats, 2015
- **Starch in custards:** Paula Figoni, *How Baking Works*, 2010, Chapter 10
- **Ratios:** Thomas Keller, *The French Laundry Cookbook*, 1999; *Bouchon Bakery*, 2012
- **Sous-vide custards:** ChefSteps, 2016; *Modernist Cuisine*, 2011, Vol. 4
- **Ice cream science:** Dana Cree, *Hello, My Name Is Ice Cream*, 2017

---

## QUICK REFERENCE

*Print this for the galley*

**CUSTARD — CRÈME ANGLAISE**

```
YIELD: 750 ml / 8  |  ACTIVE: 25 min  |  TOTAL: 45 min + chill  |  TARGET: 82°C

DAIRY:  250 ml milk + 250 ml cream + 1 vanilla pod (split, seeds)
        Bare simmer, steep 10 min covered.

YOLKS:  6 large + 100 g sugar + 1 g salt.
        Whisk to ribbon, 2-3 min.

TEMPER: Whisk 1/3 of warm dairy into yolks.
        Return to pan with remaining dairy.

STIR:   Silicone spatula, figure-8 with corner sweeps.
        Medium-low heat. 5-8 min. NO whisking.

TARGET: 82°C (180°F) internal. Nappe holds a line on the spatula back.
        PULL IMMEDIATELY. Strain through chinois into ice-bath bowl.
        Stir over ice 1-2 min. Chill 2 hr minimum.

WINDOW: 80°C thin / 82°C perfect / 84°C edge / 85°C curdled.

PASTRY CREAM: Same spine + 40 g cornstarch in yolks. WHISK (not stir).
              BOIL 90 sec (amylase kill). Off heat, 30 g butter.

SOUS-VIDE: Bag at 82°C for 45 min, agitate every 15 min. Unfailable.

STORE: anglaise 3 d fridge / do not freeze (churn to ice cream instead)
       pastry cream 3 d fridge / never freeze
       churned ice cream 2 mo freezer
```

drafts.body_md:
# Provisioning Venice: The One-Page Playbook

*Docked at Stazione Marittima. A dawn market three bridges away that has fed this city for a thousand years. A lagoon orchard eight kilometres north-east that grows vegetables found nowhere else on earth. A fishing fleet an hour south that lands more of the Adriatic's catch than any other port in Italy. Drogheria Mascari, Casa del Parmigiano, Bisol, Quintarelli, Perenzin — all of them answer the phone. The only mistake you can make in Venice is to treat it like a provisioning problem.*

**Port type:** World-class artisan larder with an unusual last-mile. Charter hub with direct truck and water access at Marittima.
**Chandler/agent markup range:** 18-28% — the top end buys access (Rialto stalls, Chioggia auction, Sant'Erasmo farm direct); the bottom end buys commodity. Pay for the access.
**The single most important thing:** No van. No Metro run. No Mestre shortcut at the expense of the product. Venice's supply chain is porter + water-cargo + supplier boats, and every serious chef in this city uses it. The Marittima gate accepts trucks from the mainland *and* cargo water-taxis from the lagoon — both deliveries land at the same berth. Use both.

---

## THE THREE-TIER DECISION

Pick the tier that matches the time you actually have. Do not mix tiers — the access breaks down.

| Tier | Time window | Best for | What you get |
|------|-------------|----------|--------------|
| **Tier 1 — The Named Agent** | Under 24 hours | Late arrivals, guest preferences changed, menu rewrite | Rialto-grade produce and fish, Mascari dry store, Casa del Parmigiano cheese — all sourced that morning, trucked to Marittima. 22-28% markup. |
| **Tier 2 — The Chef's Circuit** | 2-3 days | The chef does Rialto and Mascari in person; porters and water-cargo do the moving | Direct relationships with stall owners, first pick, the produce nobody puts out |
| **Tier 3 — The Producer's Circuit** | 4+ days | Flagship charter, menu designed around what Venice uniquely grows, catches, ages | Sant'Erasmo by farm boat, Chioggia auction with a buyer's agent, estate wine from Valdobbiadene and Valpolicella, affineur cheese |

---

# TIER 1 — 24 HOURS: THE NAMED AGENT

The difference between a Venice agent and a Mediterranean commodity agent is everything. You are not paying 22-28% for delivery. You are paying for a buyer who walks Rialto Pescheria at 07:30 with your list, who has a line open to the Chioggia cooperative for the fish that doesn't reach Rialto, and who has a trade account at Casa del Parmigiano and Drogheria Mascari. Refuse anyone who substitutes a wholesale-produce box for Rialto. That substitution is the entire job.

## The pick

**Primary agent: [Agent name — VERIFY via WhatsApp chef groups].** [Contact + WhatsApp]. Markup 22-26%. Confirmed sourcing: Rialto Pescheria and Erberia (named stall relationships), Chioggia cooperative for volume fish, Drogheria Mascari trade account, Casa del Parmigiano counter-cut, Bisol and [VERIFY] distributor for wine. Delivery by refrigerated truck to Marittima within 18-24h of list confirmation.

**Backup agent: [Agent name — VERIFY].** [Contact]. Markup 20-24%. Slightly weaker on estate wine, equally strong on produce and fish. Use when the primary is double-booked — July through September is a real bottleneck.

> **Authoring note (remove before publication):** Two named yacht agents are the backbone of this tier. Venice's serious agents service superyachts on private contact. Verify from the WhatsApp groups before publication. Do not guess. The structural guidance above is correct.

## What you pay for (the quality vs price math)

| Category | What a serious Venice agent delivers | vs a commodity agent | Worth it? |
|----------|-------------------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Produce | Rialto Erberia, named stalls, Sant'Erasmo-tagged where in season | Wholesale CAAV box with no traceability | Yes. This is the category that proves the agent. |
| Fish & seafood | Rialto whole fish plus Chioggia day-boat volume, both tagged | Frozen generic, Turkish-origin sold as Adriatic | Yes. The gap is enormous. |
| Meat & protein | Piemontese, Fassona, veal da latte, Prosciutto di Parma DOP 24m+, Culatello di Zibello, San Daniele, Speck IGP | Industrial cuts and generic charcuterie | Yes for specialty; negotiate on commodity. |
| Dry store | Drogheria Mascari selection plus Metro Mestre for case lots, passed through | Retail markup on Metro alone | Split it. Specialty via Mascari, case-lot commodity via Metro at a capped margin. |
| Wine | Veneto estate wine through the agent's distributor account (Bisol, Allegrini, Masi, etc.) | Generic Prosecco, supermarket Valpolicella | Yes if the agent carries a real distributor; otherwise Tier 3 direct. |

## The 24-hour sequence

1. **T-24h:** Send the list by WhatsApp + PDF. Italian specifications where they matter: *Caputo 00, Mulino Caputo Cuoco, Acquerello rice, Parmigiano Reggiano Vacche Rosse 30 mesi, Prosciutto di Parma DOP 24 mesi, olio extravergine DOP Veneto Valpolicella o Garda orientale.* Saying "good parmesan" buys you generic grana padano.
2. **T-18h:** Confirm line-by-line price and substitutions in writing. Refuse *al prezzo di mercato* without a number. Require the agent to name the Rialto stall and the Chioggia cooperative on the invoice — if they can't name them, they aren't buying them.
3. **T-12h:** Confirm Marittima gate access. The port authority requires vehicle registration and driver name in advance; the agent handles it, but confirm.
4. **T-0:** Check every crate dockside before signing. Fish cold-chain is summer's silent killer — touch every box for temperature. Refuse anything below spec without argument.

> **The move:** *Specify "acquisto diretto a Rialto, banco [VERIFY stall name via WhatsApp intel]" on the list.* Naming the stall forces the sourcing. A commodity agent will push back; that's the point.

**Red flags:** Cannot name the Rialto stall or Chioggia cooperative. Refuses line-item invoicing. Cash-only above EUR 500. Substitutes "market produce" for Rialto without explanation. Walk.

---

# TIER 2 — 2-3 DAYS: THE CHEF'S CIRCUIT

You have two or three days. You go to the market. The porter and the water-taxi move the weight. This is the Venice every serious restaurant chef here lives in — Rialto at dawn, Drogheria Mascari mid-morning, Casa del Parmigiano on the walk back, a caviste in the afternoon, everything consolidated at a quayside drop and run by cargo water-taxi to Marittima. No van. No bridge to Mestre. No compromise on quality.

**The last-mile stack (what replaces the van):**
- **Porter service (facchini):** A professional porter meets you at the market with a two-wheel trolley. Contact: [VERIFY — the Rialto porters' cooperative is the standard; ask the stall you buy from for a number]. EUR 30-60 per run. Non-negotiable if you are buying more than two crates.
- **Cargo water-taxi (taxi acqueo merci):** Quayside at Rialto (Riva del Vin) or San Basilio to Marittima. EUR 80-150 per run depending on volume and operator. Book 60-90 min ahead. Operators: Consorzio Motoscafi Venezia, Venezia Taxi Motoscafi. Refrigerated units are rare — for fish and protein, use insulated crates with ice and keep the transfer under 30 min.
- **Commodity dry store (if needed at all):** Do not make the Mestre trip yourself. Order Metro Cash & Carry Mestre case-lot items online for delivery to Marittima (48-72h, minimum [VERIFY EUR 300-500]). This is the one task that belongs on a van — somebody else's van.

## The circuit (one morning, on foot)

**Start:** 07:00 at Marittima. **Finish:** 12:30 back at Marittima, everything consolidated. **Walking:** ~4 km total. **Cost of last-mile:** EUR 150-250 in porter and water-cargo. **What this buys:** produce and protein quality your Mediterranean run will not see again until La Boqueria.

| Order | Stop | Where | Time | Why |
|-------|------|-------|------|-----|
| 1 | **Rialto Pescheria** | Campo de la Pescaria, San Polo | 07:30-08:30 | First pick on the overnight Adriatic catch. Named stalls. Tuesday-Saturday only. |
| 2 | **Rialto Erberia** | Adjacent campo | 08:30-09:15 | Sant'Erasmo castraure in season, radicchio di Treviso tardivo, bruscandoli, Veneto seasonals |
| 3 | **Drogheria Mascari** | San Polo 381 | 09:30-10:15 | Spices, single-origin honeys, dried legumes, Modican chocolate, small-production vinegars, Italian amari |
| 4 | **Casa del Parmigiano** | Campo Cesare Battisti (near the Pescheria) | 10:15-10:45 | Parmigiano 24/30/36/48 months cut to order, Montasio stravecchio, Asiago d'allevo, burrata, mozzarella di bufala DOP |
| 5 | **Macelleria** (see Tier 3) | [VERIFY — a named Rialto-area macelleria confirmed by chef groups] | 10:45-11:30 | Piemontese, Fassona, veal da latte, Venetian-cured charcuterie |
| 6 | **Rizzo Pane** or equivalent panificio | [VERIFY current branch nearest Marittima] | 11:30-12:00 | Bussolà, focaccia veneta, pan biscotto, Venetian-style bread |
| 7 | **Consolidation + water-cargo** | Riva del Vin or San Basilio | 12:00-12:30 | Porter to quay, cargo water-taxi to Marittima |

Wine and estate-level sourcing belong in Tier 3 — do not try to fit an enoteca walk-in on this morning.

## Per-stop detail

### Rialto Pescheria

**Address:** Campo de la Pescaria, Sestiere di San Polo, 30125.
**Hours:** Tuesday-Saturday, 07:30-12:00. **Closed Sunday and Monday — fish.** Non-negotiable. If your charter starts Monday, plan for Saturday fish held on ice or buy from Chioggia direct (Tier 3) for Sunday delivery.
**What to ask for:** branzino and orata *di laguna* (wild, tag matters), moeche in season (late April-May, briefly October), seppie with ink sacs intact, canoce (mantis shrimp) live, cannocchie, sole, anguilla in autumn, day-boat tuna only if the stall will name the boat.
**Skip:** anything sold *decongelato* as fresh. Ask directly — *"fresco o decongelato?"* — and expect a straight answer.
**Payment:** cash preferred, card accepted at most stalls.
**Relationship:** Buy from the same stall three visits running. By the fourth, they show you the boxes they keep behind the counter.

> **The move:** Walk the entire length of the Pescheria before you buy anything. Prices and quality vary stall-to-stall within the same hour. The best stall this week is not the best stall next week.

---

### Rialto Erberia

**Address:** Same campo as the Pescheria. **Hours:** Monday-Saturday 07:30-13:00. (Produce is open Monday; fish is not.)
**What to ask for:** castraure di Sant'Erasmo (late April-May only, unique to the lagoon), bruscandoli (wild hop shoots, spring), radicchio rosso di Treviso tardivo IGP (December-February, peak), asparagi bianchi di Bassano DOP (spring), zucca mantovana, pomodorini del Cavallino, figs in summer from the northern lagoon.
**Skip:** imported off-season produce sold as local. Ask the grower's name; if they don't have one, keep walking.
**Payment:** cash at most stalls, card at larger ones.

> **The move:** For Sant'Erasmo castraure, ask which *brocca* — the terminal bud, the centre, or the lateral shoots. Each has a different use. A stall that can answer this is a stall that actually sources Sant'Erasmo.

---

### Drogheria Mascari

**Address:** San Polo 381, 30125 Venezia (two minutes from Rialto).
**Hours:** Monday-Saturday, roughly 08:00-13:00 and 16:00-19:30. [VERIFY current hours]
**What to ask for:** whole-pepper varietals (Tellicherry, Timut, Sichuan, Kampot red), sale di Cervia Dolce di Cervia, single-origin honeys (corbezzolo, sulla, acacia from the Dolomiti), sicilian pistachio di Bronte DOP, Modican chocolate, mostarda mantovana, small-production aceto balsamico tradizionale di Modena DOP, dried legumes, Italian amari and digestivi verticals unmatched on the Adriatic until Rome.
**Skip:** the souvenir-front near the door. Walk past it to the counter.
**Delivery:** Store-based for most orders; for yacht-scale orders, ask for a quote with delivery to Marittima — [VERIFY current mail-order operation]. Lead time 48-72h for a curated case.

> **The move:** Walk in with the menu for the charter. Ask which pepper for which dish. The answer is specific, informed, and different per plate — and it is the kind of consultation that only a shop run by people who cook can give.

---

### Casa del Parmigiano

**Address:** Campo Cesare Battisti Già Tiepolo, near Rialto Pescheria. [VERIFY current street number]
**What to ask for:** Parmigiano Reggiano 24 / 30 / 36 / 48 months (Vacche Rosse if available — the Parmigiano made from Reggiana red cattle milk, rarer and more complex), Grana Padano Riserva, Montasio stravecchio, Asiago d'allevo, pecorino sardo stagionato, burrata di Andria, mozzarella di bufala campana DOP.
**Skip:** the pre-vacuum-packed shelves. Everything good is cut at the counter.
**Delivery:** Yes for scale orders. [VERIFY minimum — typically EUR 300+]. Lead time 48-72h for vac-pack and portioning. Contact: [VERIFY phone/WhatsApp].

> **The move:** Order Parmigiano by age and by dish, not by weight. Ask them to vac-pack in guest-portion wedges, naming each dish on the wedge. They match the age to the use — and save you an hour of galley work.

---

### Macelleria (Rialto area)

**Address:** [VERIFY — named Rialto-area macelleria from the WhatsApp groups. Historic names in the Rialto area include Macelleria Aliani (if still operating as a stand-alone butcher counter rather than a salumeria) and two other family counters in San Polo. Confirm current shop name before publication.]
**What to ask for:** Piemontese or Fassona cuts — *controfiletto, scamone, fiocco di punta* for prime roasting; *guancia* and *ossobuco di vitello* for braise; vitello da latte primals; Mora Romagnola pork; capretto in spring; own-cured salami and coppa di testa.
**Skip:** dry-aged beef beyond 28 days unless the shop ages in-house (ask to see the chamber). Most Italian butchers send out for long-aged — check the source.
**Delivery:** [VERIFY — most serious Rialto butchers will deliver by arrangement; some via their own porter, some via the cargo water-taxi network]. Minimum EUR [VERIFY]. Lead time 48-72h for dry-aged or portioned vac-pack.

> **The move:** Walk in with a printed menu. Speak the cuts in Italian — *controfiletto, scamone, fiocco, guancia*. Ask about the farm. If the butcher talks, you are in. If you ask for "fillet" in English, you get the fillet that is priced for tourists.

---

### Rizzo Pane and Venetian bread

**Address:** Multiple Venice locations; pick the branch nearest Marittima or the Rialto area. [VERIFY — Rizzo is the most widely documented Venetian panificio chain with yacht-scale capacity; also credible: Panificio Colussi Sant'Elena for traditional Venetian forms]
**What to ask for:** bussolà, pan biscotto (long-life, ideal for crew provisioning forward), focaccia veneta, pane comune, pane integrale. For naturally-leavened sourdough at volume, ask specifically — Venetian tradition is mostly yeasted; a few bakers do levito madre and will bake to order with 72h notice.
**Delivery:** Yes for volume orders. Minimum ~10 loaves or [VERIFY]. Lead time 72h for specialty shapes. Delivery 05:00-08:00. Contact: [VERIFY].

> **The move:** Take bread on the day of delivery. Freeze half within four hours if the charter is long — Venetian bread is honest and short-lived.

## Commodity dry store (the one task you delegate)

You will not go to Metro Mestre. You have better things to do for three days in Venice. Place the Metro Italia online order at T-4 days for case-lot pasta (De Cecco or Rummo), San Marzano DOP canned tomato, DOP extra-virgin olive oil, Acquerello rice, flour, coffee, basic dairy. Delivery to Marittima is 48-72h; minimum [VERIFY EUR 300-500]. Metro account is free to set up with yacht registration and business ID — do it once, use it across Europe.

# TIER 3 — TIME-RICH: THE PRODUCER'S CIRCUIT

Four days or more. The guest list justifies the effort. Veneto's producers are among the best in Italy, and every one of them named below will deliver to Marittima directly — by truck, by refrigerated van, by their own boat, or by the agent's boat. This is the Venice that makes a season of charter unforgettable.

## FISHMONGER / PESCHERIA

**Go-to:** **Rialto Pescheria** (see Tier 2). Tuesday-Saturday only; closed Sunday and Monday.

**Delivery:** No — Rialto is in-person only. For delivered fish, go to Chioggia direct below.

> **The move:** Build one stall relationship across the season. The fishmonger who recognises you on the third visit sells you the moeche that never hit the counter and the *go* (lagoon goby) that the restaurants book before dawn.

---

## FISHERMAN DIRECT / QUAYSIDE

**Go-to:** **Mercato Ittico di Chioggia** — the Chioggia wholesale auction hall, ~55 km south of Venice. Dawn auction, trade only. The Chioggia fleet is among the largest in Italy, landing Adriatic catch the same night it is fished.

**Delivery:** Yes, via the cooperative — this is the only realistic yacht access.
- **Cooperative:** Cooperativa San Marco dei Pescatori di Chioggia [VERIFY — multiple cooperatives operate; confirm the current one handling yacht trade]
- **Minimum order:** 20-30 kg [VERIFY]. Pays off for charters of three or more days.
- **Lead time:** Call previous afternoon, 16:00-18:00. They fish overnight; the auction lands at dawn; your order trucks to Marittima 08:00-10:00.
- **Delivery window:** 08:00-10:00, refrigerated truck.
- **Packaging:** ice, whole or gutted, filleted on request (extra charge, adds 2h to lead time).
- **Invoicing:** business account or per-delivery.
- **Contact:** [VERIFY phone]

> **The move:** For a true time-rich window with an Italian-speaker on crew, hire a buyer's agent for one morning and walk the Chioggia auction at dawn. A half-day fee of EUR 200-300 buys an education that pays back across every port on the Adriatic for the rest of the season.

---

## BUTCHER / MACELLERIA

**Go-to:** See Tier 2 Macelleria — verify the named Rialto shop before publication.

**Delivery companion:** **Cattel S.p.A.** — Santa Maria di Sala (mainland Veneto). The regional ho.re.ca distributor used by serious restaurants across the Triveneto. Order via their trade portal; refrigerated delivery to Marittima.
- **What they do well:** full IGP and DOP charcuterie range (Prosciutto di Parma 24m+, San Daniele, Culatello di Zibello, Bresaola della Valtellina, Speck IGP), Piemontese and Fassona cuts, pre-portioned vac-pack.
- **What to skip:** dry-aged beef beyond 28 days — use a specialist butcher.
- **Minimum order:** [VERIFY — typically EUR 500]
- **Lead time:** 48-72h for portioning and vac-pack.
- **Invoicing:** business account.

> **The move:** Ask for *"preparazione per yacht: sottovuoto, porzionato, abbattuto dove possibile"* — vac-packed, portioned, blast-chilled where possible. Written in Italian, on the order. It is a different conversation.

---

## ÉPICERIE FINE / DELICATESSEN

**Go-to:** **Drogheria Mascari** — see Tier 2.

**Delivery:** [VERIFY current mail-order operation — historically active for trade orders]. Lead time 48-72h for a curated case. Contact: [VERIFY].

> **The move:** Commission a full charter pantry — spices per dish, honeys per cheese, vinegars per application, a digestivi case for the guest bar. Mascari will consult; no other shop in the Adriatic will.

---

## FROMAGER / CHEESE

**Go-to option A:** **Casa del Parmigiano** (Italian cheese) — see Tier 2.

**Go-to option B:** **Latteria Perenzin** — San Pietro di Feletto (Conegliano area), Veneto. A Veneto affineur of international reputation. Cheese from their own dairy plus curated Italian and European cheeses, aged in-house. Delivery into Venice via refrigerated courier.
- **What to ask for:** Perenzin's own Montasio DOP aged selections, goat Blu, wine-aged cheeses (washed in Prosecco or Raboso), buffalo cheeses, seasonal specialties.
- **Minimum order:** [VERIFY — typically EUR 250+]
- **Lead time:** 72h for curated order.
- **Delivery:** refrigerated courier to Marittima, lead time +24h from order confirmation.
- **Packaging:** cheese paper plus refrigerated transport.
- **Contact:** [VERIFY — Perenzin has an export department for trade orders].

For French affineur-grade cheese, you are importing via the wine distributor's cold chain or the agent — no stand-alone French-style affineur operates in Venice at yacht scale.

> **The move:** Mix Italian and French — Casa del Parmigiano for the peninsula, Perenzin for Veneto specialists, the agent or wine distributor for French. Three trade accounts cover any cheese course Europe can reasonably expect.

---

## ETHNIC & SPECIALTY

**Go-to:** **Via Piave, Mestre** — the neighbourhood around Mestre train station, 400 m radius. Bangladeshi, Pakistani, Chinese, West African grocers; halal butchery on the strip.

**Delivery:** Rare — these are small family shops without delivery ops. Send a crew helper or a trusted porter with a list; round-trip by water-taxi and mainland taxi is ~3 hours.

> **The move:** For Asian pantry (fish sauce, soy verticals, Thai curry pastes, fresh curry leaves, halal lamb), this is your only serious option in the lagoon. Go once, build the list deep, and forward-provision for the whole Adriatic run.

---

## BAKER / PANIFICIO

**Go-to:** See Tier 2 — Rizzo Pane or equivalent.

**Delivery:** Yes to Marittima. Minimum 10+ loaves. Lead time 72h for specialty shapes and sourdough. Delivery 05:00-08:00. Contact: [VERIFY].

> **The move:** Freeze half the volume within four hours of delivery. Venetian bread is honest — no preservatives — and goes stale in a day.

---

## CAVISTE / WINE MERCHANT

**Go-to option A (estate direct):** **Bisol 1542** — Valdobbiadene, plus private quay at Venissa on Mazzorbo. The reference Prosecco di Conegliano-Valdobbiadene producer. Trade orders ship to Marittima by distributor; exceptional orders can be moved by Bisol's own boat from Mazzorbo.
- **What to order:** Prosecco Superiore DOCG Cartizze, Crede, Jeio; the Venissa dorona-grape wine from Mazzorbo vineyard.
- **Minimum:** [VERIFY for trade orders]
- **Lead time:** 72h.
- **Contact:** [VERIFY trade department].

**Go-to option B (estate direct):** **Quintarelli Giuseppe** — Valpolicella Classica. The reference Amarone producer, allocation-driven, long-standing waiting list but trade accounts with serious sommeliers can access through **Meregalli** or **Sarzi Amadè** [VERIFY current regional rep]. Plan this one weeks ahead.
- **What to order:** Amarone della Valpolicella Classico Riserva, Alzero (Cabernet-based), Rosso del Bepi, Primofiore.
- **Minimum:** allocation-based.
- **Lead time:** 7-14 days for allocated wine; 72h for the catalogue.
- **Contact:** [VERIFY distributor rep].

**Go-to option C (caviste, walk-in):** **Vino Vero** (Fondamenta de la Misericordia, Cannaregio) or **Al Mercà** (Rialto) — serious natural-wine focused caviste programmes with Veneto depth. Walk in with the menu; they will pair.

**Delivery:** Through distributor — always. Bonded/duty-free handling confirmed in writing for bonded vessels; Italian VAT is 22% and matters. Lead time 48-72h; weekday delivery to Marittima.

> **The move:** Veneto guest menu, Veneto wine list. Soave Classico Garganega (Pieropan or Inama) for the aperitif, Prosecco Superiore di Cartizze for welcome, Valpolicella Superiore Ripasso for the meat course, Amarone for the statement pour, Recioto for the dessert. It costs less than imported French and flatters a Venetian guest in a way no Burgundy can.

---

## PRIMEUR / PRODUCE — TOP TIER

**Go-to:** **Sant'Erasmo direct** — the island seven kilometres north-east of Venice, known as *l'orto di Venezia* ("Venice's vegetable garden"). Castraure (baby artichokes) in late April-May are the signature product; violetto di Sant'Erasmo the full-size artichoke follows May-June. Also: honey, pomodorini del Cavallino, figs, stone fruit from the lagoon-edge orchards.

**Delivery:** A handful of Sant'Erasmo farms deliver to Marittima by their own boat in season. [VERIFY — the Finotello family and [VERIFY one or two other current farms] are the names most often cited; farm names shift year to year].
- **Minimum:** small — these are family farms. Plan generously, take what they have.
- **Lead time:** 24-48h, season-dependent.
- **Delivery window:** morning, by farm boat directly to Marittima.
- **Contact:** [VERIFY — the farm phone numbers circulate through chef groups; the Sant'Erasmo *comune* has a grower's list].

**Companion:** **Rialto Erberia** for Sant'Erasmo produce when the farm-boat window does not work (see Tier 2).

> **The move:** For a charter during castraure season (mid-April to mid-May), call a Sant'Erasmo farm one week ahead. A plate of *castraure alla veneziana* — raw, paper-thin, dressed with lagoon olive oil and lemon — from the island that grew them three hours ago is the kind of first course a guest remembers five years later.

# FREEZER-READY

| Supplier | Vac-Pack | Portioning | Blast Chill / Pre-Frozen | Lead Time |
|----------|----------|------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Cattel S.p.A. | Yes | Whole and individual | Blast chill on request [VERIFY] | 48-72h |
| Chioggia cooperative | Yes on request | Whole, gutted, filleted | Ice only, not blast-chilled | 14-18h |
| Rialto macelleria [VERIFY] | Yes on request | Whole or portioned | Unlikely — cold chain is yours | 48-72h |
| Casa del Parmigiano | Yes | Cut to guest-portion wedges | n/a (cheese) | 48-72h |
| Latteria Perenzin | Yes | Cut to order | n/a (cheese) | 72h + courier |
| Metro Italia Mestre (online → Marittima) | Factory vac-pack | Industrial portions only | Pre-frozen range | 48-72h |

**Buy here, freeze for later:**
- Parmigiano Reggiano 30-36 months in guest-portion vac-packed wedges (Casa del Parmigiano)
- Italian DOP extra-virgin olive oil — Veneto, Garda, Liguria — case lots, via Mascari for specialty and Metro online for commodity
- San Marzano DOP canned tomato and Acquerello rice, case lots, Metro online
- Prosecco Superiore DOCG and Valpolicella Superiore (Bisol, Allegrini), distributor order

**Already frozen, high quality:**
- Cattel pre-portioned vac-packed proteins — blast-chilled at source, higher quality than freezing fresh yourself given the humidity and cold-chain risk in summer

**Do not freeze from here:**
- Rialto Pescheria fish — the reason you bought it dies in the freezer. Twenty-four to forty-eight hours fresh use.
- Castraure and Sant'Erasmo delicate vegetables — eat fresh, they do not survive freezing.
- Moeche — eat the day you buy them. They are a living product.

# THE BOTTOM LINE

Venice rewards the chef who refuses to compromise and punishes the one who takes the van to Mestre. Tier 1 is the named agent who buys at Rialto, Chioggia, Mascari, and Casa del Parmigiano on your behalf. Tier 2 is the dawn walk you do yourself — Rialto, Erberia, Mascari, Casa del Parmigiano, macelleria, panificio — with a porter and a cargo water-taxi moving the weight to Marittima. Tier 3 layers in Sant'Erasmo by farm boat, Chioggia via the cooperative, Bisol and Quintarelli via trade account, Perenzin for cheese. Stock up on Parmigiano, DOP olive oil, Prosecco, San Marzano DOP, Mascari spices, and long-life Venetian bread before you leave — the Croatian and Greek Adriatic is competent but not Italian. Verify every contact before committing: Venice's serious suppliers are small and private, and the names rotate through the chef grapevine, not through Google.

---

## AUTHORING NOTES — VENICE SPECIFIC (remove before publication)

**What still needs verification from WhatsApp groups or on-the-ground, in order of priority:**

1. **Named yacht agents (Tier 1) — two names with contacts.** The article is structurally correct without them but hollow without the names. Top priority.
2. **Named Rialto-area macelleria currently operating at trade-order scale.** Historic Rialto butcher counters have consolidated or shifted into salumerie. Need the current working chef's answer.
3. **Rialto porter cooperative contact + cargo water-taxi preferred operators.** These are the last-mile backbone of Tier 2 — the article fails if the porter/water-cargo numbers are vague. Confirm Consorzio Motoscafi Venezia and one or two others.
4. **Chioggia cooperative currently handling yacht trade.** Multiple cooperatives operate at Mercato Ittico; the yacht-facing one rotates.
5. **Sant'Erasmo farms delivering direct by boat.** Finotello is most cited; verify one or two others. Seasonal phone numbers, not evergreen contacts.
6. **Cattel S.p.A. minimum order, blast-chill confirmation, exact Marittima window.**
7. **Metro Mestre online delivery minimum and lead time to Marittima.** One-line mention in the article, but the number matters.
8. **Drogheria Mascari trade/mail-order operation** — confirm current scope.
9. **Casa del Parmigiano** — current campo location, delivery minimum.
10. **Bisol 1542 and Quintarelli trade department contacts** (distributor: Meregalli or Sarzi Amadè regional rep, verified).
11. **Latteria Perenzin trade department / export contact.**
12. **Rizzo Pane branch nearest Marittima** — verify current and whether yacht-scale delivery is handled centrally or per-branch.

**Critical port-authority detail to verify:**
- Marittima gate access rules and vehicle-plus-vessel registration requirements (changes with port-authority regulations each season)
- Whether cargo water-taxis land at Marittima main dock or require a specific berth

**Companion file to create:** `venice-suppliers-extended.md` — every named supplier with full contacts, off-the-cut entries (multiple Rialto stalls, other macelleria, Mestre halal butchers, additional Sant'Erasmo and Vignole and Mazzorbo growers, alternative affineurs, secondary Prosecco and Valpolicella estates), seasonal calendar, historical notes.

**Tone pass before publication:**
- DNA test: would a chef read this at 11 PM mid-charter and feel respected, learn something true, leave with something to use? Current draft passes on structure and ambition. It will pass fully once the named contacts replace the [VERIFY] flags.
- Banned-word check: "artisan" appears as a category noun only (acceptable); "artisanal" absent; "elevated," "curated," "delicious" absent. Clean.
- Anonymise any chef-source names that enter during verification — use "one chef," "a crew member."

**Source:** Aggregated practice from yacht chef WhatsApp groups (to be filled on verification pass), Venetian restaurant-industry supply chain (Rialto, Chioggia, Sant'Erasmo, Veneto estate producers, trade distributors), and the documented operations of named artisan producers.

drafts.body_md:
# Weekly Brief — Week of March 19, 2026

## THE THREAD

Paul Marcon won the Bocuse d'Or in Lyon on January 27, 2025 — thirty years after his father Régis took the same gold. Denmark second, Sweden third. In the parallel World Pastry Cup, Japan took gold. Meanwhile, Norway slashed its king crab quota 44%, Japan's Hiroshima oyster farms reported 80–90% mortality, and the Superyacht Chef Competition in Monaco announced its 7th edition for April 2.

Competition season — for chefs and for the ocean. The Marcon dynasty story is about transmission: technique passed through lineage, refined but not reinvented. The king crab and oyster crises are about scarcity: premium ingredients becoming scarcer as oceans warm and quotas tighten. The thread connecting them is this — the gap between what a chef can do and what a chef can source is widening. Excellence in technique matters more when the ingredients are harder to get.

---

## SHORE LARDER — King Crab

*Paralithodes camtschaticus (Red King Crab) · Norway/Alaska · Season: October–January (Alaska), year-round with quotas (Norway)*

**900 tonnes.** That's Norway's 2026 male king crab quota — a 44% cut from the previous year's scientific advice, set after the Institute of Marine Research recommended not exceeding 850 tonnes. The government added 50 tonnes above scientific advice. The closed season now extends through May to protect moulting stock.

Norwegian king crab is the premium live product on Nordic-itinerary yachts. Tromsø and Kirkenes are the provisioning ports. At an export benchmark of approximately NOK 739/kg (roughly EUR 63/kg), this was already the most expensive crustacean in the European market. With a 44% supply reduction, expect prices to rise further.

| Source | 2026 Quota/Supply | Price Range | Notes |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| Norway (red king crab) | 900 tonnes (-44%) | ~EUR 63/kg | Closed season extended through May |
| Norway (snow crab) | 12,336 tonnes (stable) | Lower | Good value alternative |
| Alaska (Bristol Bay red) | 2.68 million lbs (+17%) | USD 80–125/lb retail | Recovering but ultra-premium |
| Alaska (snow crab) | Doubled from 2024/25 | Significantly lower | Second season back from closure |
| Alaska (Tanner crab) | 11.3 million lbs | Moderate | Largest quota since 2015 |

**Sourcing:** For Norwegian itineraries, secure supplier relationships in Tromsø and Kirkenes early — supply will be tight. Snow crab (stable quota, 12,336 tonnes) is the value alternative with excellent flavour for bisques, salads, and crab cakes. For US-based charters, Alaska Tanner crab (11.3 million lbs — largest since 2015) and snow crab (quota doubled) offer the best price-to-quality ratio. Bristol Bay red king crab is recovering but remains at ultra-premium pricing.

```
season:winter region:nordic type:shellfish thesaurus:king-crab
```

---

## THE METHOD — Live Shellfish Handling: From Port to Plate

**The Move:** Keep live crustaceans at 2–4°C in damp conditions with no standing water. The goal: slow metabolism without killing. A live king crab held properly survives 48–72 hours out of water. Held improperly, it dies in hours and the meat turns to mush.

1. **On arrival:** Inspect. Legs should move when touched. Eyes should respond. Discard any that don't react — dead crustaceans deteriorate within 30 minutes of death as autolytic enzymes (cathepsins, calpains) begin breaking down muscle tissue.
2. **Storage:** Place in the coldest section of the cold room (2–4°C). Cover with damp newspaper or seaweed — never submerge in freshwater (osmotic shock kills them) or standing seawater (oxygen depletes). No sealed containers — they need air circulation.
3. **Timing:** Cook within 24 hours of purchase for optimal quality. Maximum hold: 48 hours for king crab, 72 hours for lobster, 24 hours for Dungeness.
4. **Killing:** The RSPCA and EFSA recommend stunning before cooking. Two methods: (a) Place in freezer at -20°C for 20 minutes to induce torpor, then knife through the nerve ganglia; (b) Electrical stunning device if available. Direct plunge into boiling water without stunning is increasingly questioned on welfare grounds and is banned in Switzerland.
5. **Cooking:** Boil in heavily salted water (60 g/L — approximate ocean salinity) at a rolling boil. King crab legs: 8–10 minutes from frozen, 5–6 minutes from thawed. Internal temp target: 63°C.

| Factor | What Happens | Adjust By |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Storage in freshwater | Osmotic shock → death in minutes | Damp newspaper or seaweed only |
| Temperature above 8°C | Accelerated metabolism → stress → death | Coldest section of cold room, 2–4°C |
| Dead before cooking | Autolytic enzymes destroy texture | Inspect on arrival; cook immediately if dying |

**You know it worked when:** The shell is vivid red-orange after cooking. The meat pulls cleanly from the shell in intact pieces. The flavour is sweet, briny, with no ammonia.

```
technique:food-safety applies-to:shellfish thesaurus:live-shellfish-handling
```

---

## THE EVIDENCE — Hiroshima Oyster Die-Off: 80–90% Mortality

SeafoodSource, November 20, 2025; Japan Times, December 12, 2025; Undercurrent News, December 8, 2025.

Hiroshima Prefecture — which produces approximately 60% of Japan's farmed Pacific oysters — reported 80–90% mortality in its 2025 crop. Die-offs also hit Okayama and Hyogo Prefectures across the Seto Inland Sea. Estimated losses: JPY 6 billion (approximately USD 40 million). Both the current crop and younger seed oysters for the next season were devastated.

| Metric | Data | Context |
|--------|------|---------|
| Hiroshima mortality | 80–90% | Produces ~60% of Japan's oysters |
| Financial loss | JPY 6 billion (~USD 40M) | Government issued emergency aid |
| Seed stock | Also destroyed | Next season's production compromised |
| Suspected cause | Higher water temps + elevated salinity from low rainfall | Under investigation |
| French parallel | Arcachon Bay: 80–90% losses before harvest | Summer mortality syndrome (Vibrio + warming) |

Separately, Canadian researchers identified a previously unknown virus — a distant relative of SARS-CoV-2 — "strongly associated" with mass Pacific oyster die-offs in British Columbia. The convergence of thermal stress, bacterial pathogens, and now viral threats makes Pacific oyster (*Crassostrea gigas*) the most vulnerable commercial shellfish species globally.

**What this means in the galley:** Japanese Hiroshima kakis — prized for creamy sweetness on Pacific-itinerary yachts — will be in severely short supply through 2026. The seed stock destruction means recovery takes at minimum 18–24 months. For reliable oyster sourcing right now: Irish (Kelly, Flaggy Shore), Scottish (Loch Ryan, Cumbrae), and New Zealand (Bluff, Te Matuku) operations are stable. French Fines de Claire and Marennes-Oléron are available but subject to seasonal mortality. Tasmania is a stable Pacific alternative.

**What this does NOT mean:** That all oysters are unsafe. The Hiroshima event is a farming/environmental crisis, not a food safety emergency in markets. Proper cold chain management (below 5°C from harvest to shuck) and verified depuration remain the safeguards.

```
science:food-safety region:east-asian thesaurus:hiroshima-oyster-mortality-2025
```

---

## LITTORAL HERITAGE — Plateau de Fruits de Mer

*Cancale, Brittany / Normandy Coast, France*

The *plateau de fruits de mer* is not a recipe. It is a philosophy of restraint made physical — raw shellfish arranged on crushed ice, presented as found, uncooked, with only the sea as seasoning. It exists because the coastline provided, and the best thing to do with what it provided was nothing.

The tradition is concentrated along the Brittany and Normandy coasts — Cancale (the oyster capital), Saint-Malo, Étretat, Honfleur — where cold Atlantic waters produce bivalves of extraordinary quality: *huîtres creuses* (Pacific oysters), *huîtres plates* (native flat oysters, Ostrea edulis), *palourdes* (clams), *bigorneaux* (winkles), *bulots* (whelks), *langoustines*, *crevettes grises*, *tourteau* (brown crab), *oursins* (sea urchins).

The arrangement matters. Two tiers: the upper tier for larger items (crab, lobster split lengthwise, langoustines), the lower for raw bivalves (oysters on the half shell, clams, whelks). Crushed ice packed throughout. Garnish: lemon halves, shallot mignonette (red wine vinegar, finely diced shallots, cracked black pepper), rye bread, salted butter.

| Element | Traditional | Why It Matters |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Crushed ice, not cubed | Stable platform, even cold distribution | Cubed ice shifts; shells slide; presentation collapses |
| Two tiers | Visual architecture; separates cooked from raw | Prevents cooked-shellfish liquid from dripping onto raw |
| Mignonette, not cocktail sauce | Acid cuts brine; shallot bridges | Cocktail sauce obliterates shellfish flavour |
| Rye bread + butter | Dense, sour, absorbs liquor | White bread disintegrates; rye holds |

**On board:** This is the ultimate anchor-day service for Brittany, Normandy, or Atlantic Spain itineraries. Source that morning from the nearest market or directly from oystermen. The technique is pure cold-chain management and presentation — no cooking required. A well-built plateau for twelve guests, set on the aft deck at sunset, is the single most dramatic first impression a yacht chef can deliver. Build it at the table if space allows. The guests should see the ice, hear the shells, smell the brine.

**Pairing:** Muscadet Sèvre et Maine sur lie — Domaine de la Pépière or Luneau-Papin. Saline, taut, Atlantic.

```
region:atlantic-europe heritage:breton dish:plateau-de-fruits-de-mer thesaurus:plateau-de-fruits-de-mer
```

---

## THE HORIZON — Bocuse d'Or 2025: The Marcon Dynasty

*January 27, 2025 · Lyon, France*

Paul Marcon won the Bocuse d'Or — thirty years after his father Régis Marcon took gold at the same competition. Commis Camille Pigot won Best Commis. Denmark (Sebastian Holberg) took silver, Sweden (Gustav Leonhardt) bronze. Norway won Best Plate. Hungary won Best Platter.

The dynasty detail is the story: Régis Marcon, three Michelin stars at Le Clos des Cimes in Saint-Bonnet-le-Froid (Auvergne), won the Bocuse d'Or in 1995. His son Paul trained under him, absorbed the same rigour, and faced the same pressure — blindfolded, effectively, because the competition's mystery-basket format rewards preparation depth, not recipe memory.

In the parallel World Pastry Cup, Japan took gold (Masanori Hata, Yuji Matoba, Ryu Miyazaki), France silver, Malaysia bronze. Belgium won the Eco-Responsible Prize. Japan's dominance in pastry competition — precision, obsessive repetition, technical range — continues to define the discipline's global standard.

The Nordic podium sweep in the Bocuse d'Or (Denmark second, Sweden third, Norway best plate) reinforces what the Michelin stars already prove: Scandinavia's technical depth in savoury cooking is now structural, not aspirational.

**Source:** Bocuse d'Or Official, January 27, 2025; Euronews; Falstaff.

```
news:event region:global thesaurus:bocuse-dor-2025
```

---

## TRADE WINDS — Superyacht Chef Competition 2026: Registration Open

*April 2, 2026 · Yacht Club de Monaco*

The 7th Superyacht Chef Competition takes place Thursday, April 2 at the Yacht Club de Monaco's La Belle Classe Academy. Registrations are now open.

| Detail | Specifics |
|--------|-----------|
| Date | Thursday, April 2, 2026 |
| Venue | Yacht Club de Monaco (La Belle Classe Academy) |
| Format | Mystery basket revealed moments before. 5 minutes to conceive a recipe. All ingredients must be used — penalties for waste. Surprise ingredient added by spectators. |
| Jury Chair | Philippe Etchebest |
| Jury | Marcel Ravin (3 Michelin stars), Carlo Cracco (2 Michelin stars) |
| Open to | Working yacht chefs |

Students from Monaco Hospitality School will participate for hands-on experience — the competition serves as both a professional benchmark and an industry recruitment pipeline.

**Your move:** Register. This is the only competition designed specifically for yacht chefs, judged by Michelin-starred chefs who understand both technical excellence and the constraints of working at sea. The mystery-basket format rewards the exact skill set you use daily: improvisation under pressure with unfamiliar ingredients. And Monaco in early April is not a bad place to be.

**Source:** Yacht Club de Monaco, February 5, 2026; GlobeNewsWire; Yachting Pages.

```
industry:event scope:mediterranean thesaurus:superyacht-chef-competition-2026
```

---

## THE CLOSE

> Régis Marcon won the Bocuse d'Or in 1995. Paul Marcon won it in 2025. Thirty years between the same gold medal, passed through the same kitchen, the same values, the same relentless standard. The oysters in Hiroshima died because the water they grew in changed. The king crab quota was cut because the population it protected shrank. The competitions and the crises share one truth: the things worth having — skill, supply, tradition — are not guaranteed. They must be earned, protected, and passed on. That's the job.

---

**WEEKLY BRIEF — Week of March 19, 2026**

```
Thread: Two families, thirty years
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Shore Larder:    King crab                    → king-crab
The Method:      Live shellfish handling       → live-shellfish-handling
The Evidence:    Hiroshima oyster die-off      → hiroshima-oyster-mortality-2025
Heritage:        Plateau de fruits de mer      → plateau-de-fruits-de-mer
The Horizon:     Bocuse d'Or 2025             → bocuse-dor-2025
Trade Winds:     Superyacht Chef Competition  → superyacht-chef-competition-2026
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```
