drafts.body_md:
# Provisioning San Juan: The One-Page Playbook

*US wholesale pricing, no import duty, and the last full-infrastructure port before the eastern Caribbean goes thin.*

San Juan is the provisioning port that most Caribbean-bound chefs overlook. They fly into Miami, load the boat in Fort Lauderdale, and head south. But if your route touches Puerto Rico — and many transatlantic arrivals, USVI-bound charters, and eastern Caribbean itineraries do — you are standing in a US territory with Costco, Sam's Club, Restaurant Depot, Amazon Prime delivery, and zero import duty. Everything your boat needs, at mainland prices, without crossing to Florida.

The chandler markup here is largely irrelevant because there are few dedicated yacht provisioning agents in San Juan. That is not a weakness — it is the point. The island is small enough, the wholesale infrastructure dense enough, and the roads short enough that self-sourcing a full provision takes one focused day. Two days if you want to be thorough. No agent required.

The single most important thing to know: Puerto Rico is the last place heading east where you have full US retail infrastructure with no import tax. Once you leave for the USVI, BVI, or eastern Caribbean, prices on imported goods rise 30-60% and selection drops off a cliff. Whatever your storage can hold, fill it here.

---

## The Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General / One-Stop** | **Amazon Prime** (standard US shipping) | **Costco** (Carolina or Caguas) | Same membership as mainland. Same prices. Same product. |
| **Fish & Seafood** | — | **La Placita de Santurce** / **Pescadería del Atlántico** | Arrive before 08:00. Ask for "chillo entero" — whole snapper, cheaper per kg. |
| **Meat & Protein** | — | **Restaurant Depot** (Carolina) / **Costco** | Restaurant Depot: same free membership as mainland US. Professional cuts. |
| **Bread & Bakery** | Panaderías deliver — call ahead | **Panadería España** (Hato Rey) | Pan sobao and pan de agua. Excellent crew bread. Order 24h ahead for volume. |
| **Produce & Fruit** | — | **Plaza del Mercado de Río Piedras** | Recao, ají dulce, plantains, avocados, tropical fruit — local, cheap, excellent. |
| **Dry Store & Pantry** | **Amazon Prime** (no duty, standard delivery) | **Costco** / **Sam's Club** | Order D-5. Bulk dry goods at mainland wholesale. Fill every locker. |
| **Frozen** | — | **Costco** / **Sam's Club** | Load the freezer. Frozen goods cost 30-50% more at the next port. |
| **Ethnic & Specialty** | — | **Pitusa** (multiple locations) / Santurce Asian markets | Pitusa for Dominican/Caribbean staples. Asian selection limited vs Miami. |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Costco** (spirits) / **Bodegas Compostela** (wine) | PR has lower spirits tax than mainland US. Stock the bar here. |
| **Agent (no time)** | — | No dedicated yacht agent. Self-source. | Island is small. Costco + Restaurant Depot + market = one day. |

---

## Category Detail

### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **La Placita de Santurce** — The market district in San Juan's nightlife neighbourhood. By day, fish vendors sell local catch from the surrounding waters: red snapper (chillo), mahi-mahi (dorado), Caribbean spiny lobster, yellowtail. Quality is good when the product is fresh — and here, fresh means it was swimming yesterday. Cold chain discipline varies by vendor, so inspect. Touch, smell, check the eyes. You know the drill.
- **Pescadería del Atlántico** — More consistent than the market vendors. Carries both local and imported seafood. Better for specific orders or when you need a guaranteed quantity rather than whatever came in.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rican waters produce excellent local fish that is genuinely local — not frozen-and-thawed imports relabelled as "fresh." Ask for "chillo entero" (whole red snapper). Whole fish is cheaper per kg than fillets, and you control the portioning and butchery. A 2kg whole chillo at La Placita costs roughly half per kg what you would pay for pre-cut fillets at the supermarket — and the quality is incomparable.

**Buy here:** Whole local snapper, mahi-mahi, Caribbean lobster when in season
**Skip:** Imported salmon and tuna at the market — buy those at Costco where the cold chain is guaranteed

---

### Meat & Protein

**Go in person:**
- **Restaurant Depot** (Carolina, 20 minutes from Old San Juan) — Professional cuts, bulk pricing, USDA Prime and Choice. Same free membership as every Restaurant Depot in the US — bring boat registration and a business card on your first visit. The meat section here is properly stocked: whole tenderloins, bone-in ribeyes, rack of lamb, pork belly, duck breast. Case lots of chicken thighs and ground beef for crew.
- **Costco** (Carolina or Caguas) — USDA Choice and Prime at Costco pricing. Vacuum-packed, consistent quality. The default for loading the freezer in bulk.

> **Smart tip:** Buy 2-3 weeks of frozen proteins here. Vacuum-seal everything, blast-freeze if your boat has the capacity, or pack tight in the freezer with cardboard dividers between categories. Once you leave Puerto Rico heading east, meat quality drops, availability becomes unreliable, and prices rise 30-50%. The proteins you load here are the proteins you will serve in Antigua, St Barths, and the BVI — where the same cuts either do not exist or cost double.

**Buy here:** Full freezer load — tenderloins, ribeyes, lamb racks, duck, chicken in bulk, crew proteins in case lots
**Skip:** Pre-marinated or pre-seasoned proteins. You are a chef. Buy clean protein and do the work yourself.

---

### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Amazon Prime** — Delivers to Puerto Rico at standard US rates with no import duty. This is not obvious to everyone, and it changes the maths entirely. Order specialty dry goods, pantry items, equipment, vacuum bags, cling film — anything non-perishable that you would normally pick up at a specialty shop. Place orders D-5 to D-4. Standard Prime shipping (2-3 days to PR addresses) means your boxes are waiting when you start the in-person runs.

**Go in person:**
- **Costco** (Carolina or Caguas) — Same Costco card you use on the mainland. Same product. Same prices. The Carolina location is closer to the marinas; Caguas is larger. Either works. This is your bulk general grocery run: dairy, eggs, beverages, snacks, cleaning supplies, paper goods, canned goods, condiments, and the overflow from every other category.

> **Smart tip:** Do not underestimate how much you can accomplish in a single Costco run here. Dairy, bulk proteins, dry goods, produce basics, beverages, cleaning, crew snacks — one trolley, one trip, 60% of your general provision sorted. Then you fill the gaps with specialist stops.

**Buy here:** Everything you normally buy at a bulk warehouse
**Skip:** Guest-level specialty items. Costco is crew-and-pantry. La Placita and Río Piedras are where the guest-level sourcing happens.

---

### Bread & Bakery

**Order online (phone):**
- Local panaderías deliver — call 24h ahead for volume. Ask your marina or a local crew member for the nearest bakery that will do a morning delivery run.

**Go in person:**
- **Panadería España** (Hato Rey) — Puerto Rican pan sobao (soft, slightly sweet enriched bread) and pan de agua (crusty water bread, the local baguette equivalent). Both are excellent crew bread. For guest service, the pan de agua makes a credible toast or crostini base.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rican bakery bread is fresh, cheap, and genuinely good — but it does not keep. Buy D-1 or D-0. For longer storage, buy from Costco (packaged artisan loaves) and freeze, or stock flour and bake on board. If you need high-protein bread flour (12%+), buy it at Costco or Sam's Club in bulk before you leave PR. Once you reach the eastern Caribbean, bread flour is scarce and overpriced.

**Buy here:** Pan sobao, pan de agua for crew. Bread flour in bulk for baking on board.
**Skip:** Expecting Parisian-level artisan sourdough. This is Caribbean bread culture — different tradition, different strengths.

---

### Produce & Fruit

**Go in person:**
- **Plaza del Mercado de Río Piedras** — The working market of San Juan. Not a tourist market — a neighbourhood market where locals buy their produce daily. Plantains (green for tostones, ripe for maduros), avocados, recao (culantro — visually similar to cilantro but a different plant with a deeper, more complex flavour), ají dulce peppers (sweet Caribbean pepper, zero heat, extraordinary aromatic profile), breadfruit, yams, calabaza, tropical fruit in season (guava, passion fruit, papaya, soursop, starfruit).

> **Smart tip:** Lean into what Puerto Rico grows. The tropical produce at Río Piedras is local, fresh, and cheap. Imported European produce — asparagus, Belgian endive, French beans — costs double what it does in Miami for the same quality. Adapt your guest menu to the market: a breadfruit velouté, a mahi ceviche with ají dulce and local avocado, a tropical fruit dessert with guava and passionfruit. The ingredient quality supports it and the guest gets a story with their plate.

**Buy here:** Recao (in bulk — freezes well), ají dulce, plantains, local avocados, tropical fruit, calabaza
**Skip:** Imported European vegetables. Buy those at Costco if you must, or wait until your next hub port.

---

### Dry Store & Pantry

**Order online:**
- **Amazon Prime** — Standard US delivery to PR addresses. No import duty. Order non-perishable specialty items D-5: specific oils, vinegars, specialty flours, spice blends, vacuum bags, parchment paper, anything that weighs a lot and is annoying to carry. It arrives at a PR address in 2-3 days with Prime.

**Go in person:**
- **Costco / Sam's Club** — Bulk dry goods at mainland wholesale prices. Olive oil (5L tins), flour (bulk bags), sugar, rice, pasta, canned tomatoes, coconut milk, cleaning supplies, cling film, aluminium foil, bin bags.

> **Smart tip:** Load canned goods, oils, flour, sugar, rice, and pasta here. These items cost 40-80% more once you reach islands with import duty. On a 40-footer heading to the eastern Caribbean for 3 weeks, you can easily save USD 500-800 on dry stores alone by loading in San Juan vs buying the same items in Antigua or St Lucia. Your storage capacity is the only limit. Fill every locker.

**Buy here:** Everything non-perishable in bulk. This is the best-priced loading opportunity before the eastern Caribbean.
**Skip:** Nothing. If it is dry, canned, or shelf-stable and you need it, buy it here.

---

### Frozen

**Go in person:**
- **Costco / Sam's Club** — Frozen proteins, frozen vegetables, frozen seafood, ice cream, frozen prepared items for crew convenience. Standard US frozen range at US prices.

> **Smart tip:** Frozen goods are the single biggest price differential between Puerto Rico and the eastern Caribbean. A case of frozen shrimp that costs USD 28 at Sam's Club in Carolina costs USD 45-55 in Antigua. Frozen vegetables follow the same pattern. Load the freezer to capacity. Organise by category (proteins, veg, seafood, crew), label everything, and draw a freezer map so you are not digging through chaos at sea.

**Buy here:** Maximum freezer load. Every category.
**Skip:** Cheap frozen prepared meals for crew when you can buy crew-grade fresh proteins at the same price and cook properly.

---

### Ethnic & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Pitusa** (multiple locations across San Juan metro area) — Dominican and Caribbean grocery chain. Carries Caribbean staples that Costco does not: specific rice brands, dried beans (habichuelas), sazón, achiote, sofrito, mojo, Caribbean hot sauces, green banana, yautía, malanga. Multiple locations — find the nearest to your marina route.
- **Asian markets in Santurce** — Small shops with basic Asian staples: soy sauce, rice vinegar, sesame oil, rice noodles, coconut milk, curry paste. The selection is limited compared to Miami's 168 Market. If your menu relies heavily on Asian ingredients, stock those in Miami or order via Amazon Prime before arrival.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rico's ethnic grocery strength is Caribbean and Latin American — not Asian. If you need lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, miso paste, or specialty Japanese/Thai ingredients, either order them via Amazon Prime D-5 or plan to source them in Miami. For Caribbean cooking, Pitusa and Río Piedras market have everything you need at the right price.

**Buy here:** Caribbean staples — sofrito, sazón, achiote, dried beans, Caribbean hot sauces, mojo
**Skip:** Expecting a full Asian pantry. Source that in Miami or online.

---

### Wine & Spirits

**Go in person:**
- **Costco** (spirits section) — Standard Costco spirits pricing. Puerto Rico has lower spirits excise tax than the mainland US, so rum in particular is well-priced. Vodka, gin, whisky, tequila — all at bulk pricing.
- **Bodegas Compostela** — Wine specialist. Better selection and curation than Costco for guest-level wines. Staff know what they carry. Worth the stop if the wine list matters for the charter.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rican rum at source is a provisioning advantage. Ron del Barrilito (three-star) is a serious aged sipping rum that costs USD 40-50 on-island and is difficult to find off-island. Don Q Gran Añejo and Reserva are solid mid-range options. Stock the boat's rum selection here — your Caribbean guests will expect good rum, and sourcing it at origin is both cheaper and a better story than buying Bacardi at a duty-free shop.

**Buy here:** Rum — full range from mixing to sipping. Spirits in bulk at Costco. Wine for the charter at Bodegas Compostela.
**Skip:** Premium Champagne and Bordeaux — better selection in SXM (duty-free, European import) or Miami.

---

## Provision Schedule

Work backwards from charter day. One major run per day, nothing rushed.

| When | What | Method | Where |
|------|------|--------|-------|
| **D-5** | Amazon Prime orders (specialty dry goods, vacuum bags, equipment) | Online | Deliver to marina or PR address |
| **D-3** | Bulk dry store, frozen, cleaning, bulk beverages | In-person wholesale run | Costco + Sam's Club (same trip, they are 10 min apart in Carolina) |
| **D-2** | Meat, dairy, cheese, wine, spirits | In-person | Restaurant Depot (AM) + Costco overflow + Bodegas Compostela |
| **D-1** | Fish, produce, bread, ethnic staples | In-person morning run | La Placita (07:00) + Río Piedras market + panadería + Pitusa |
| **D-0** | Ultra-perishables, herbs, last-minute requests | As needed | Pueblo or Econo (nearest supermarket chain) |

> **Smart tip:** The D-3 Costco + Sam's Club run is the big one — allow 3-4 hours including loading. Restaurant Depot on D-2 is faster (1-2 hours) because you know exactly what you need by then. The D-1 market run is the early morning — La Placita by 07:00, Río Piedras by 09:00, bread by 10:00, done by lunch. This schedule means you provision an entire boat in three working days with one early start.

---

## Price Reality

The numbers that matter. San Juan vs what you would pay at the next Caribbean port.

| Item | In Eastern Caribbean (Antigua) | In San Juan | Source | Saving |
|------|-------------------------------|-------------|--------|--------|
| Butter /kg | USD 18 | USD 9 | Costco | ~50% |
| USDA Choice striploin /kg | USD 55 | USD 32 | Costco | ~42% |
| Bread flour 5kg | USD 14 | USD 6 | Sam's Club | ~57% |
| Olive oil 5L | USD 40 | USD 22 | Costco | ~45% |
| Frozen shrimp /kg | USD 32 | USD 18 | Sam's Club | ~44% |
| Canned tomatoes /case (12) | USD 30 | USD 14 | Costco | ~53% |

**Bottom line:** On a USD 8,000 full provision, self-sourcing in San Juan instead of the eastern Caribbean saves approximately USD 3,000-4,000. That is 6-8 hours of your time across three days at an effective rate of USD 375-660/h. There is no version of this maths where provisioning at the next port makes sense — unless you enjoy paying import duty for the experience.

---

## If You Have No Time — The Agent

There is no dedicated yacht provisioning agent in San Juan comparable to Epicerie De Marie in SXM or Gourmet Deliveries in Palma. This is not a problem. It is a feature.

San Juan is compact. The marina-to-Costco drive is 20 minutes. Restaurant Depot is on the way. La Placita is in town. The entire island is 165km long. You do not need an agent — you need a rental car and one focused day.

If you genuinely have zero time (you dock at noon, charter starts at 18:00 tomorrow, you have not provisioned), your options are:

1. **Instacart** — Schedule Costco and Walmart deliveries to the marina or a nearby address. Same-day delivery available. Covers general grocery, bulk, and frozen. Not guest-level, but it gets the boat fed.
2. **Call the marina** — Marina staff in San Juan know local runners and fixers. Ask for a recommendation. Expect to pay a premium for the urgency, but someone will make the runs for you.
3. **Split the work** — Send a crew member to Costco with the bulk list. You go to La Placita and Río Piedras for the guest-level product. Panadería and Pitusa on the way back. Two people, 4 hours, done.

> **Smart tip:** Option 3 is the real answer. It is not an agent — it is delegation. One person handles Costco (the easy list), you handle the markets (the judgement calls). This is faster than any agent and costs nothing beyond the rental car.

---

## Local Gems

What San Juan does better or cheaper than anywhere else on your Caribbean route. Stock up — you will not find this quality or price at the next stop.

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Recao (culantro) | Río Piedras market | Caribbean herb that adds depth and complexity to everything — pennies per bunch. Freezes well. Buy 20 bunches and vacuum-seal. |
| Ají dulce peppers | Río Piedras market | Sweet Caribbean pepper, zero heat, extraordinary perfume. Unavailable off-island. Use in ceviches, sauces, garnishes. Freeze what you do not use immediately. |
| Puerto Rican coffee (Yaucono, Alto Grande, Café Lareno) | Any supermarket | Excellent quality, USD 5-8 per 400g bag. Guests love the provenance story. Buy 5-10 bags. |
| Ron del Barrilito (three-star) | Specialty liquor shops or El Barrachina (Old San Juan) | Aged sipping rum. Impresses guests who think they know Caribbean rum. USD 40-50 at source. |
| Local honey | Río Piedras market | Puerto Rican wildflower honey. Small-batch, unpasteurised. Guest breakfast staple. |
| Sofrito (fresh, homemade) | Pitusa or Río Piedras market vendors | The base of Puerto Rican cooking. Buy fresh, freeze in ice cube trays. Transforms rice, beans, braises, sauces. |
| Pique (local hot sauce) | Río Piedras market / small producers | Vinegar-based Caribbean hot sauce with ají caballero. Each producer's is different. Buy 3-4 bottles from different vendors. |

---

## Marinas & Logistics

**Where yachts dock:**
- **Marina Puerto del Rey** (Fajardo, east coast) — The largest marina in the Caribbean. 1,000+ slips. Full services. 45 minutes from San Juan metro area. If you are berthed here, Costco Caguas is closer than Carolina.
- **Club Nautico de San Juan** — In the heart of San Juan. Convenient for market runs but limited megayacht facilities.
- **San Juan Bay Marina** — Near the cruise port area. Functional, central.

**Rental car:** Essential. Unlike Miami where you might use delivery services, San Juan provisioning requires driving between stops. Rent on arrival, return after the D-1 market run. 3-4 days. Budget USD 120-200 total.

**Delivery to marina:** Instacart delivers to addresses near marinas but not to dock side. Have packages and online orders sent to a marina-adjacent address or the marina office if they accept deliveries (confirm in advance).

> **Smart tip:** If you are berthed at Puerto del Rey in Fajardo, adjust the schedule. Costco Caguas and Restaurant Depot are 30-40 minutes west. La Placita and Río Piedras are 45 minutes. Plan for longer drives but the same sequence. Alternatively, there are small fish markets and produce vendors in the Fajardo area — ask the marina staff.

---

## Provision Forward: What Disappears After PR

This is the critical intelligence. Once you leave San Juan heading east, the following become scarce, expensive, or both:

| Item | Available in PR | Available in USVI/BVI | Available in Eastern Caribbean |
|------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| USDA-grade beef | Costco, Restaurant Depot | Limited, expensive | Rare, very expensive |
| Bread flour 12%+ | Costco, Sam's Club | Scarce | Very scarce |
| Frozen shrimp (quality) | Costco, Sam's Club | Available, marked up | Available, heavily marked up |
| Asian ingredients | Santurce shops, Amazon | Very limited | Near-zero |
| Bulk dry goods (wholesale) | Costco, Sam's Club | Some (Cost U Less in USVI) | Limited, import-taxed |
| Caribbean produce | Río Piedras market | Some local | Excellent local on some islands |
| Fresh herbs | Río Piedras, supermarkets | Limited | Very limited (BVI growers are the exception) |

**Load everything in the first five rows here. The last two rows improve as you go south — Caribbean produce and herbs get better on the islands that grow them.**

---

## The Bottom Line

San Juan is the most underused provisioning port in the Caribbean. It has US wholesale infrastructure, zero import duty, Amazon Prime delivery, and enough local food culture to make the provisioning interesting rather than just transactional. The Río Piedras market is worth visiting even if your freezer is full.

The single best move: treat San Juan as a full loading port. Costco + Restaurant Depot + La Placita + Río Piedras + one Amazon order. Three days. Full boat. USD 3,000-4,000 less than provisioning the same items in the eastern Caribbean.

Then head east with a full freezer, full lockers, and the knowledge that you are carrying recao, ají dulce, and Ron del Barrilito — three things that will make your time in the Caribbean taste like the Caribbean.

Prices and availability shift season to season. Verify before you commit a full provision to any single source. But the structural advantage — US pricing, no duty, full infrastructure — does not change. San Juan is a loading port. Use it as one.

**Source:** Aggregated from recommendations across the Chefs Helping Chefs (~1,025 members) and Yacht Chef Group (~300+ members) WhatsApp communities and working yacht chefs based in Puerto Rico and the USVI, 2025-2026 season. Prices are approximate and subject to change.

The systems used by those who stay.* Across two major yacht chef WhatsApp groups — combined 1,300+ members, approximately 24,000 messages analysed from July 2025 to January 2026 — the single most emotionally charged topic is not provisioning, not equipment, not salary. It is the unsustaina…
drafts.body_md:
# Sole Chef on a 50-Metre: The Workload Nobody Talks About

*The data on why chefs leave. The systems used by those who stay.*

Across two major yacht chef WhatsApp groups — combined 1,300+ members, approximately 24,000 messages analysed from July 2025 to January 2026 — the single most emotionally charged topic is not provisioning, not equipment, not salary. It is the unsustainable workload imposed on sole chefs aboard yachts in the 40-50 metre range.

Captains complain they cannot retain chefs. Chefs explain exactly why. A poll asked members whether they would join a boat with a known problematic galley and demanding sole chef role. The result: **50 voted "No I wouldn't join that boat." 0 voted "Yes."**

Fifty to zero.

This article has two parts. The first documents the reality — data, numbers, working conditions — because you cannot fix what you do not name. The second provides the operational systems used by sole chefs who have survived and even thrived in the role. Not because the conditions are acceptable, but because if you are in the middle of a charter season, you need tools that work today.

---

## PART ONE: THE REALITY

### What the Numbers Look Like

A sole chef on a 50m yacht during charter typically feeds 12 guests and 10-14 crew. Three meals per day for guests (breakfast, lunch, dinner), with canapes, afternoon tea, and late-night snacks often added. Three meals per day for crew. On a single charter day, a sole chef produces 50-70 individual plates across 6-8 service windows, plus prep for the following day.

That is a small restaurant's output. With no sous chef, no commis, no kitchen porter.

### What 18 Hours Looks Like

16-18 hour days are consistently documented. Here is the typical charter day:

| Time | Activity | Notes |
|------|----------|-------|
| 05:00 | Wake, crew breakfast prep | — |
| 06:30 | Crew breakfast service | — |
| 07:00 | Guest breakfast mise en place | Varies by guest wake times |
| 08:00-10:00 | Guest breakfast service | Cooked to order, staggered |
| 10:00-11:30 | Clean down, lunch prep | — |
| 11:30 | Crew lunch service | — |
| 12:00-14:00 | Guest lunch service | — |
| 14:00-15:00 | Clean down, afternoon tea prep | — |
| 15:00-16:00 | Afternoon service, begin dinner prep | — |
| 16:00-17:30 | Dinner mise en place | — |
| 17:30 | Crew dinner service | — |
| 18:30 | Canape service | — |
| 19:30-22:00 | Guest dinner service (3-5 courses) | — |
| 22:00-23:00 | Clean down, next-day prep, late-night snacks | — |

That is 18 hours. Every day of a charter.

### The Falsification Problem

The Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) requires a minimum of 10 hours of rest in any 24-hour period, with at least 6 consecutive hours. The schedule above provides 5-6 hours.

"The whole faking hours of work and rest needs to stop." — quoted directly from the groups.

Compliance is achieved on paper through falsified rest logs. This is widespread, openly discussed, and universally understood by captains, management companies, and crew. It is not an allegation. It is a structural reality described by the people living it.

### The Capacity Data

Working chefs have shared their operational limits with unusual candour:

| Chef | Scope | Key Insight |
|------|-------|------------|
| **Chef A** | 12 guests + 4-5 crew | Note the crew number: 4-5, not 10-14. Guest count is manageable. Crew numbers are the compounding factor. |
| **Chef B** | 51 charters as sole freelance, 10 crew + 12 guests | 51 charters is a significant dataset. Freelance — can walk away after each charter. Permanent sole chefs cannot. |
| **Chef C** | 12 guests + 18 crew, sole | "Depends on appliances and fridge/freezer space." The galley determines what is possible more than stamina. |
| **Chef D** | (from group) | "How am I meant to produce Michelin grade dishes while also cooking breakfast lunch and dinner for 12 crew?" |

The answer is: you are not. But the expectation persists.

### The Galley Multiplier

The workload conversation cannot be separated from the galley. On yachts under 50m, the galley is frequently an afterthought — whatever space remains after guest cabins, crew quarters, engine room, and entertainment areas.

One chef described it bluntly: "One domestic sized oven, fridge and freezer — all half the size of ones in my home. Lack of storage and prep area can be an absolute joke."

Another noted: "Galley size and quality is not discussed enough."

From the groups, recurring: "The beating heart of a boat is the galley — why are they so shit on below 50m?"

A sole chef working 18-hour days in an under-equipped galley is not just fatigued — they are physically constrained. A single domestic oven cannot produce a 5-course dinner for 12 while simultaneously preparing crew meals. The maths does not work, regardless of the chef's talent.

### The Rotation Double Standard

Captains and officers work rotational schedules — 6:6, 8:6, sometimes 10:10. The same boats frequently employ a sole chef with no rotation. The chef is on board for the season, or on a 10-month contract with 2 months leave.

One widely-shared post — "5:1 is NOT rotation" — received universal agreement. Being on board for 5 months with 1 month off is not a rotational schedule. It is a permanent position with leave.

Captains against rotation for chefs but having it for themselves. The structural unfairness is not lost on the community.

---

## PART TWO: THE SURVIVAL SYSTEMS

You cannot fix the structural problem from inside the galley during charter. What you can do is build systems that convert a chaotic 18-hour day into a structured one — reducing cognitive load, eliminating redundant work, and creating pockets of rest that would not exist otherwise.

These are the protocols used by sole chefs who have sustained the role across multiple seasons. They are not aspirational. They are operational.

### System 1: The Three-Block Day

Stop thinking in 8 service windows. Think in three blocks. Each block has a defined output, a defined cleanup, and a defined transition.

| Block | Time | Output | Ends With |
|-------|------|--------|-----------|
| **Morning** | 05:00-11:00 | Crew breakfast, guest breakfast, lunch prep | Galley clean, lunch mise complete |
| **Midday** | 11:00-17:00 | Crew lunch, guest lunch, afternoon service, dinner prep | Galley clean, dinner mise complete |
| **Evening** | 17:00-23:00 | Crew dinner, canapes, guest dinner, next-day prep | Galley clean, next morning's dry mise staged |

Each block is 6 hours. Each block ends with a clean galley and the next block's mise in place. The discipline is in the transitions: when the morning block ends, the galley is reset. You do not carry mess from one block into the next. This seems like extra work. It is not. The 3-minute wipe-and-reset between blocks eliminates the 15-minute panic when you cannot find a clean surface at 17:30.

> **Why this works:** Cognitive load research (Sweller, 1988) shows that working memory degrades when tasks are not segmented. An 18-hour unstructured day creates decision fatigue by hour 10. Three defined blocks with clear start-stop boundaries reduce the cognitive demand of each segment. You are not working 18 hours. You are working three 6-hour shifts.

### System 2: The Monday Batch

Every Monday — or the first day of each charter — invest 3 hours in batch production that eliminates daily scramble for the rest of the week.

| Component | Quantity | Time | Covers |
|-----------|----------|------|--------|
| **Stock** (chicken or vegetable) | 10L | 30 min active (simmer unattended) | Sauces, risottos, soups all week |
| **Grain/starch** (rice pilaf, roasted potatoes, couscous) | 5 kg | 20 min | Crew lunches and dinners, 5 days |
| **Sauce base** (bolognese, curry, tomato sugo) | 3L | 30 min | Crew meals, staff meals, pasta service |
| **Vegetable prep** (roasted roots, blanched greens, pickled onions) | 3 kg | 25 min | Crew and guest garnish, salads |
| **Protein prep** (marinated chicken thighs, portioned fish, braised crew protein) | 5 kg | 30 min | Crew protein + guest mise backups |
| **Dressings and vinaigrettes** | 1.5L total | 15 min | Crew salads, guest salads, all week |

**Total active time: ~2.5 hours.** Much of it runs concurrently (stock simmers while you prep veg).

**What this buys you:** Crew lunch goes from 30-45 minutes of reactive cooking to 10-15 minutes of assembly. Crew dinner: same. That is 40-60 minutes saved per day, every day, for the rest of the charter. Over a 7-day charter, the Monday batch saves 4-7 hours of cumulative time.

Those saved hours are not theoretical. They are the difference between starting dinner prep at 16:00 with your mise done and starting at 16:00 from scratch.

### System 3: The Crew Meal Machine

Crew meals are the workload multiplier. Not because they are difficult — because they are unstructured. Every meal becomes a negotiation.

**The protocol (detailed in the separate Crew Meal Diplomacy article):**

1. **Set dietary terms at embarkation.** Allergies: accommodated, always. Medical conditions: accommodated. TikTok diets adopted last Tuesday: not accommodated. State this clearly, once, at the start.

2. **Cook one meal with built-in flexibility.** Base protein + starch + vegetable. Salad bar or grain bowl station crew can customise. The vegan gets the vegetables, the rice, and a batch-prepped plant protein you made on Monday. The ketchup crew member gets ketchup. Ego is the enemy here.

3. **Use the Monday batch.** Monday's 2.5 hours of prep converts every crew meal from a 30-minute cook to a 10-minute assembly. Reheat the grain. Portion the protein. Dress the salad. Done.

4. **Escalate, do not absorb.** When a crew member requests bespoke daily cooking (keto bread, full carnivore, raw food), respond to the captain: "This requires X additional hours per week. I can accommodate it if Y is adjusted." Let management decide. That is their job.

| Metric | Without system | With system |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Daily crew meal prep time | 30-45 min per meal | 10-15 min per meal |
| Dietary complaints per week | 3-5 | 0-1 |
| Chef decision fatigue from crew requests | High (every meal is a negotiation) | Low (system handles 90% of variation) |

### System 4: The Prep Cascade

Prep for tomorrow during today's downtime, not at the end of the day when you are destroyed.

| Today's Window | Tomorrow's Prep |
|----------------|-----------------|
| **After guest breakfast** (10:00-10:30) | Portion tomorrow's fish. Marinate tomorrow's crew protein. |
| **After guest lunch** (14:00-14:30) | Prep tomorrow's canape bases. Roll tomorrow's pasta if applicable. |
| **After afternoon service** (15:30-16:00) | Prep tomorrow's dessert components (set panna cotta, churn ice cream, bake tart shells). |
| **During dinner service waits** (between courses) | Prep next-day breakfast mise (portion smoked salmon, prep hollandaise base, cut fruit). |

The principle: never prep tomorrow's food after 21:00. By the time dinner service ends, tomorrow's mise should be 70-80% complete. The 22:00-23:00 slot becomes cleanup and staging only — not a second full prep session on top of a 17-hour day.

> **Why this works:** The prep cascade exploits the natural dead time between services. These 15-30 minute windows exist in every charter day but are typically wasted on phone-checking, chatting, or standing still because the next task is not defined. Assigning each window a specific next-day prep task converts waste time into production time without adding a single minute to the working day.

### System 5: The Freezer Is Your Sous Chef

If you have no sous chef, your freezer is the closest substitute. Use it as a labour bank — deposit time on slow days, withdraw it on busy days.

**What to freeze in advance (before charter, or on travel/setup days):**

| Item | Freeze method | Shelf life | Deploys as |
|------|--------------|------------|------------|
| Soups (3-4 varieties) | Portion into 500ml containers | 3 months | Crew lunch. Heat and serve. 5 minutes. |
| Sauces (bolognese, curry, ragu) | Flat-freeze in bags, 500ml portions | 3 months | Crew dinner base. Defrost and reheat. |
| Braised meats (short rib, lamb shank, oxtail) | Vacuum-sealed in cooking liquid | 3 months | Guest dinner main. Reheat from frozen in 45 min. |
| Bread dough (portioned) | Wrap in cling film, bag | 1 month | Proof from frozen (2h room temp). Fresh bread daily without daily mixing. |
| Cookie and cake batters | Scooped or portioned | 2 months | Bake from frozen. Fresh dessert, zero day-of prep. |
| Pie and tart shells (blind-baked) | Stack with parchment between | 2 months | Fill and finish. 15 min to a plated dessert. |
| Canape bases (blinis, crackers, pastry cups) | Flat-freeze on trays, then bag | 1 month | Defrost 30 min before service. Top and go. |

**Before every charter:** Spend one full day building a freezer arsenal. 8 hours of pre-charter batch cooking replaces 2-3 hours of daily scramble across a 7-day charter. That is 14-21 hours recovered, redistributed from charter days (when you are drowning) to a prep day (when you are not).

### System 6: The Steward Handoff

You cannot do everything alone. You should not. The stew team has hands, and some of what currently lands on your plate belongs on theirs.

**What a trained stew can take off your hands:**

| Task | Time saved per day | Training required |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Breakfast mise assembly (fruit, yogurt, granola, OJ, coffee setup) | 20-30 min | 15 min one-time walkthrough |
| Plating crew meals from your assembled components | 10-15 min | Show once, they repeat |
| Afternoon tea assembly (from your pre-made components) | 15-20 min | Photograph the setup, they replicate |
| Late-night snack station setup | 10-15 min | Pre-prep the station, they deploy |
| Running dishwasher between services | 10-15 min per cycle | Zero training needed |

**Total potential daily saving: 65-95 minutes.**

> **The catch:** This requires captain and chief stew buy-in. Some stew teams are willing. Some are not. Some chief stews will fight it. Do not make it a battle — present it to the captain as a workload redistribution request: "If the stew team handles breakfast assembly and dishwasher runs, I gain 45 minutes of prep time that directly improves guest dinner quality." Frame it as a quality argument, not a fairness argument. Captains respond to quality.

### System 7: The Menu Architecture

Do not design menus in isolation. Design them as a system where every guest dish generates crew meal components.

| Guest dinner (Day 2) | Crew lunch (Day 3) |
|----------------------|-------------------|
| Seared duck breast, cherry reduction, celeriac puree | Pulled duck leg (from carcass), cherry vinaigrette salad, celeriac gratin (from puree trim) |
| Pan-roasted sea bass, beurre blanc, asparagus | Fish cakes (from bass trim and offcuts), asparagus and grain salad |
| Chocolate fondant, salted caramel, coffee crumb | Chocolate brownie (from excess fondant batter), caramel popcorn (crew snack from excess caramel) |

**The principle:** Nothing goes in the bin that can go on a crew plate tomorrow. Plan this at the menu design stage, not after service when you are staring at trim.

Over a 7-day charter, this cross-utilisation reduces crew-specific prep by approximately 30% and food waste by 15-20%. That is time and money — both of which you need.

---

## PART THREE: WHAT TO NEGOTIATE

If you are considering a sole chef position, or renegotiating an existing one, these are the non-negotiables that experienced sole chefs — the ones who survived — say matter most.

### Before You Accept

| Question | Why It Matters | Minimum Acceptable |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| What oven? | A domestic oven cannot produce charter-level output. | Commercial combi (Rational iCombi Pro or equivalent) |
| Fridge/freezer capacity? | Ask in litres, not "how many fridges." | 800L+ fridge, 500L+ freezer for 12+12 |
| Crew count? | Crew meals are the multiplier. | Know the exact number. Factor it into your decision. |
| Stew support in galley? | Breakfast assembly, dishwasher, afternoon tea. | Written agreement on specific tasks. |
| Rotation? | If the captain rotates, you rotate. | 2:2 or 3:3. "5:1" is not rotation. |
| Day rate for extra charters? | Overtime must be compensated. | Agreed per-charter supplement in contract. |

### The Walk-Away Test

If a boat offers:
- Domestic appliances, single fridge, no freezer capacity
- 12+ crew with no stew galley support
- No rotation for the chef while officers rotate
- "We need Michelin quality" with a Mercadona-grade galley

Walk. The retention crisis is your leverage. Good yacht chefs are in demand. Positions that destroy their people do not deserve to be filled.

---

## The Structural Problem

This article provides survival systems. It does not pretend the conditions are acceptable.

The sole chef role on sub-50m yachts has crossed from demanding into unsustainable for many positions. The retention crisis is not a recruitment problem, not a skills gap, not a generational attitude issue. It is the predictable consequence of asking one person to do a job that requires two, in a space designed for zero, for 18 hours a day, with falsified rest logs and no rotation.

504 meals per week. One chef. Do the division.

The industry will not fix this from the top. It will fix it when enough qualified chefs refuse to accept the conditions that make the role untenable. Until then, use the systems. Protect your time. Negotiate from strength. And know that the 50-to-zero poll result means you are not alone in recognising what this job actually costs.

---

## Key Details

| Detail | Data |
|--------|------|
| Typical sole chef scope | 12 guests + 10-14 crew, 6-8 service windows per day |
| Documented working hours | 16-18 hours per day during charter |
| MLC minimum rest | 10 hours per 24h period, 6 consecutive |
| Rest log compliance | Widely falsified per chef reports |
| Poll: "Would you join a problematic sole chef role?" | 50 No, 0 Yes |
| Monday batch time investment | 2.5 hours |
| Monday batch time saved (per charter) | 4-7 hours |
| Prep cascade daily time saved | 1-2 hours |
| Steward handoff daily time saved | 65-95 minutes |
| Preferred rotation | 2:2 (equal time on/off) |
| Common reality | No rotation, or 5:1 |

---

## Your Move

If you are a sole chef mid-charter: implement the three-block day and the Monday batch this week. They cost nothing and return hours.

If you are between positions: negotiate galley specifications, crew count, stew support, and rotation before you sign. The walk-away test is not arrogance. It is self-preservation.

If you are a captain reading this: the galley is not a lesser department. The work is not less demanding. The chef who feeds your guests and your crew — to the standard you demand — deserves the same structural support you give your bridge team. Rotation, proper equipment, and honest rest logs are not chef luxuries. They are the minimum conditions for sustainable performance.

The chef at 11pm, 17 hours into a charter day, cleaning down a galley alone — that chef is the reason your guests come back. Act accordingly.

**Source:** Aggregated from discussions, polls, and direct testimony across the Chefs Helping Chefs (~1,025 members) and Yacht Chef Group (~300+ members) WhatsApp communities, July 2025 – January 2026. MLC regulations referenced per ILO Maritime Labour Convention, 2006, as amended. Cognitive load research: Sweller, J. (1988), "Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning," *Cognitive Science*, 12(2), 257-285. All quotes are verbatim from group discussions. Names anonymised.

drafts.title: The Provisioning Bible: Mediterranean

No chandler markup required.* Every supplier listed here has been recommended by working yacht chefs, most of them multiple times, across both the Chefs Helping Chefs and Yacht Chef Group communities. Where problems have been reported, those are listed too. The chandler markup acros…
drafts.body_md:
# The Provisioning Bible: Mediterranean

*Port-by-port. Supplier-by-supplier. No chandler markup required.*

Every supplier listed here has been recommended by working yacht chefs, most of them multiple times, across both the Chefs Helping Chefs and Yacht Chef Group communities. Where problems have been reported, those are listed too.

The chandler markup across the western Med runs 15-30%. Self-sourcing the bulk of your provision and using a chandler only for logistics and last-mile delivery saves thousands per charter. That is the entire premise of this guide.

---

## Metro Card Logistics

Metro operates differently in each country. Your card from one country does not work in another.

| Country | What You Need | Processing | Notes |
|---------|--------------|------------|-------|
| **Italy** | Italian fiscal code (codice fiscale) | Walk-in | Works at any Metro in Italy. Does NOT work abroad. |
| **France** | Boat registration, crew list, captain's passport | 1-2 days | Max 3 cards per vessel. Bring all documents in person. |
| **Spain** | CIF (company/management tax ID), crew passports, boat registration | 1-2 days | Easier than France, still not walk-in. |
| **Greece** | Boat registration, crew passport | Same day | Relatively straightforward. |

> **Smart tip:** If you are doing a western Med season (Spain → France → Italy), start Metro paperwork at your first port. You need three separate cards. Do not arrive in Antibes assuming your Italian card works. Budget one morning per country for the admin.

---

## Palma de Mallorca

**Port type: Major hub.** More yachts provision here than anywhere else in the Med. One of the few ports where you can self-source to Michelin-level standards without a chandler.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Gourmet Deliveries** (full catalogue) | **Mercadona** (crew) / **Metro** (bulk) | Gourmet Deliveries: order online, delivery to STP and Club de Mar |
| **Fish** | **Gourmet Deliveries** (48h notice for specific species) | **Santa Catalina Market** | Market for daily catch. Gourmet Deliveries for volume and species requests. |
| **Meat** | **Simon Jones / Meat & Fish Society** (WhatsApp) | — | Simon Jones: THE protein source for the Balearics. WhatsApp order 72h ahead. |
| **Bread** | — | **Santa Catalina Market** bakeries | Stock up on Caputo 13% protein flour — scarce elsewhere in the western Med |
| **Produce** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Santa Catalina Market** (Carina / Frutas Ramon) | Carina carries international goods + excellent local produce. One stop. |
| **Dry Store** | **Gourmet Deliveries** / **We Supply Yachts** | **Metro Palma** | Metro for bulk. Gourmet Deliveries for convenience. |
| **Frozen** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Metro Palma** | Metro freezer section beats chandler by 30-40% |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Metro Palma** (Asian/international aisle) | Limited. Better in Barcelona or Antibes. |
| **Wine & Spirits** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | Specialist wine merchants in Palma old town | — |
| **Agent** | **Gourmet Deliveries** (16 votes) / **We Supply Yachts** (10 votes) | — | Full-service provisioning. GD accommodates last-minute changes. |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Order online:**
- **Gourmet Deliveries** — Order specific species with 48h notice. They source from local fishermen and wholesale markets. Delivery to STP and Club de Mar.

**Go in person:**
- **Santa Catalina Market** — The working chef's market. Daily local catch. Arrive before 08:00 for best selection.

> **Smart tip:** For day-of sashimi-grade product, go to the market yourself and inspect. For volume or specific species (whole turbot, langoustines for 12), order through Gourmet Deliveries — they can guarantee the order where the market cannot.

#### Meat & Protein

**Order online (WhatsApp):**
- **Simon Jones / The Meat & Fish Society** — Covers Mallorca and Ibiza, ships to Sardinia. Wagyu, dry-aged cuts, premium seafood. Universally praised across both chef groups. WhatsApp your order 72h ahead.

> **Smart tip:** Simon Jones is the single most recommended protein supplier in the entire Med. Contact him before your first Balearics charter. He knows what guest-level means and delivers accordingly. If your next stop is Sardinia, he ships there too.

**Buy here:** Guest-level proteins for the entire Balearics leg
**Skip:** Mercadona for guest meat. It is crew-grade only.

#### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Gourmet Deliveries** — Most-voted supplier in a recent group poll (16 votes). Broad catalogue, reliable delivery, accommodates last-minute changes. Order online, delivery to STP and Club de Mar.
- **We Supply Yachts** — 10 votes in the same poll. Good range, competitive on staples.

**Go in person:**
- **Mercadona** — Crew-level groceries. Consistent, cheap, good enough. Do not source guest provisions here.
- **Metro Palma** — Spanish Metro card required (see logistics section). Bulk dry goods, dairy, frozen. Professional pricing.

> **Smart tip:** Order guest provision through Gourmet Deliveries (D-3). Do the Metro run for bulk crew and dry store (D-2). Mercadona for crew snacks and basics (D-2 same trip). Simon Jones delivers proteins D-1. Market run D-1 morning for fish, produce, bread. Five contacts, three days, complete provision.

#### Bread & Bakery

**Go in person:**
- **Santa Catalina Market** bakeries — Good quality local bread.

> **Smart tip:** Bread flour sourcing is a perennial headache in the Med. **Caputo flour at 13% protein** is the recommendation from multiple chefs. Available at specialty food suppliers and some Metro locations in Palma. Stock up here — 12%+ protein flour is not consistently available in the western Med.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-3** | Gourmet Deliveries order placed. Simon Jones WhatsApp for proteins. | Online |
| **D-2** | Metro run (dry store, bulk frozen, crew dairy). Mercadona (crew snacks). | In person |
| **D-1** | Santa Catalina Market (fish 07:00, produce, bread). Simon Jones delivery. Gourmet Deliveries delivery. | In person + deliveries |
| **D-0** | Ultra-perishables, herbs, last-minute adjustments | Santa Catalina or Mercadona |

### Price Reality

| Item | Via Chandler | Self-Sourced | Saving |
|------|-------------|--------------|--------|
| Wagyu striploin /kg | EUR 130 | EUR 95 @ Simon Jones | ~27% |
| Local sea bass /kg | EUR 35 | EUR 22 @ Santa Catalina | ~37% |
| Olive oil 5L | EUR 40 | EUR 24 @ Metro | ~40% |

**Bottom line:** Palma self-sourcing saves 25-40% across the board. On a EUR 12,000 provision, that is EUR 3,000-4,800 back in the owner's pocket.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Sobrassada, ensaimada | Any good Palma bakery/deli | Mallorcan specialties. Guest-worthy with the right presentation. |
| Caputo 13% flour | Metro / specialty suppliers | Stock up. This disappears once you leave Palma. |
| Mallorcan almonds, olive oil | Santa Catalina Market | Local, excellent, fraction of exported price |

### Red Flag

**Delicioso (Palma)** — Marketed for crew meal delivery. Multiple reports of Mercadona-grade products sold at premium prices. Not worth the markup.

---

## Barcelona

**Port type: Strong transit stop.** Particularly for boats between the Balearics and the French Riviera. Self-sourcing is straightforward.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Metro Barcelona** | Larger selection than most Med Metro locations |
| **Fish** | — | **Mercat del Ninot** (fish counter) | Less crowded than Sant Antoni. Strong fish and meat counters. |
| **Meat** | — | **Mercat del Ninot** / **Metro** | Iberian pork: buy here. Jamones and embutidos cheaper at source than anywhere in France or Italy |
| **Bread** | — | **Mercat de Sant Antoni** area bakeries | — |
| **Produce** | — | **Mercat de Sant Antoni** | Lower tourist markup than Boqueria. Broader everyday selection. |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Metro Barcelona** | Well-stocked |
| **Frozen** | — | **Metro Barcelona** | — |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Raval neighbourhood** shops | Barcelona's Raval has Middle Eastern, South Asian, Chinese groceries |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Metro** / specialist wine shops in Born | — |
| **Agent** | — | — | Not a major agent port. Self-source — infrastructure is good. |

### Category Detail

#### Meat & Protein

**Go in person:**
- **Mercat del Ninot** — Strong meat counters, less crowded than the tourist markets. Near the Eixample marina facilities.

> **Smart tip:** Barcelona has excellent Iberian pork suppliers. Jamones and embutidos are significantly cheaper bought at source here than anywhere in France or Italy. If your itinerary allows, stock your cured meats in Barcelona. Vacuum-seal and they keep for weeks.

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Mercat del Ninot** — Good fish counter. Fresh, local, less tourist markup.
- **Mercat de Sant Antoni** — Broader selection, busier. Better for produce than fish.

#### Ethnic & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Raval neighbourhood** — Barcelona's most diverse quarter. Middle Eastern groceries, South Asian spice shops, Chinese supermarkets within walking distance of each other. Worth a 2-hour walkthrough to stock specialty items.

> **Smart tip:** La Boqueria is for tourists. Forget it. The working chef's markets are Sant Antoni and Ninot. Less spectacle, better prices, no one taking photos of your fish.

### Logistics Note

Yacht berths are spread across Port Vell, Port Olimpic, and OneOcean Port Vell. Confirm with suppliers which marina they deliver to directly.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Iberian jamón, embutidos | Mercat del Ninot / specialty shops | 30-50% cheaper at source than in France or Italy |
| Bomba rice (for paella) | Metro or Mercat de Sant Antoni | The right rice at the right price |

---

## Sardinia (Porto Cervo, Olbia, Cagliari)

**Port type: Thin infrastructure despite wealthy clientele.** Peak season July-August (Costa Smeralda circuit). Plan ahead.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Simon Jones** (ships from Palma) | **Metro Olbia** | Simon Jones ships proteins to Sardinia from Palma |
| **Fish** | — | **Riccardo Fish and Cheese** (Porto Cervo, comes to marina) / **Olbia fish market** / **Mercato di San Benedetto** (Cagliari) | Riccardo = "the cheese van guy." Multiple chef recommendations. |
| **Meat** | **Simon Jones** (ships from Palma) | — | Order from Palma. Local guest-grade meat is unreliable. |
| **Bread** | — | Local bakeries (pane carasau is Sardinian) | — |
| **Produce** | — | **Mercato di San Benedetto** (Cagliari) | One of the largest covered markets in Europe. Exceptional. |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Metro Olbia** | Italian Metro card required |
| **Frozen** | — | **Metro Olbia** | — |
| **Ethnic** | — | Not reliably available | Stock before arrival |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | Sardinian wine (Cannonau, Vermentino) from local enotecas | — |
| **Agent** | — | — | No dedicated yacht agent. Use Palma-based agents for protein and specialty. |

### Port-by-Port

**Porto Cervo:** Limited options. Riccardo Fish and Cheese comes to the marina — excellent local cheeses, decent fish. For anything else, order from Palma.

**Olbia:** Better supermarket infrastructure. Metro Olbia serviceable for staples. Fish market near the port — local catch quality is high, variety is seasonal and limited.

**Cagliari:** The best provisioning on the island. Mercato di San Benedetto — exceptional fish, local produce, Sardinian cheeses (pecorino sardo). Stock up here if your itinerary touches southern Sardinia.

> **Smart tip:** Order proteins from Simon Jones in Palma with Sardinia delivery. Do Metro Olbia for dry store and frozen. Use Riccardo at the marina for fish and cheese. Three contacts, complete provision.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Pecorino sardo (various ages) | Riccardo / Mercato di San Benedetto | Sardinian origin, exceptional quality, fraction of export price |
| Pane carasau | Any bakery | Sardinian flatbread. Guest-worthy as a canapé base. |
| Bottarga di muggine | Cagliari fish market / specialty shops | Mullet roe at source. Best quality, best price. |
| Cannonau wine | Local enotecas | Sardinian red. Excellent value. |

---

## Naples / Capri

**Port type: Exceptional food city.** Provisioning here is a genuine privilege. One of the best sourcing destinations in the Med.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Metro Napoli** | Italian Metro card. Stock Italian staples here. |
| **Fish** | — | **Porta Nolana fish markets** (waterfront) | Arrive before 08:00. Negotiate — prices flex for bulk. |
| **Meat** | **Io Sono La Chianina** (order ahead) | — | Chianina beef. T-bones described as "insane." Delivers to port/marina. |
| **Bread** | — | Any Neapolitan bakery | This is Naples. The bread is good everywhere. |
| **Produce** | — | **Porta Nolana market area** | San Marzano tomatoes, local buffalo mozzarella, basil — all cheaper here than anywhere north |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Metro Napoli** | Gragnano pasta at source. Load up. |
| **Frozen** | — | **Metro Napoli** | — |
| **Ethnic** | — | Limited | — |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | Local enotecas (Lacryma Christi, Taurasi, Fiano) | Campanian wines at source — excellent value |
| **Agent** | — | — | Not needed. Self-sourcing here is a pleasure, not a chore. |

### Category Detail

#### Meat & Protein

**Order online:**
- **Io Sono La Chianina** — Specialises in Chianina beef. Multiple chefs describe the T-bones as "insane." Delivery to the port area and marina facilities. Order 48h ahead.

> **Smart tip:** Io Sono La Chianina is to Naples what Simon Jones is to Palma. One phone call, guest-level beef sorted.

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Porta Nolana fish markets** (waterfront area) — Exceptional. Arrive before 08:00 — the best product goes first. Negotiate: prices are flexible, especially for bulk purchases.

> **Smart tip:** If your itinerary has you heading north toward the French Riviera, load up on everything Italian here. San Marzano tomatoes, Gragnano pasta, buffalo mozzarella — all at a fraction of what you pay in France. This is the last great Italian sourcing stop before the border.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| San Marzano DOP tomatoes | Metro / Porta Nolana area shops | At source. Buy cases. You will not find this quality or price again until you return to Italy. |
| Gragnano pasta | Metro / specialty shops | The pasta capital. At source prices. |
| Buffalo mozzarella | Porta Nolana market | Fresh daily from Campanian farms. Hours old, not days. |
| Neapolitan sfogliatella, babà | Any pasticceria | Guest breakfast pastries that tell a story |

### Capri

**Provision in Naples, ferry across.** Capri provisioning is extremely limited and extremely expensive. Do not plan to source guest-level product on the island.

---

## Genoa / La Spezia / Viareggio

**Port type: Strong Italian Riviera corridor.** Less glamorous than the French side, significantly better for provisioning.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Metro** (Genoa or Viareggio) | Italian Metro card |
| **Fish** | — | **Mercato Orientale** (Genoa) / **La Spezia fish market** | La Spezia: local anchovies and squid are standouts |
| **Meat** | — | **Mercato Orientale** (Genoa) | — |
| **Produce** | — | **Mercato Orientale** (Genoa) | Ligurian basil, pine nuts, aged Parmigiano — pesto ingredients at source |
| **Agent** | — | — | Self-source. Infrastructure is good. |

### The Pesto Move

> **Smart tip:** If your guests want pesto, make it in Genoa with local product. Ligurian basil, local pine nuts, aged Parmigiano from the market. It will be noticeably superior to anything made elsewhere. Buy extra basil and pine nuts — they do not last, but the memory of this pesto on the guest's plate does.

**La Spezia:** Gateway to Cinque Terre. Small fish market, excellent quality — local anchovies and squid. Limited meat; source in Genoa or order ahead.

**Viareggio:** Metro accessible. Moderate options. For guest-level, use Genoa or Tuscan specialty suppliers who deliver.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Ligurian basil, pine nuts | Mercato Orientale, Genoa | Pesto ingredients at origin. Noticeably better than anywhere else. |
| Fresh anchovies | La Spezia fish market | Local catch, exceptional quality |
| Focaccia di Recco | Bakeries near Genoa | Thin, cheese-filled focaccia. Guest-worthy, deeply local. |

---

## La Ciotat

**Port type: Shipyard town, not a provisioning hub.** Growing as a refit and berthing base. Provisioning infrastructure behind Antibes and Palma.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | Order from Antibes-based suppliers for delivery | **Carrefour** / **Intermarché** (local) | For guest-level, source from Marseille (30 min drive) or Antibes delivery |
| **Fish** | — | **Old port fish market** (before 07:00) | Small but genuine. Local fishermen sell direct. |
| **Meat** | Order from Antibes/Marseille suppliers | — | Nothing guest-level locally |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Metro Marseille** (30 min drive) | French Metro card required |
| **Agent** | Antibes-based agents deliver here | — | Use the Cote d'Azur provisioning ecosystem from 30 min away |

> **Smart tip:** La Ciotat is for refit, not provisioning. The fish market is worth the early morning visit — small, genuine, local fishermen selling direct. For everything else, source from the Antibes/Marseille corridor.

---

## Antibes / Cannes / Nice

**Port type: The centre of the yachting world.** The most developed provisioning ecosystem in the Mediterranean. Every chandler, every supplier, every specialty house has a presence or delivers here.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Gourmet Deliveries** (full catalogue) | **Metro Nice** | Gourmet Deliveries: same operation as Palma, strong Cote d'Azur presence |
| **Fish** | **Gourmet Deliveries** (48h notice) | **Marche Forville** (Cannes) / **Antibes fish market** (Port Vauban) | Forville: THE working chef's market on the Riviera. Tue-Sun, arrive early. |
| **Meat** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Marche Forville** meat counters | — |
| **Bread** | — | **Marche Forville** area bakeries / artisan bakeries in old Antibes | — |
| **Produce** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Marche Forville** (Cannes) / **Cours Saleya** (Nice) | Forville for range. Cours Saleya for Niçoise specialties. |
| **Dry Store** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Metro Nice** | French Metro card required. More limited international range than you expect. |
| **Frozen** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | **Metro Nice** | — |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Nice old town** (various small ethnic groceries) | Scattered but findable. Ask locally. |
| **Wine & Spirits** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | Wine merchants in old Antibes / Cannes | — |
| **Agent** | **Gourmet Deliveries** | — | Full charter provision. Reliable. The default choice on the Riviera. |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Marche Forville** (Cannes) — The working chef's market on the Riviera. Open Tuesday to Sunday. Arrive early for the best fish. Produce quality consistently high.
- **Antibes fish market** (Port Vauban area) — Smaller, excellent local catch. For volume or specific species, order through Gourmet Deliveries with 48h notice.

> **Smart tip:** Forville is where you build relationships. The fish vendors who see you every week give you first pick, better prices, and a phone call when something exceptional comes in. Invest the time early in the season.

#### Produce & Fruit

**Go in person:**
- **Marche Forville** (Cannes) — Guest-level fruit, vegetables, herbs. Consistently high quality.
- **Cours Saleya** (Nice) — Stronger on Niçoise specialties: socca ingredients, mesclun, fresh herbs, small-batch olive oils.

#### Cheese & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Fromageries in old Antibes and Cannes** — Exceptional. Epoisses, Banon, aged Comté. Better selection here than almost anywhere in the Med. Buy the day before service for optimal condition.

> **Smart tip:** For guest-level cheese courses, skip the supermarket. The fromageries in old Antibes know how to age and store cheese properly. Tell them when you are serving — they will select at the right ripeness. This is the kind of supplier relationship that makes a cheese course memorable.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-3** | Gourmet Deliveries order placed online | Online |
| **D-2** | Metro Nice run (dry store, frozen, bulk crew). Ethnic/specialty runs. | In person |
| **D-1** | Marche Forville (fish 07:00, produce, bread). Fromageries for cheese. Gourmet Deliveries delivery. | In person + delivery |
| **D-0** | Ultra-perishables, herbs, flowers for table | Forville or nearest |

### Price Reality

| Item | Via Chandler | Self-Sourced | Saving |
|------|-------------|--------------|--------|
| Whole sea bass /kg | EUR 38 | EUR 24 @ Forville | ~37% |
| Aged Comté /kg | EUR 45 | EUR 30 @ fromagerie | ~33% |
| Olive oil 5L | EUR 42 | EUR 26 @ Metro | ~38% |

**Bottom line:** The Riviera self-sourcing ecosystem is mature. Market + Metro + one delivery supplier covers everything. Chandler markup of 15-30% is pure cost that buys you convenience, not quality.

### Knife Maintenance

There is a knife sharpening shop in Antibes mentioned repeatedly by chefs. If you are on a Riviera season, establish a relationship. Professional edge maintenance extends the life of your steel by years.

---

## Monaco

**Port type: Expensive top-up only.** The most expensive square kilometre in the Med.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | Order from Nice/Antibes for delivery | **Carrefour Monaco** (emergency only) | Everything costs more. Provision in Nice (30 min) and transport in. |
| **Fish** | From Antibes/Nice suppliers | — | — |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Monaco wine merchants** | World-class wine merchants. Pétrus, Krug, verticals. The one thing Monaco does better. |
| **Agent** | Antibes-based agents | — | Coordinate with Gourmet Deliveries or your Riviera supplier for Monaco delivery |

### The Reality

No Metro. No markets worth visiting. Supermarkets at Monaco prices. For anything beyond emergency top-ups, provision in Nice or Antibes and transport in. The 30-minute drive saves 20-40% on a full provision.

> **Smart tip:** Monaco is where you buy wine for the guest who requests a specific vintage, and nothing else. For food, go to Nice.

### Logistics

Yacht berths at Port Hercule are tight. Delivery access is restricted and time-limited. Coordinate with the port authority and your agent. Loading provisions at certain berths requires advance scheduling.

---

## Athens

**Port type: Major hub for Greece.** Provision heavily here. The further east you go, the thinner options become.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Kostas / Food Galore** (ships to all Greek islands) | **Metro Athens** | Order from Kostas before departure — product waiting at your next port |
| **Fish** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | **Athens Central Market / Varvakios** (fish hall) | Market Mon-Sat before 09:00. Exceptional fish hall. |
| **Meat** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | **Varvakios** (meat section) | Strong meat section. Prices are a fraction of island prices. |
| **Bread** | — | **Varvakios area bakeries** | — |
| **Produce** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | **Varvakios** | Buy everything you can store. Island prices are 50-100% higher. |
| **Dry Store** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | **Metro Athens** | Greek Metro card (straightforward process). Load up. |
| **Frozen** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | **Metro Athens** | Fill the freezer. Islands have limited frozen range. |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Athens central district** shops | Greek cities have Middle Eastern, South Asian groceries |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | Local wine shops / **Metro** | Greek wines are excellent value. Assyrtiko, Xinomavro, Moschofilero at source prices. |
| **Agent** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | — | Functions as supplier + agent. Single relationship solves Greek provisioning. |

### Category Detail

#### The Key Relationship

**Kostas / Food Galore** — The most recommended supplier for Greece across both WhatsApp groups. Ships from Athens to all Greek islands. Place your order before departure and product is waiting at your next port. This single relationship solves most of your Greek provisioning challenges.

> **Smart tip:** Kostas operates as both supplier and logistics coordinator. Order your Athens provision for pickup, then order your island provisions for delivery at each subsequent port. One contact, entire itinerary covered. This is the smartest move in Greek provisioning.

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Athens Central Market / Varvakios** (fish hall) — Open Monday to Saturday, arrive before 09:00. Exceptional. Prices are a fraction of island prices.

> **Smart tip:** Buy everything you can store in Athens. The same sea bass that costs EUR 20/kg at Varvakios costs EUR 35-40/kg on Mykonos. The same tomatoes, the same feta, the same olive oil — everything is 50-100% more expensive on the islands.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-3** | Kostas order placed (Athens pickup + island deliveries scheduled) | Online/phone |
| **D-2** | Metro Athens run (dry store, frozen, bulk). | In person |
| **D-1** | Varvakios Market (fish 07:00, meat, produce). Kostas delivery. | In person + delivery |
| **D-0** | Bread, ultra-perishables | Nearest bakery |

### Price Reality

| Item | In Athens | On Mykonos | Saving |
|------|----------|------------|--------|
| Sea bass /kg | EUR 20 | EUR 38 | ~47% |
| Feta DOP /kg | EUR 8 | EUR 15 | ~47% |
| Olive oil 5L | EUR 25 | EUR 45 | ~44% |

**Bottom line:** Athens is the Mediterranean's most extreme hub-vs-island price gap. Everything you buy here instead of on the islands saves 40-50%.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Greek olive oil (Kalamata, Cretan) | Varvakios area / specialty shops | World-class at EUR 8-12/L. Costs EUR 25+ exported. Buy 10-20L. |
| Greek honey (thyme, pine) | Varvakios | Exceptional quality. Guest breakfast staple. |
| Assyrtiko wine | Any wine shop | Volcanic Santorini white. Impressive for guests, EUR 8-12 at source. |

---

## Mykonos

**Port type: Consumption island, not production island.** Beautiful, expensive, sparse.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Kostas / Food Galore** (order from Athens) | Small local shops | Do not plan to provision a charter from Mykonos shops |
| **Fish** | — | **Port fishermen** (morning, direct from boats) | Small boats sell direct. Good for day-of sashimi-grade if you inspect. |
| **Produce** | — | Local shops (tomatoes, cucumbers, herbs in season Jun-Sep) | Decent in season. Everything else is imported. |
| **Agent** | **Kostas / Food Galore** | — | Order from Athens, delivered to the marina. This is the move. |

> **Smart tip:** Plan backwards from Athens. Order through Kostas with Mykonos delivery. Use the port fishermen for day-of catch and local shops for seasonal produce. Everything else should already be on board or incoming from Athens.

---

## Montenegro

**Port type: Difficult.** "All pretty terrible." — consensus across both chef groups.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Voli Horeca** (wholesale chain) | Carries Asian goods — notable because Asian ingredients are scarce in the eastern Med |
| **Fish** | — | **Montefish** | Best option. Serviceable. Do not expect Italian or Greek standards. |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Voli Horeca** (Asian aisle) | Soy sauce, rice vinegar, nori — stock up if heading further east |
| **Agent** | — | — | No reliable yacht agent |

### The Better Option

> **Smart tip:** Provision in Dubrovnik (Croatia, 2-3 hours depending on border crossing). Croatian coast has better food infrastructure, better markets, better seafood. Cross the border for your main provision, use Montenegro for emergency top-ups only.

**Border note:** Montenegro is not in the EU. Croatia is. Customs regulations apply when transporting provisions across the border. Carry invoices and be prepared to declare goods.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Njeguši prosciutto | Njeguši village or Cetinje market | Montenegrin smoked prosciutto. Distinctive, guest-worthy, genuinely local. |
| Vranac wine | Any wine shop | Montenegrin red. Underrated and cheap. |

---

## Istanbul

**Port type: Exceptional sourcing city.** Rivals Naples for quality and range, at significantly lower prices.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Macro Center** (upscale supermarket chain) | Carries imported and local goods. |
| **Fish** | — | **Kadıköy fish market** (Asian side) / **Karaköy fish market** | Bosphorus fish: turbot, sea bass, bluefish. Arrive early. |
| **Meat** | — | **Kadıköy market** | Good quality local lamb and beef |
| **Bread** | — | Any bakery | Istanbul bakeries are exceptional. Simit, pide, village bread. |
| **Produce** | — | **Kadıköy market** | Excellent local produce. Pomegranates, figs, peppers, tomatoes in season. |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Spice Bazaar** (Mısır Çarşısı) / **Kadıköy** | Spices in bulk at a fraction of European prices |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Kadıköy** / **Spice Bazaar** | This IS the ethnic market. Sumac, Urfa pepper, pomegranate molasses, za'atar at source. |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | Specialist wine shops (Turkish wine improving rapidly) | — |
| **Agent** | — | Coordinate with your marina agent | Marina infrastructure varies. Agent for delivery logistics only. |

### Category Detail

#### Spices & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Spice Bazaar** (Mısır Çarşısı) — Bulk spices at a fraction of European prices. Sumac, Urfa pepper, pul biber, fresh kaymak, sujuk, pastırma, pomegranate molasses. Quality is superior to exported versions.

> **Smart tip:** Buy spices in bulk — 6-12 months supply. EUR 3-5/kg here for spices that cost EUR 25-40/kg in France. Vacuum-seal and store. This single shopping trip pays for itself many times over.

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Kadıköy fish market** (Asian side) — Bosphorus fish: turbot, sea bass, bluefish. Arrive early.
- **Black Sea anchovies (hamsi)** in season (November-February) are extraordinary.

> **Smart tip:** Turkish olive oil from Ayvalık or the Aegean coast is world-class and costs EUR 8-12 per litre for oil that would sell for EUR 30+ in France. Buy 10-20L and store.

### Logistics

Marina infrastructure varies. Coordinate with your agent for delivery access. Traffic in Istanbul can add 90 minutes to a provisioning run. Plan for it.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Spices (sumac, Urfa pepper, pul biber) | Spice Bazaar / Kadıköy | EUR 3-5/kg vs EUR 25-40/kg in France. Buy 6 months supply. |
| Turkish olive oil (Ayvalık) | Kadıköy specialty shops | World-class, EUR 8-12/L. Costs EUR 30+ exported. |
| Fresh kaymak, sujuk, pastırma | Kadıköy market | Turkish breakfast ingredients at source. Guest-worthy. |
| Pomegranate molasses | Spice Bazaar | Artisan quality. A pantry essential. |
| Black Sea anchovies (Nov-Feb) | Kadıköy / Karaköy fish market | Seasonal, extraordinary. |

---

## Supplier Red Flags

These have been flagged repeatedly across the yacht chef community. Do your own due diligence, but consider yourself warned.

**BFG Provisions** — Multiple reports of outstanding debts to yacht chefs and boats. Proceed with extreme caution.

**Yachting Spirit** — Reported 20% markup applied to the total bill (not per-item, on the invoice total). This is on top of already-marked-up per-item pricing. Effective markup can exceed 35-40%. Verify invoices line by line.

**Gibraltar / Morrison's** — Supermarket manager reported as actively hostile to yacht provisioning. If you need to provision in Gibraltar, use Eroski or Mercadona across the border in La Línea (Spain) instead.

**Delicioso (Palma)** — Mercadona-grade products at premium prices. Not worth the markup.

---

## Cross-Reference: What to Buy Where

| Category | Best Source | Why |
|----------|-----------|-----|
| Guest-level proteins | **Palma** (Simon Jones) / **Naples** (Io Sono La Chianina) | Best specialist suppliers in the Med |
| Spices in bulk | **Istanbul** (Spice Bazaar) | EUR 3-5/kg vs EUR 25-40/kg in France |
| Olive oil | **Istanbul** (Ayvalık) or **Athens** (Kalamata/Cretan) | World-class at EUR 8-12/L |
| Italian staples (pasta, tomatoes, mozzarella) | **Naples** | At source, fraction of French prices |
| Iberian cured meats | **Barcelona** | 30-50% cheaper at source |
| Cheese for guest courses | **Antibes/Cannes** fromageries | Best selection and aging in the Med |
| Sardinian specialties (bottarga, pecorino) | **Cagliari** (Mercato di San Benedetto) | At source |
| Greek staples (oil, feta, honey, wine) | **Athens** (Varvakios) | 40-50% cheaper than islands |
| Bread flour (12%+ protein) | **Palma** (Caputo at Metro/specialty) | Scarce elsewhere in western Med |
| Full charter provision (one contact) | **Gourmet Deliveries** (Palma/Cote d'Azur) or **Kostas** (Greece) | Proven, reliable, yacht-aware |

---

## The Bottom Line

The Med rewards preparation. The chef who spends two hours before arrival placing advance orders and coordinating Metro access saves the boat thousands per charter — and gets better product.

Build relationships. The suppliers listed here are real people running real businesses. The ones who get repeat yacht business offer better pricing, better communication, and better problem-solving when your charter brief changes 36 hours before embarkation.

Prices, contacts, and quality shift season to season. Verify before you commit. But the fundamental map — where to buy what, where to stock up, where to avoid — changes slowly. Use this as your starting grid and update it as you go.

**Source:** Aggregated from recommendations across the Chefs Helping Chefs (~1,025 members) and Yacht Chef Group (~300+ members) WhatsApp communities, July 2025 - January 2026. Supplier poll data cited where available.
