In the Med, you drive between major food cities with deep agricultural hinterlands and wholesale infrastructure. In the Caribbean, you hop between islands where almost…
drafts.body_md:
# The Provisioning Bible: Caribbean & US East Coast

*Port-by-port. What you can get, what you cannot, and where to load up before it is too late.*

Caribbean provisioning is not Mediterranean provisioning. In the Med, you drive between major food cities with deep agricultural hinterlands and wholesale infrastructure. In the Caribbean, you hop between islands where almost everything is imported, cold chains are unreliable, and a 40% import tax can turn a EUR 12,000 provision into EUR 17,000 before you dock.

Every supplier listed here has been recommended by working yacht chefs provisioning during the 2024-2025 and 2025-2026 seasons. No googled suggestions, no tourism board recommendations.

### The Cardinal Rule

**Provision forward.** Whatever you can source and store at your current port, buy it — because the next island may not have it. In the Caribbean, "the next stop" might not have bread flour, decent butter, or fresh herbs at all.

---

## Miami / Fort Lauderdale

**Port type: Major hub.** The best provisioning city in the western hemisphere for yacht chefs. Zero import tax, massive wholesale infrastructure, every cuisine represented. If your season starts here or you transit through, load everything you can carry.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | Instacart (Whole Foods, Costco) | **Costco** (Davie or N Miami) | Costco Business Center in Doral has restaurant-grade bulk |
| **Fish** | — | **Captain Jim's Fish Market** (N Miami) | Arrive before 08:00. Stone crab in season (Oct-May) is exceptional |
| **Meat** | **Wild Fork Foods** (online + stores) | **Restaurant Depot** (membership) | Wild Fork delivers — order 48h ahead for full selection |
| **Bread** | Zak the Baker (online ordering) | **Zak the Baker** (Wynwood) | Sourdough and challah. Order 48h ahead for volume. |
| **Produce** | — | **Robert Is Here** (Homestead, tropical) / **Restaurant Depot** | Robert Is Here for tropical exotics you cannot find elsewhere in the US |
| **Dry Store** | Amazon Fresh / Instacart | **Costco Business Center** (Doral) | Order D-5. Arrives next day. |
| **Frozen** | Wild Fork Foods | **Restaurant Depot** | Restaurant Depot freezer section is vast and well-priced |
| **Ethnic** | — | **168 Market** (Hialeah, Asian) / **Sedano's** (Latin) | 168 Market has the best Asian selection in South Florida |
| **Wine & Spirits** | Total Wine (online order) | **Total Wine** (multiple locations) | Duty-free spirits at the port if heading international |
| **Agent** | **Yacht Provisions USA** | — | 15-18% markup. Worth it only for full-boat loads when time is critical |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Captain Jim's Fish Market** (N Miami Beach) — Local catch, stone crab, Florida lobster. The fishmongers know yacht chefs. Arrive before 08:00 for the best selection.

> **Smart tip:** Miami fish markets sell by the boat — if you see a whole yellowtail or cobia that looks good, buy the whole fish. Price per kg drops significantly and you control the butchery.

#### Meat & Protein

**Order online:**
- **Wild Fork Foods** — Online ordering with home/marina delivery. Broad range from crew-grade to Wagyu. 48h lead time for full catalogue. Multiple retail stores across Miami-Dade if you prefer to inspect.

**Go in person:**
- **Restaurant Depot** — Wholesale membership required (boat registration and business card usually accepted). Guest and crew proteins, dairy, dry goods. One stop for 60% of your provision.

> **Smart tip:** Get the Restaurant Depot membership on your first visit. Bring boat registration, a business card, and the captain's ID. Card is free. This single membership covers every Restaurant Depot in the US.

#### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Instacart** — Pulls from Costco, Whole Foods, Publix. Schedule delivery to the marina or a nearby address. Useful for the general grocery run when you do not want to leave the boat.

**Go in person:**
- **Costco Business Center** (Doral) — Not the regular Costco. The Business Center carries restaurant-format packaging: 5kg flour bags, case lots, portion-controlled proteins, bulk dairy. This is where you do the heavy lift.

> **Smart tip:** Regular Costco sells consumer packs. The Business Center in Doral sells professional packs. The difference in value and format is significant. Make the extra 20-minute drive.

#### Ethnic & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **168 Market** (Hialeah) — Full Asian supermarket. Lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, rice noodles, miso paste, dashi, nori, Thai curry pastes, wonton wrappers. The only reliable large-scale Asian source in South Florida.
- **Sedano's** — Cuban/Latin groceries. Plantains, yuca, black beans, mojo, sazón, achiote. Multiple locations.

**Buy here:** Asian pantry staples in bulk. These are scarce or absent once you leave the US mainland.
**Skip:** Sushi-grade fish at Asian markets. Go to Captain Jim's.

#### Bread & Bakery

**Order online:**
- **Zak the Baker** (Wynwood) — Artisan sourdough, challah, focaccia. Order 48h ahead for volume. Delivers.

> **Smart tip:** Stock up on Caputo flour and bread flour at Restaurant Depot. Once you hit the Caribbean, 12%+ protein flour is hard to find and expensive.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-4** | Dry store, cleaning, bulk frozen, flour | Costco Business Center + Amazon Fresh delivery |
| **D-3** | Meat, dairy, cheese, frozen proteins | Restaurant Depot + Wild Fork |
| **D-2** | Ethnic pantry, wine, specialty items | 168 Market + Total Wine + specialty runs |
| **D-1** | Fish, produce, bread | Captain Jim's (07:00) + produce market + Zak the Baker |
| **D-0** | Ultra-perishables, herbs, last-minute | Whole Foods or nearest Publix |

> **Smart tip:** Place your Instacart/Amazon Fresh orders on D-5. Do the Costco Business Center run D-4 morning. Restaurant Depot D-3. This leaves D-1 free for the market run — which is the only day you actually need to be out early.

### Price Reality

| Item | Via Agent | Self-Sourced | Saving |
|------|----------|--------------|--------|
| Wagyu striploin /kg | USD 120 | USD 88 @ Wild Fork | ~27% |
| King crab legs /kg | USD 95 | USD 65 @ Restaurant Depot | ~32% |
| Organic mixed greens /case | USD 45 | USD 28 @ Costco | ~38% |

**Bottom line:** Miami is where self-sourcing saves the most. No import tax, wholesale access everywhere, delivery infrastructure that works. Use an agent here only if you genuinely have zero time.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Stone crab claws (Oct-May) | Captain Jim's | Peak season, direct from boats, half the restaurant price |
| Tropical exotics (mamey, sapodilla, jackfruit) | Robert Is Here, Homestead | Unique tropical fruits grown in South Florida, unavailable elsewhere |
| Cuban bread and pastries | Any Sedano's or Latin bakery | Authentic, cheap, crew will thank you |

### If You Have No Time — The Agent

**Yacht Provisions USA** — Full-service provisioning. They source from the same wholesale suppliers and deliver to the marina. Markup 15-18%. On a USD 15,000 provision, that is USD 2,250-2,700. Worth it only when your charter starts in 24 hours and you have not left the boat.

## San Juan, Puerto Rico

**Port type: Strong hub.** US territory — no customs, no import duty, US supply chains. Better provisioned than any Caribbean island. The Sam's Club and Costco alone make it a viable loading port for the eastern Caribbean.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | Instacart (Costco, Walmart, Econo) | **Costco** (Caguas or Carolina) | Same Costco membership as mainland US |
| **Fish** | — | **La Placita de Santurce** fish vendors / **Pescadería del Atlántico** | Arrive before 08:00. Local red snapper, mahi, lobster. |
| **Meat** | — | **Costco** / **Sam's Club** / **Restaurant Depot** | Restaurant Depot in Carolina has professional cuts |
| **Bread** | Panaderías deliver — call ahead | **Panadería España** (Hato Rey) | Puerto Rican pan sobao and pan de agua are excellent crew bread |
| **Produce** | — | **Plaza del Mercado de Río Piedras** | Best local produce: plantains, avocados, recao, ají dulce, tropical fruit |
| **Dry Store** | Amazon (US shipping, no duty) | **Costco** / **Sam's Club** | Amazon delivers with standard US Prime shipping |
| **Frozen** | — | **Costco** / **Sam's Club** | Load freezer here — frozen goods are 30-50% cheaper than island prices |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Pitusa** (multiple, Dominican/Caribbean) / Asian markets in Santurce | Pitusa for Caribbean staples; limited Asian compared to Miami |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Costco** (spirits) / **Bodegas Compostela** (wine) | Puerto Rico has lower spirits tax than mainland US |
| **Agent** | — | Limited options — self-source is the move here | PR is small enough that self-sourcing is fast |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **La Placita de Santurce** — The local market district. Fish vendors sell local catch: red snapper, mahi-mahi, Caribbean lobster. Quality is good when product is fresh. Inspect — cold chain varies by vendor.
- **Pescadería del Atlántico** — More consistent than market vendors. Carries local and imported seafood.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rican waters produce excellent local fish. Red snapper here is genuinely local, not frozen-and-thawed imports. Ask for "chillo entero" (whole red snapper) — cheaper per kg than fillets and you control the portioning.

#### Meat & Protein

**Go in person:**
- **Costco / Sam's Club** — US-grade proteins at US prices. USDA Prime and Choice available. This is where you load the freezer for the Caribbean leg.
- **Restaurant Depot** (Carolina) — Professional cuts, bulk pricing, same membership as mainland.

> **Smart tip:** Buy 2-3 weeks of frozen proteins here. Vacuum-seal and blast-freeze everything. Once you leave Puerto Rico heading east, meat quality and availability drop significantly and prices rise 30-50%.

#### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Amazon Prime** delivers to Puerto Rico at standard US rates, no duty. Order dry goods and specialty items D-5 to D-4.

**Go in person:**
- **Costco** (Carolina or Caguas) — Same card, same prices, same product as mainland US.

> **Smart tip:** Puerto Rico is the last place before the eastern Caribbean where you have full US retail infrastructure with no import tax. Treat it as your Miami alternative if your route goes PR → USVI → BVI → eastern Caribbean.

#### Produce & Fruit

**Go in person:**
- **Plaza del Mercado de Río Piedras** — Local produce at local prices. Plantains (green and ripe), avocados, recao (culantro — not cilantro), ají dulce peppers, breadfruit, yams, tropical fruit. Use what Puerto Rico grows — it is excellent and cheap.

**Buy here:** Recao, ají dulce, local avocados, plantains in bulk, tropical fruit
**Skip:** Imported European produce — pay double what you would in Miami for the same product

#### Dry Store & Pantry

**Order online:**
- **Amazon Prime** — Standard US delivery. No duty. Order non-perishables D-5.

**Go in person:**
- **Costco / Sam's Club** — Bulk dry goods at mainland prices.

> **Smart tip:** Load canned goods, oils, flour, sugar, rice, pasta here. These are the items that cost 40-80% more once you reach islands with import duty. Your storage capacity is the only limit.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-5** | Amazon orders (specialty dry goods) | Online |
| **D-3** | Dry store, frozen, bulk | Costco + Sam's Club |
| **D-2** | Meat, dairy, wine, spirits | Restaurant Depot + Costco + Bodegas Compostela |
| **D-1** | Fish, produce, bread | La Placita (07:00) + Río Piedras market + panadería |
| **D-0** | Last-minute, herbs, ultra-perishables | Pueblo or Econo (nearest supermarket) |

### Price Reality

| Item | In Caribbean (Antigua) | In San Juan | Saving |
|------|----------------------|-------------|--------|
| Butter /kg | USD 18 | USD 9 @ Costco | ~50% |
| USDA Choice striploin /kg | USD 55 | USD 32 @ Costco | ~42% |
| Bread flour 5kg | USD 14 | USD 6 @ Sam's Club | ~57% |

**Bottom line:** San Juan gives you US wholesale pricing without crossing to the mainland. If your route touches Puerto Rico, treat it as a major loading port. The savings over provisioning the same items in the eastern Caribbean are 30-50%.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Recao (culantro) | Río Piedras market | Caribbean herb that adds depth to everything — pennies per bunch here |
| Ají dulce peppers | Río Piedras market | Sweet Caribbean pepper, no heat, extraordinary aroma — unavailable off-island |
| Puerto Rican coffee (Yaucono, Alto Grande) | Any supermarket | Excellent quality, cheap, guests love the story |
| Ron del Barrilito | Specialty liquor shops | Three-star aged rum. Serious sipping rum that impresses guests who think they know Caribbean rum |

## St Maarten / SXM

**Port type: Major hub.** The best-provisioned island in the Caribbean. Duty-free on the Dutch side. This is your loading port — stock up here for the entire first leg of your Caribbean itinerary.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Carrefour** (French side — online ordering) | **Carrefour** (French side) | Online order 48h ahead, collect at service desk — no aisles, no trolley |
| **Fish** | — | **Fish market** (Simpson Bay, past the bridge) | Arrive before 07:00. Local catch only — inspect everything |
| **Meat** | **Alan Guichard** (WhatsApp order) | **Alan Guichard** (butcher) | WhatsApp your order 72h ahead. Guest-level proteins rare in the Caribbean — this is the source |
| **Bread** | — | **Carrefour bakery** (French side) | French-standard bakery. Stock baguettes and freeze for the leg |
| **Produce** | — | **Garden Market** (Dutch side) | Best produce on the island by Caribbean standards |
| **Dry Store** | **Carrefour** online / **Cost U Less** delivery | **Cost U Less** | Bulk warehouse — Costco format. Dry goods, cleaning, crew provisions |
| **Frozen** | — | **Cost U Less** / **Carrefour** | Load freezer here. Next islands have limited frozen range |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Carrefour** (French side, Asian aisle) | Limited but functional Asian selection — lemongrass, soy, coconut milk |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Carrefour** (French side) | Duty-free. European wine at European prices. Buy here. |
| **Agent** | **Epicerie De Marie / Stephan** | — | The consensus: "this guy is the best." Specialty, European imports, delivers to marinas |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Fish market** (Simpson Bay side, just past the bridge) — Local catch, variable daily. Arrive before 07:00. Cold chain discipline varies by vendor — inspect carefully.

> **Smart tip:** Ask what came in that morning. Do not buy anything that has been sitting. If the market is thin, Stephan at Epicerie De Marie can source guest-level seafood with 48h notice.

**Buy here:** Whatever is fresh and local that day
**Skip:** Pre-ordering specific species — this is a local catch market, not a wholesaler

#### Meat & Protein

**Order online (WhatsApp):**
- **Alan Guichard** — Butcher. Quality meat sourcing that is rare anywhere in the Caribbean. Guest-level proteins: dry-aged, specialty cuts. WhatsApp your order 72h ahead.

**Go in person:**
- **Carrefour** (French side) — European-standard meat counter. Decent for crew proteins, chicken, pork. Not guest-level.

> **Smart tip:** Alan Guichard is the Simon Jones of the Caribbean. Build this relationship on arrival. For guest charters in the eastern Caribbean, this is your primary protein source — order here even if you are picking up in a different port.

#### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Carrefour** (French side) — Has an ordering system for bulk and specialty items. Ask at the service desk or order online. Place your order 48h before you need it. They prepare the order — you collect at the desk. Saves 2-3 hours of walking the aisles.

**Go in person:**
- **Carrefour** (French side) — The closest thing to a European supermarket in the Caribbean. European butter, cream, cheese, cured meats, baking supplies, cleaning products. This is where you do the general grocery run.
- **Cost U Less** (Dutch side) — Bulk warehouse format. Dry goods, cleaning supplies, crew provisions, bulk beverages. Not for guest product.

> **Smart tip:** Do Carrefour online order for guest groceries (D-3). Do Cost U Less in person for bulk crew/dry store (D-3 same day — they are 15 minutes apart). Two stops, one morning, 70% of your general provision done.

#### Dry Store & Pantry

**Order online:**
- **Carrefour online** — Bulk flour, sugar, oils, pasta, canned goods. European brands.

**Go in person:**
- **Cost U Less** — Case lots, bulk rice, bulk canned goods. Cheapest per-unit prices on the island.

> **Smart tip:** SXM is duty-free on the Dutch side. This means dry goods here cost 30-50% less than on any island with import tax. Fill every locker. Your storage capacity is the only limit. Whatever you do not buy here, you will pay more for at every subsequent port.

#### Ethnic & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Carrefour** (French side, Asian/ethnic aisle) — Limited but functional: soy sauce, coconut milk, rice noodles, curry pastes, lemongrass (sometimes fresh, usually paste).
- **Chef's Market** — Curated toward professional kitchens. Specialty items, harder-to-find imports.

**Buy here:** Whatever Asian or specialty pantry staples you can find — stock up. This is the last reliable source before you hit islands with nothing.
**Skip:** Fresh Asian herbs (better sourced in Miami or Panama)

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-3** | Carrefour online order placed. WhatsApp Alan Guichard for proteins. | Online |
| **D-2** | Collect Carrefour order. Cost U Less run for bulk dry/crew/frozen. | French side AM, Dutch side PM |
| **D-1** | Fish market (06:30). Garden Market for produce. Epicerie De Marie for specialty. | In person |
| **D-0** | Bread from Carrefour bakery. Last-minute. | French side |

### Price Reality

| Item | Via Chandler | Self-Sourced | Saving |
|------|-------------|--------------|--------|
| European butter /kg | EUR 18 | EUR 10 @ Carrefour | ~44% |
| Chicken breast /kg | EUR 16 | EUR 9 @ Cost U Less | ~44% |
| Olive oil 5L | EUR 45 | EUR 28 @ Carrefour | ~38% |

**Bottom line:** SXM duty-free pricing is the single biggest advantage in Caribbean provisioning. A EUR 10,000 provision self-sourced here saves EUR 3,000-5,000 compared to the same provision on any taxed island.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| French cheese and charcuterie | Carrefour French side | European import prices without Caribbean markup — load the cheese fridge |
| European baking supplies | Carrefour French side | Caputo flour, French butter, European cream — stock for the leg |
| Duty-free spirits | Any Dutch side liquor store | No import tax — buy all spirits and wine for the entire Caribbean itinerary here |

### If You Have No Time — The Agent

**Epicerie De Marie / Stephan** — The most recommended supplier in the Caribbean across both chef groups. Specialty items, European imports, quality control to Med standards. Delivers to marinas. Contact Stephan before arrival, send your provision list, he pre-sources and flags gaps. Not a bulk agent — best for specialty and guest-level items.

**LuxC'mar Yacht Services** — Full-service provisioning. Handles logistics-heavy, multi-vendor orders. More structured, less personal than Epicerie De Marie. Use when you need one company to coordinate the entire provision.

## Antigua

**Port type: Transit stop.** Functional but limited. Do not expect Mediterranean range. If SXM was your previous stop, you should have loaded up.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Epicurean** (St John's) | Best supermarket on the island. Prices are high. |
| **Fish** | — | **Aris Fish** | Quality depends on the day. Inspect. |
| **Meat** | **Hall Valley Farm** (order ahead) | — | Frozen meat supplier. Order 48h ahead for crew-grade bulk |
| **Bread** | — | Epicurean bakery section | Functional, not artisan |
| **Produce** | — | **Epicurean** / local roadside vendors | Local tropical produce from roadside vendors is cheaper and fresher |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Epicurean** | Limited range, high prices |
| **Frozen** | **Hall Valley Farm** | **Epicurean** | Load what you can — options thin out heading south |
| **Ethnic** | — | Not available | Nothing reliable |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **Cork and Basket** | Specialty wines and imported cheeses. Limited but worth checking |
| **Agent** | **Horizons Suppliers** | — | Coordinates across multiple vendors. Useful when you cannot spend a day running around |

### Category Detail

#### Fish & Seafood

**Go in person:**
- **Aris Fish** — Local fish supplier. Quality depends on the day and what came in. When it is good, it is genuinely good local catch. When it is thin, it is thin.

> **Smart tip:** If Aris is empty, ask at the English Harbour docks. Local fishermen sell direct from the boat in the morning. No signage — ask around.

#### Meat & Protein

**Order online:**
- **Hall Valley Farm** — Frozen meat supplier. Bulk crew-grade protein. Order 48h in advance.

> **Smart tip:** For guest-level proteins, order from Alan Guichard in SXM and have Horizons coordinate the shipment. This is the standard workaround.

#### General / One-Stop

**Go in person:**
- **Epicurean** (St John's) — The better supermarket. Carries some imported goods, decent bakery section, basic produce. Prices are high (import duty) but it covers the basics.

> **Smart tip:** Described in the chef groups as "best from a bad bunch." Calibrate expectations. You can feed a boat from here. You cannot provision a high-end charter from scratch.

### The Reality

Antigua is a provisioning compromise. For anything specialist, order through Horizons or have Epicerie De Marie in SXM ship it.

### If You Have No Time — The Agent

**Horizons Suppliers** — Yacht provisioning coordination across multiple local vendors. They know who has what, which saves you a day of driving around an island where nothing is conveniently located.

## St Lucia

**Port type: Surprisingly workable.** Largely because of one supplier.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | Local supermarkets (basic) | Do not rely on supermarkets for guest-level product |
| **Fish** | **Faye Gastronomie / Marcel** | **Castries fish vendors** | Marcel can fly fish in from other islands if local catch is thin |
| **Meat** | **Faye Gastronomie / Marcel** | — | Marcel operates a refrigerated truck and sources from multiple islands |
| **Bread** | — | Local bakeries | Functional. Not European standard. |
| **Produce** | — | **Castries Market** (arrive 05:00) | Local tropical produce is excellent — this is where to lean in |
| **Dry Store** | — | Local supermarkets | Basic range. Stock up in SXM if heading here. |
| **Frozen** | **Faye Gastronomie / Marcel** | — | Marcel can source and deliver |
| **Ethnic** | — | Not reliably available | — |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | Duty-free shops in Castries | Reasonable prices on spirits |
| **Agent** | **Faye Gastronomie / Marcel** | — | Part agent, part supplier. Refrigerated truck, inter-island sourcing. The reason St Lucia works. |

### Category Detail

#### Produce & Fruit

**Go in person:**
- **Castries Market** — Reportedly open almost 24 hours. Arrive at 05:00 for the best selection. This is genuinely local produce — not imported. Mangoes, passionfruit, breadfruit, plantains, dasheen, christophene, soursop.

> **Smart tip:** Lean into what St Lucia grows. The tropical produce at Castries Market is better and cheaper than anything imported. Adapt your menu to the market, not the other way around. Breadfruit roasted over charcoal is guest-worthy if you present it right.

#### The Key Relationship

**Faye Gastronomie Caraibes / Marcel** — Operates a refrigerated truck. Can fly items in from other islands or from Miami. Functions as supplier, agent, and logistics coordinator combined. Without Marcel, St Lucia is local produce and whatever the supermarkets carry. With Marcel, most charter requirements are covered.

> **Smart tip:** Establish contact before arrival. Send your provision list as early as possible. Inter-island freight is not next-day — lead time matters. Marcel is the difference between St Lucia being a provisioning headache and a workable port.

**Port type: Difficult.** "Last year was so so difficult to find fresh products." — direct quote from a working chef, 2024-2025 season.

Provision the bulk of your charter requirements before arriving. Grenada's strengths are narrow but exceptional:

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |

> **Smart tip:** Grenada is a sourcing destination for spices, not a provisioning destination for a charter. Buy 6 months of nutmeg, cinnamon, and turmeric here at source prices. For everything else, you should already have it on board.

## St Barths

**Port type: Luxury top-up.** Quality is higher than most Caribbean islands. Prices are dramatically higher too.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **Chef's Market** | Carries items you cannot find elsewhere in the eastern Caribbean |
| **Fish** | — | Local fishermen (Gustavia harbour, morning) | Small scale, local catch only |
| **Meat** | **Chef's Market** (order ahead) | **Chef's Market** | Wagyu, specialty cuts available. Order 48h ahead. |
| **Produce** | — | **Chef's Market** / small grocery shops | Limited, imported, expensive |
| **Agent** | — | — | No dedicated yacht agent. Use SXM-based agents with ferry delivery. |

### The Cost Reality

A provision that costs EUR 8,000 in SXM costs EUR 12,000-14,000 here. Everything is imported and taxed.

> **Smart tip:** For a charter departing St Barths, provision as much as possible in SXM and ferry it across. SXM to St Barths is a 45-minute ferry. The savings on a full provision will exceed the freight cost several times over.

**Transport warning:** Sinclair Taxi — EUR 5,000 for a 20-minute ride on New Year's Eve. St Barths transport during peak season is extraordinary. Arrange marina-side delivery in advance or walk.

### Local Gems

| Item | Where | Why Here |
|------|-------|----------|
| Last-minute luxury items | Chef's Market | Wagyu, specific cheeses, imported European produce — when the guest wants the best and cost is not the question |

### The Move

**Vareen / Virgin Islands Organic Herbs** and **Chris Driver** grow herbs and vegetables locally. Fresh basil, thyme, rosemary grown on-island — in a region where fresh herbs are otherwise flown in and wilting. Contact before arrival.

> **Smart tip:** The BVI is where charters happen, not where provisions happen. Everything on your boat should already be there when you arrive. Use local growers for fresh herbs and daily produce only.

## Barbuda

**Port type: Nothing.** Provision before arrival. No true grocery stores, no suppliers, no backup options. Everything you need must be on board before you anchor.

## Bahamas / Nassau

**Port type: Functional hub with a tax problem.** Better infrastructure than the eastern Caribbean, but approximately 40% import duty on everything defines the economics.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | **Sysco** (recurring orders) | **Fresh Market** | Sysco for volume recurring. Fresh Market for weekly. |
| **Fish** | — | **Milton Fresh Fish Market** | Local fish, generally good quality. Inspect on arrival. |
| **Meat** | **Sysco** / **BWA** | **Fresh Market** | Sysco and BWA for wholesale pricing. |
| **Dry Store** | **Sysco** / **BWA** | **Fresh Market** | Bulk ordering through Sysco saves vs retail + tax |
| **Frozen** | **Sysco** | — | Institutional but reliable for volume |
| **Agent** | **Dion Provisioning** (Exumas only) | — | Essential in the Exumas where options are near-zero |

### Category Detail

#### General / One-Stop

**Order online:**
- **Sysco** — Yes, Sysco operates in the Bahamas. Institutional format, reliable for volume, recurring order capability. For boats based here, build this relationship.
- **BWA** — Wholesale supplier. Useful for bulk orders at better-than-retail pricing.

**Go in person:**
- **Fresh Market** — Good produce and imported goods. Prices reflect the 40% import duty.

> **Smart tip:** If you are transiting from Florida, provision in Fort Lauderdale or Miami and bring everything on board. The savings on a full charter provision can exceed USD 5,000 vs buying the same items in Nassau with the 40% duty.

### Exumas

**Dion Provisioning** — The go-to and effectively the only option in the Exumas, where grocery infrastructure is near-zero. Contact well in advance — this is not a next-day operation.

> **Smart tip:** Plan Exumas provisioning as if there are no options. Bring everything. Be pleasantly surprised if Dion can fill gaps. Do not plan around Dion being able to source everything on your list.

### Price Reality

| Item | In Nassau (with 40% duty) | Pre-loaded from Miami | Saving |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------|
| USDA Choice beef /kg | USD 65 | USD 35 @ Restaurant Depot | ~46% |
| Imported cheese /kg | USD 40 | USD 22 @ Costco | ~45% |
| Olive oil 5L | USD 55 | USD 30 @ Costco | ~45% |

**Bottom line:** The 40% import duty makes self-sourcing in Nassau expensive regardless. The real savings come from provisioning in the US before crossing.

---

## Turks and Caicos

**Port type: Bare minimum.** "It's a bastard." — direct quote from a chef who provisioned here recently.

No reliable infrastructure for guest-level provisioning. Basic groceries exist. Anything beyond basic does not. Provision in the Bahamas or Florida depending on your route.

---

## Panama

**Port type: Major hub.** Surprises chefs who arrive expecting eastern Caribbean scarcity. Panama City has genuine provisioning depth. Treat it like a mini-Miami.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | **RIBA Smith** | Best supermarket chain in Panama. Good imports, decent produce, reasonable prices |
| **Fish** | — | **Merca Panama** fish section | Wholesale market. Large, well-stocked. |
| **Meat** | — | **Merca Panama** | Wholesale pricing on proteins |
| **Bread** | — | **RIBA Smith** bakery / local panaderías | Decent. Stock up on flour here if heading to remote Pacific anchorages |
| **Produce** | — | **Merca Panama** | Wholesale produce at excellent prices |
| **Dry Store** | — | **Merca Panama** / **RIBA Smith** | Wholesale bulk at Merca, branded at RIBA Smith |
| **Frozen** | — | **Merca Panama** | Good frozen range at wholesale prices |
| **Ethnic** | — | **Fruteria Mini Max** | THE Asian market. Lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, rice noodles, Thai staples. Rare find. |
| **Wine & Spirits** | — | **RIBA Smith** / duty-free at transit ports | — |
| **Agent** | **Megayacht Provisions** | — | Expensive but handles everything. Use only when time is the constraint. |

### Category Detail

#### Ethnic & Specialty

**Go in person:**
- **Fruteria Mini Max** — Full Asian market. Lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, rice noodles, Southeast Asian pantry staples, soy sauces, fish sauce, curry pastes, wonton wrappers. A rare find in Central America and the Caribbean.

> **Smart tip:** This is the best (and possibly only) proper Asian market between Miami and the Pacific coast of South America. Stock your entire Asian pantry here. Buy in bulk — what you do not buy now, you will not find at the next port.

#### General / One-Stop

**Go in person:**
- **RIBA Smith** — Multiple locations in Panama City. Imported goods, decent produce, reasonable prices. The day-to-day supermarket for guest and crew.
- **Merca Panama** — Wholesale market. Large, well-stocked. Produce, proteins, dairy in bulk. Worth a full morning to do the heavy lift here.

> **Smart tip:** RIBA Smith for the curated selection (guest-specific items, brands you recognise). Merca Panama for the volume and price. Do both on the same day — they cover different needs.

### Provision Schedule

| When | What | Where |
|------|------|-------|
| **D-3** | Dry store, bulk frozen, cleaning | Merca Panama |
| **D-2** | Asian pantry, specialty items, wine | Fruteria Mini Max + RIBA Smith |
| **D-1** | Meat, fish, produce, bread | Merca Panama (AM) + RIBA Smith (PM) |
| **D-0** | Ultra-perishables | RIBA Smith or nearest |

### If You Have No Time — The Agent

**Megayacht Provisions** — Full-service for yachts transiting the Canal or cruising the Pacific side. Expensive. Panama City has excellent self-sourcing, so the agent is only worth the markup when time is genuinely critical (Canal transit scheduling, tight turnaround).

---

## Costa Rica

**Port type: Improving, requires planning.** Strong local produce, growing yacht infrastructure.

### Quick Map

| Category | Order Online | Go In Person | Smart Tip |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **General** | — | Local supermarkets (Auto Mercado is the best chain) | Auto Mercado carries some imported goods |
| **Fish** | — | **Golfito fish shops** (Pacific coast) | Mahi-mahi, yellowfin tuna, snapper. Inspect — freshness depends on the day |
| **Meat** | **Prime Provisions** (order ahead) | Local butchers | Prime Provisions: "fresh from the mountain" — good local product |
| **Produce** | — | **Local markets / ferias del agricultor** | Costa Rican produce is exceptional. Coffee, chocolate, tropical fruit, Central Valley vegetables |
| **Agent** | **Sea Masters** | — | Handles Pacific coast marina logistics |

### The Local Advantage

Costa Rica grows exceptional produce. Coffee, chocolate, tropical fruits, and vegetables from the Central Valley are outstanding. Lean into local product — it is better and cheaper than importing alternatives.

> **Smart tip:** Buy Costa Rican coffee at source (Tarrazú, Central Valley). Quality matches or exceeds what you pay EUR 30/kg for in Europe, at a third of the price. Buy 5-10kg and vacuum-seal. Guests love the story and the quality.

### If You Have No Time — The Agent

**Sea Masters** — Yacht provisioning logistics for the Pacific coast. Use them for coordination. Supplement with **Prime Provisions** for fresh local product.

---

## Cross-Reference: What to Buy Where

This is the critical intelligence. Provision forward based on what each port does and does not have.

| Category | Best Source | Why |
|----------|-----------|-----|
| Everything (full load) | **Miami** or **San Juan** | US wholesale, zero Caribbean import tax, every cuisine represented |
| European dairy, butter, cream, cheese | **SXM** (Carrefour French side) | Duty-free, European supply chain, European prices |
| Guest-level proteins | **Miami** (Wild Fork, Restaurant Depot) or **SXM** (Alan Guichard) | Best selection and cold chain |
| Specialty/imported items | **SXM** (Epicerie De Marie) or **Miami** | Nowhere else carries them reliably |
| Tropical produce | **St Lucia** (Castries Market) or **Costa Rica** | Locally grown, peak quality, best prices |
| Spices | **Grenada** (direct from source) | World-class at origin prices |
| Asian pantry staples | **Miami** (168 Market) or **Panama** (Fruteria Mini Max) | Scarce everywhere else |
| Bulk dry goods | **Miami** (Costco Business Center) or **SXM** (Cost U Less) or **Panama** (Merca) | Wholesale pricing |
| Fish & seafood | **Local at each island**, inspected daily | Freshness varies — never pre-order blindly |
| Frozen backup | **Miami** or **San Juan** (Costco, Restaurant Depot) | Load freezer at US wholesale before entering the Caribbean |

---

## The Bottom Line

Caribbean provisioning demands a fundamentally different mindset than the Med. You do not resupply at every port. You plan three ports ahead and carry redundancy.

The chefs who thrive here do four things: load heavily at hub ports (Miami, San Juan, SXM, Panama), build relationships with the two or three key suppliers at each island, adapt menus to what is locally excellent rather than fighting to source European standards in a tropical environment, and carry enough dry stores and frozen backup to ride out a provisioning gap.

The Caribbean will never provision like the Cote d'Azur. But with the right loading strategy, the right contacts, and realistic expectations, you can feed guests at the same standard. You just have to think further ahead.

**Source:** Aggregated from recommendations across the Chefs Helping Chefs (~1,025 members) and Yacht Chef Group (~300+ members) WhatsApp communities, July 2025 - January 2026. US supplier recommendations from working yacht chefs based in Fort Lauderdale and Miami. Import tax figures are approximate and subject to change.

This connects to a bartender's technique that belongs in every galley (oleo-saccharum: sugar extracting essential oils from citrus peel), new r…
drafts.body_md:
# Weekly Brief — Week of February 12, 2026

## THE THREAD

Blood oranges peak this week in eastern Sicily. The anthocyanins — pigments triggered by cold nights near Etna — are at maximum concentration in Tarocco, Moro, and Sanguinello. This connects to a bartender's technique that belongs in every galley (oleo-saccharum: sugar extracting essential oils from citrus peel), new research on why citrus flavonoids promise more than they deliver in the body, and a salad that traces the Arab introduction of citrus to Sicily in the 9th century. One principle ties the thread: heat destroys what makes these fruits valuable. The anthocyanins, the vitamin C, the volatile terpenes — all degrade rapidly above 70°C. The best citrus cooking is no cooking at all.

---

## SHORE LARDER — Blood Oranges

*Arancia Rossa di Sicilia PGI · Eastern Sicily · Peak: January–March*

**Three varieties. One volcano.** Tarocco (the sweetest, highest vitamin C of any orange worldwide), Moro (deepest red, raspberry note, most bitter), and Sanguinello (latest ripening, begins now, harvested through April). All three hold PGI protection across 32 municipalities in the provinces of Syracuse, Catania, and Enna.

The red comes from anthocyanins — specifically cyanidin 3-glucoside and cyanidin 3-(6"-malonylglucoside), which together constitute over 90% of total anthocyanin content. These pigments require nighttime temperatures of 7–10°C to develop. No cold nights, no red flesh. This is why blood oranges don't work in Florida.

| Compound | What It Does | Chef Note |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Anthocyanins (cyanidin 3-glucoside) | Red-purple pigment, antioxidant | Half-life at 90°C: under 30 minutes. Never cook blood orange juice. |
| D-limonene (peel) | 85–95% of essential oil; bright citrus aroma | Extracted by oleo-saccharum (see Method) |
| Hesperidin (flavonoid) | Anti-inflammatory, but poor bioavailability | Present in pith — ironic, since we discard it |
| Vitamin C | Highest of any orange variety (Tarocco) | Boiling 3 minutes destroys ~13%. Serve raw. |

**One pairing worth trying:** Blood orange + fennel + anchovy. The anthocyanin sweetness bridges the anise and the brine. This is the Sicilian insalata di arance (see Heritage).

**Sourcing:** Catania and Syracuse markets, direct. Palagonia holds a Blood Orange Festival in late February. When transiting the Amalfi Coast, Sfusato Amalfitano lemons start their season now (February–September). Bergamot from Calabria's Ionian coast is also peaking.

```
season:winter region:mediterranean type:citrus thesaurus:blood-orange
```

---

## THE METHOD — Oleo-Saccharum

**The Move:** Use granulated sugar to extract essential oils directly from citrus peel. No heat, no equipment, no waste. The result is a concentrated aromatic syrup — pure citrus flavour that you can stir into sorbets, granitas, vinaigrettes, cured fish, cocktails, or pastry glazes.

1. Peel citrus with a Y-peeler — flavedo only (the coloured outer layer). Avoid the white pith; it's bitter.
2. Place peels in a non-reactive bowl. Add sugar: approximately 60 g per lemon, or roughly equal parts peel and sugar by weight.
3. Toss to coat. Cover. Rest at room temperature — 4–6 hours minimum, 12–24 hours for full extraction.
4. The sugar draws moisture from peel cells, collapsing oil glands in the flavedo and releasing terpenes (D-limonene, linalool, citral) into the forming syrup.
5. Strain out spent peels. Refrigerate. Keeps one month; best within the first week.

**Accelerated galley method:** Vacuum-seal peels and sugar, then immerse at 54°C for 45 minutes. Twice the speed of ambient extraction.

| Variable | What Happens | Adjust By |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Pith included | Bitter, cloudy syrup | Peel only the coloured layer |
| Too little sugar | Incomplete extraction, peels dry out | Match weight 1:1, peel to sugar |
| Over 24 hours | Diminishing returns, off-flavours develop | Strain at 12–24 hours |

**You know it worked when:** Golden, viscous, intensely aromatic. One teaspoon transforms a glass of sparkling water. Two teaspoons in a vinaigrette makes citrus the lead.

```
technique:extraction applies-to:citrus,cocktail thesaurus:oleo-saccharum
```

---

## THE EVIDENCE — Citrus Flavonoids and the Bioavailability Problem

Xu, Y., He, P., He, B., & Chen, Z. (2025). "Bioactive flavonoids metabolites in citrus species: their potential health benefits and medical potentials." *Frontiers in Pharmacology*, 16, 1552171. DOI: 10.3389/fphar.2025.1552171

Over 250 citrus flavonoid metabolites identified. Naringin (grapefruit, 30–40% of total citrus flavonoids) and hesperidin (orange peel, 20–30%) show antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and hypoglycemic properties in lab studies. The catch: both have poor oral bioavailability. They're converted to their active forms (naringenin, hesperetin) in the gut, but absorption is limited and distribution to target tissues is minimal.

| Flavonoid | Found In | Lab Activity | Real-World Bioavailability |
|-----------|----------|--------------|---------------------------|
| Hesperidin | Orange peel, blood orange pith | Anti-inflammatory, lipid-lowering | Poor — limited GI absorption |
| Naringin | Grapefruit | Antioxidant, hypoglycemic | Poor — requires gut conversion |
| Anthocyanins | Blood orange flesh | Antioxidant | Moderate — but heat-labile |

**What this means in the galley:** Don't sell guests on "superfood" citrus. The compounds are real but the delivery mechanism (eating the fruit) is inefficient. What is real: blood oranges are an excellent source of vitamin C (Tarocco holds the record), the flavour is extraordinary at peak season, and the anthocyanins add genuine visual impact — provided you don't cook them out.

**What this does NOT mean:** That citrus is nutritionally irrelevant. It means the health claims are ahead of the absorption science. Serve it because it tastes right, not because it's medicine.

**Practical note on heat stability:** Blood orange anthocyanins follow first-order degradation kinetics. At 90°C, half-life is under 30 minutes. At refrigerator temperature (5°C), half-life extends to 55–115 days. Acid stabilises the pigments. If you must heat blood orange juice (for a gastrique, say), add vinegar and reduce time to absolute minimum.

```
science:food-chemistry region:mediterranean thesaurus:citrus-flavonoids-bioavailability
```

---

## LITTORAL HERITAGE — Insalata di Arance

*Catania, Syracuse, Sicily*

This salad exists because the Arabs brought citrus trees to Sicily in the 9th century, along with advanced irrigation (the qanat system), sugar, rice, saffron, and pine nuts. They transformed Sicilian cuisine from Roman austerity into something layered, sweet-and-savoury, aromatic. Blood oranges developed their red pigmentation centuries later in the volcanic soils of the Catania plain near Etna — where temperature differentials between warm days and cold nights trigger anthocyanin synthesis.

The traditional preparation: blood oranges sliced crosswise into rounds or supremed. Paper-thin fennel. Sliced red onion (Tropea, when available). Black olives — Nocellara del Belice. Extra virgin olive oil. Salt. Black pepper. Some versions from Syracuse omit fennel. Others add capers from Pantelleria, fresh mint, or anchovies.

| Element | Traditional | Why It Matters |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Blood oranges | Tarocco preferred | Sweetest, highest vitamin C, less bitterness |
| No cooking | Assembled raw | Preserves anthocyanins and vitamin C entirely |
| Fennel | Sliced paper-thin crosswise | Anise crunch bridges sweet and savoury |
| Anchovies (optional) | Laid on top | Glutamate bridges citrus acid and olive oil fat |

**On board:** Transit the Aeolian Islands or anchor off Catania. Morning market. Blood oranges, fennel, olives — all from the same volcanic coast. Assemble at lunch. Tell the guests the Arabs planted these trees a thousand years ago. Four ingredients, one coastline, one civilisation's legacy on a plate.

**Pairing:** Etna Bianco — Graci or Benanti. Carricante grape, mineral, volcanic, same mountain.

```
region:mediterranean heritage:arab-sicilian dish:insalata-di-arance thesaurus:insalata-di-arance
```

---

## THE HORIZON — Khufu's Named Best Restaurant in MENA

*February 3, 2026 · Abu Dhabi*

Khufu's in Giza, Egypt, named No. 1 at the Middle East & North Africa's 50 Best Restaurants 2026 — the first Egyptian restaurant to take the top spot. The ceremony was held at Emirates Palace Mandarin Oriental in Abu Dhabi. Dubai's Kinoya ranked second, Tresind Studio third. Sara Aqel of Dara Dining honoured as MENA's Best Female Chef.

Meanwhile, the Michelin Guide Great Britain & Ireland 2026 ceremony takes place February 9 in Dublin — the first time Ireland has hosted. New stars and Green Stars announced that evening.

Asia's 50 Best will be announced March 25 in Hong Kong — another first for the city.

**Source:** The World's 50 Best Restaurants, February 3, 2026; Michelin Guide, February 2026.

```
news:award region:middle-east thesaurus:khufus-mena-50-best
```

---

## TRADE WINDS — EU ETS Full Phase-In Hits Superyachts

*January 1, 2026 · EU/EEA Ports*

The EU Emissions Trading System reached full implementation on January 1, 2026. Shipping companies must now surrender allowances for **100% of verified CO2 emissions** for all voyages involving EU/EEA ports (up from 70% in 2025). Additionally, **methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O)** are now covered for the first time.

| Detail | Specifics |
|--------|-----------|
| Scope | Vessels over 5,000 GT calling at EU ports |
| Coverage | 100% of emissions between EU ports; 50% on voyages touching non-EU port |
| New gases | CH4 and N2O added to CO2 |
| Private yachts | Most under 5,000 GT with 12 or fewer guests remain outside scope — but don't assume exemption without checking |

**Your move:** If your vessel carries passenger-ship certification or exceeds 5,000 GT, confirm ETS compliance with your management company. If you're below threshold, stay informed — the scope tends to expand. boot Düsseldorf 2026 (17–25 January) reported renewed market confidence in the 24m+ segment despite the regulatory tightening, with over 200,000 visitors and 1,500 exhibitors.

**Source:** EU ETS Directive 2023/959; SuperyachtNews, January 2026; boot Düsseldorf 2026 Market Report.

```
industry:regulation scope:eu thesaurus:eu-ets-2026-full-phase-in
```

---

## THE CLOSE

> The blood orange doesn't know it's red because of a volcano. It just responds to the temperature it was given. The best cooking works the same way — read the conditions, respond honestly, don't force what isn't there. Etna provides the cold nights. You provide the restraint not to cook the colour out.

---

**WEEKLY BRIEF — Week of February 12, 2026**

```
Thread: Red from the volcano
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Shore Larder:    Blood oranges              → blood-orange
The Method:      Oleo-saccharum             → oleo-saccharum
The Evidence:    Citrus flavonoid bioavail.  → citrus-flavonoids-bioavailability
Heritage:        Insalata di arance         → insalata-di-arance
The Horizon:     Khufu's MENA No. 1        → khufus-mena-50-best
Trade Winds:     EU ETS full phase-in       → eu-ets-2026-full-phase-in
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Files: 7 articles in /content/review/
Total: ~2,200 words / 10 min
```

drafts.title: Finish What You Open: The Zero-Leftovers Protocol
drafts.excerpt: *Category: Waste Reduction / Storage / Provisioning* **The Pain:** You open the fridge and find half an onion in cling film turning translucent, a third of a passata bottle growing a skin, two tablespoons of coconut milk in a ramekin covered in foil, and a wedge of lemon drying out next to yesterday's half-avocado now …
drafts.body_md:
## Finish What You Open
*Category: Waste Reduction / Storage / Provisioning*

**The Pain:**
You open the fridge and find half an onion in cling film turning translucent, a third of a passata bottle growing a skin, two tablespoons of coconut milk in a ramekin covered in foil, and a wedge of lemon drying out next to yesterday's half-avocado now the color of a bruise. You wrapped them all with good intentions. Now you're throwing them all away — plus the plastic you used to save them.

**The Protocol:**
1. **Open it, finish it.** If a recipe calls for half a tin of passata, use the full tin — thicken a sauce, build tomorrow's staff meal base, reduce it for pizza sauce. Plan the second use *before* you open the first.
2. **Write tonight's crew meal around today's offcuts.** Half an onion, a pepper end, two carrots past their prime — that's a soup, a stew, a fried rice, a stock. The crew meal is your waste-elimination system, not an afterthought.
3. **Menu forward, not ingredient backward.** When planning the day, scan what's already open in the fridge *first*. Build lunch or crew dinner from there. Provision and prep around what needs finishing, not what's easiest to start fresh.
4. **Kill the cling film reflex.** Before wrapping anything: will I actually use this within 4 hours? If not, use it now or convert it into something stable (stock, purée, dressing, marinade). A half-onion wrapped in plastic is not storage — it's procrastination.
5. **On passage: cook in complete units.** Crossing days multiply the problem. Every half-ingredient becomes inventory to track, rotate, and eventually throw overboard. Provision in exact quantities. Cook in whole tins, whole onions, whole packets. A 14-day Atlantic crossing with half-portions accumulating in the fridge is how you lose control of your galley.

**Why It Works:**
The instinct to save half an ingredient feels efficient. It isn't. It creates cognitive load — you have to remember what's in there, check if it's still good, find a use before it turns. Every wrapped fragment is a micro-decision deferred. Multiply that across a week, and you're spending 15-20 minutes daily just managing the graveyard shelf. The "finish what you open" rule eliminates the decision entirely. There's nothing to check, nothing to remember, nothing to throw away. The fridge stays clean because nothing in it is waiting to die.

**The Proof:**

| Metric | Before (saving halves) | After (finish what you open) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Fridge audit time | 15-20 min/day | 3-5 min/day |
| Food waste (weekly) | 2-3 kg of wrapped fragments | Under 500g (peels, trims only) |
| Cling film / plastic use | 1 roll per 10-day charter | 1 roll per 3-4 weeks |
| "Mystery containers" in fridge | 5-8 at any time | 0-1 |
| Crew meal planning time | 20 min (starting from scratch) | 5 min (built from what's open) |

**Where Most Go Wrong:**
You tell yourself "I'll use that tomorrow." You won't. Tomorrow has its own menu, its own prep list, its own ingredients. That half-tin of coconut milk will sit there for three days until you smell it and bin it. The problem isn't laziness — it's optimism. You're betting your future self will remember and prioritize a ramekin of leftover ingredient over a clean fresh start. Future you has other plans. Use it now.

**The Catch:**
This requires menu flexibility. If the preference sheet locks every meal to a fixed recipe, you lose the ability to absorb offcuts into crew meals or tomorrow's lunch. Works best when crew meals and at least one guest meal per day are chef's choice. On rigid charter menus, adapt by provisioning in exact quantities from the start — which takes more planning upfront but eliminates the problem entirely.

Also: some ingredients genuinely store well halved — hard cheeses, cured meats, dense root vegetables. The protocol targets perishables and opened containers, not everything. Use judgment. But when in doubt: finish it.

drafts.title: To Nurture: Why Littoralicious Exists
drafts.excerpt: *Nutrire* — Latin. To feed. To nourish. To educate. To protect growth. One word that contains everything a chef does and everything this publication exists to be. You feed people. Not just calories — understanding. Not just plates — moments. The act of transforming raw into cooked, of choosing an ingredient because you…
drafts.body_md:
## To Nurture

*Nutrire* — Latin. To feed. To nourish. To educate. To protect growth.

One word that contains everything a chef does and everything this publication exists to be.

You feed people. Not just calories — understanding. Not just plates — moments. The act of transforming raw into cooked, of choosing an ingredient because you know its story, its chemistry, its tradition — that's not service. That's nurturing. And it's the oldest human act there is.

This is what Littoralicious is for.

---

### Where It Starts

A million years ago, on the southern coast of Africa, someone held food over fire and waited. We don't know their name. We know what happened next: [everything](the-first-cook).

Cooking is the foundational technology of civilization. Not the plow, not the wheel, not writing. Fire applied to food. Richard Wrangham's research at Harvard showed that cooking externalized digestion — gelatinizing starches, denaturing proteins — freeing 30-50% more calories from the same food. The human gut shrank. The human brain tripled in size. We traded digestive power for cognitive power, and the currency of that trade was heat.

At [Pinnacle Point](the-first-cook), South Africa, 164,000-year-old shellfish middens sit beside hearth remnants — among the oldest evidence of systematic food gathering on earth. The coast was the first pantry. Shellfish rich in omega-3 DHA fed the very brains that would eventually build language, art, and everything that followed.

The first cook stood beside water. We still do.

---

### What the Shore Teaches

Every ingredient has a story older than any recipe that uses it. We believe a chef who knows that story cooks differently — with more respect, more precision, more wonder.

[**Saffron.**](shore-larder-saffron) 150,000 flowers for one kilogram. Each crocus produces three stigmas, picked by hand before dawn. Every saffron crocus alive today is a clone — a sterile triploid that exists only because humans have propagated it for 3,500 years. In Nuremberg, 1444, the city council passed the Safranschou code: adulterating saffron was punishable by being burned alive alongside your counterfeit goods. A spice worth painting on Minoan palace walls. Worth killing for. And in your galley — worth steeping properly. Steep it. Let it bloom. [The Iranians grind it with a sugar cube for a reason.](shore-larder-saffron)

[**Chocolate.**](shore-larder-chocolat) Not a bean — a seed. 600 volatile flavor compounds, more aromatic complexity than wine. The Maya traded it as currency: a turkey for 100 beans, a tomato for one. The Aztec emperor Montezuma drank 50 cups daily from gold goblets. The original preparation — *xocolatl*, bitter, spiced with chili and vanilla, frothed from height — was never sweet. The Spanish added sugar and hid the recipe from Europe for a century. And inside every piece: anandamide, an endocannabinoid named from the Sanskrit word for *bliss*. [The same receptor system as cannabis. In chocolate.](shore-larder-chocolat)

These are not trivia. They are craft. A chef who knows that crocin is water-soluble (which is why saffron colors rice but turmeric stains it) handles the ingredient differently. A chef who knows that [a single drop of water seizes melted chocolate but a large amount rescues it](shore-larder-chocolat) doesn't panic — they add cream. Knowledge changes the hand.

---

### The Science That Earns Its Place

We publish research. Not opinions, not wellness trends, not what a podcast host learned last week. Peer-reviewed, published, verified findings translated into language a chef can use at the pass.

[The Kuna people](shore-larder-chocolat) of Panama's San Blas Islands drink up to five cups of minimally processed cocoa daily. Their blood pressure does not rise with age — virtually unheard of in any studied population. When Kuna individuals move to Panama City and switch to commercial cocoa, their blood pressure rises within years. A randomized clinical trial of 21,442 participants confirmed cocoa flavanols reduce cardiovascular death by 27%. The mechanism is specific: epicatechin triggers nitric oxide production, relaxing blood vessel walls. Dutch-process alkalization — the treatment that makes cocoa darker and milder — destroys 60-90% of those flavanols.

The science is beautiful. But only when it's honest.

[Saffron and depression.](shore-larder-saffron) Multiple randomized controlled trials have found 30mg/day of saffron extract comparable to fluoxetine for mild-to-moderate depression symptoms. The compounds crocin and safranal modulate serotonin metabolism. Humans have reached for this spice in rituals of comfort for millennia — the chemistry partially explains why.

[82% of superyacht crew](silver-lining-protocol) report low mood or anxiety onboard. We published [the evidence on what works](silver-lining-protocol) — cognitive reframing, mindfulness protocols, emotional regulation — drawn from research on Antarctic stations, submarines, and NASA missions. Not because it was trendy. Because crew members needed it and nobody was writing it.

[Your spice rack contains more documented pharmacology](medicinal-plants-your-galley) than most people realize — and we published what the peer-reviewed literature actually supports, with every limitation named.

This is what evidence looks like when nobody is selling anything.

---

### The Myths We Kill

Not everything you were taught is true. Some of it was purchased.

[**"Breakfast is the most important meal of the day"**](the-evidence-breakfast-propaganda) was written in 1917 by Lenna Frances Cooper in *Good Health* magazine — edited by Dr. John Harvey Kellogg, who manufactured breakfast cereal. In the 1920s, Edward Bernays — Sigmund Freud's nephew, the father of public relations — got 4,500 doctors to endorse bacon and eggs for a pork company. No clinical trial. No control group. One loaded question, multiplied by authority. A 2019 BMJ meta-analysis of 13 randomized controlled trials found no evidence that breakfast promotes weight loss. Breakfast eaters consumed 260 extra calories per day. The "most important meal" was the most successful campaign.

[**In 1967, the Sugar Research Foundation paid Harvard scientists**](the-evidence-follow-the-money) the equivalent of $50,000 to publish a review in the *New England Journal of Medicine* concluding that dietary fat — not sugar — was the primary cause of heart disease. The funding was never disclosed. The lead researcher went on to draft the U.S. government's dietary guidelines. For fifty years, the world ate low-fat, high-sugar food based on research bought and paid for. Published in *JAMA Internal Medicine*, 2016. Not conspiracy. Documented.

Industry-funded nutrition studies produce results favorable to the sponsor [4 to 8 times more often](the-evidence-follow-the-money) than independent research. The supplement industry is worth $177 billion. Your favorite health podcast is reading ads for products that didn't exist three years ago. The podcaster isn't lying — the podcaster believes. That's what makes it work.

We got tired of checking the label on every piece of advice and finding a barcode underneath.

---

### What Lives Here

Littoralicious covers the craft in full — for professional yacht chefs and anyone who believes cooking deserves better than content marketing.

**Shore Larder** — Ingredients with their real stories, real chemistry, real traditions. Not "10 facts about avocado." The compound that matters, the pairing you haven't tried, the mistake you're making, the science that surprises.

**The Method** — One technique, taught like a coach beside you in the galley. Precise. Measurable. With the science named and the myth killed.

**Littoral Heritage** — Coastal dishes with verified origins. A grandmother who happens to have a chemistry degree. Where the dish came from, why it had to exist, what the tradition teaches, why the science proves grandmother was right.

**The Evidence** — Peer-reviewed research translated to the galley. What the study found, what it didn't, who funded it, what to do about it.

**The Blueprint** — Science-backed recipes with elevation tiers, troubleshooting, quick reference cards. Every temperature verified, every technique sourced.

**Signal Fire** — One chef, one idea. Not a profile — a transferable principle you can steal.

**The Horizon** — What happened in the chef world this week, stated plainly. No hagiography.

**Trade Winds** — Industry intelligence. Regulations, certifications, hiring, market shifts. What changed and what to do about it.

**Tight Ship** — Galley efficiency protocols. Problem, fix, proof, catch. Quantified.

**Port Call** — Provisioning intelligence for specific ports. Tested, priced, honest.

**The Locker** — Cuisine-specific pantry deep dives. Named brands, three quality tiers, sourcing matrices.

Every section serves the same purpose: to make you better at your craft, more curious about your ingredients, and more independent in your thinking.

---

### The Architecture of Trust

This publication is free. No subscription. No paywall. No sponsors. No affiliate links. No advertisements. No "premium tier" behind a login.

This is not idealism. It is a quality control decision.

The moment money enters, the question shifts from *"what's true?"* to *"what's true that doesn't upset the sponsor?"* — and that second question, however quietly it operates, bends everything it touches. We'd rather publish less, slower, with fewer resources, and know that every sentence survived a single test: *is this accurate, or is this profitable?*

Every source is named. Every funding conflict is flagged. Every claim is checked against who profits from its being believed. If we don't know something, we say so — and we tell you our confidence level.

This is what independence costs: everything commercial. This is what it earns: your trust.

---

### A Safe Harbour

The sea is full of noise. Marketing that looks like education. Advertising that sounds like independence. Algorithms that reward outrage over accuracy. Everyone selling something — and the better they sell it, the less it feels like selling.

Littoralicious is a harbour.

Come here when you need signal instead of noise. When you want to know why your chocolate seized and the answer isn't trying to sell you a tempering machine. When you want to understand an ingredient the way it deserves — its history, its chemistry, its tradition, its science. When you need someone to check the funding on that study your guest is quoting.

The harbour is open. The harbour is free. And everything in it exists for one reason:

**To nurture.**

To feed your craft. To nourish your curiosity. To protect the growth of knowledge that belongs to everyone who cooks.

We are not humans who learned to cook. We are apes that cooking made human. Every technique, every ingredient, every tradition mapped to a coastline descends from that [first fire beside the water](the-first-cook). This is the craft. It is older than language, older than cities, older than money.

It does not belong behind a paywall. It belongs to you.

---

### Keep the Harbour Open

We don't run ads. We won't start. But harbours need maintenance. If you've found something here worth coming back to:

**Buy us an Espresso Martini.** One drink. That's what it costs to keep independent publishing alive for another week. [Tip here →]

**Support Educated Traveler.** Our sister project — immersive skill experiences around the world, built on the same principle: craft first, commerce never. [educatedtraveler.co →](https://educatedtraveler.co)

**Share one article with one chef.** Someone on another boat, in another galley, who deserves better than sponsored content. That's free, and it's the most powerful thing you can do.

No guilt. No pressure. Everything stays free. The knowledge belongs to the craft, not to us.

---

*Littoralicious. Where the shore meets the truth.*

*To nurture — always.*

drafts.title: Follow the Money: Why the Nutrition Advice You Trust Was Paid For
drafts.excerpt: **The Study:** In 2016, Cristin Kearns, a dentist-turned-researcher at UCSF, published what she found in the archives of a deceased Illinois sugar industry executive: internal documents proving that in 1967, the Sugar Research Foundation (now the Sugar Association) paid three Harvard scientists — including the head of …
drafts.body_md:
## Industry-Funded Nutrition Research Produces Favorable Results Up to 8 Times More Often Than Independent Studies

**The Study:**
In 2016, Cristin Kearns, a dentist-turned-researcher at UCSF, published what she found in the archives of a deceased Illinois sugar industry executive: internal documents proving that in 1967, the Sugar Research Foundation (now the Sugar Association) paid three Harvard scientists — including the head of the nutrition department — the equivalent of $50,000 in today's currency to publish a review in the *New England Journal of Medicine*. The review's conclusion: dietary fat, not sugar, was the primary driver of coronary heart disease. The funding was never disclosed. The lead Harvard researcher, D. Mark Hegsted, went on to draft the U.S. government's first dietary guidelines. For fifty years, the world ate low-fat, high-sugar products based on research bought and paid for by the sugar industry.

This is not conspiracy theory. It was published in *JAMA Internal Medicine* (2016), one of the most rigorous peer-reviewed journals in medicine. The documents are public.

**The Pattern:**
Kearns' finding was dramatic. But the Cochrane Collaboration — the gold standard for systematic evidence review — had already quantified the broader problem. Lundh et al. (2017) analyzed the relationship between industry sponsorship and research outcomes across all medical fields: industry-sponsored studies were significantly more likely to report favorable efficacy results and favorable conclusions than non-industry studies. In nutrition specifically, Lesser et al. (2007) in *PLOS Medicine* found that industry-funded studies were **4 to 8 times** more likely to produce conclusions favorable to the sponsor than independently funded research.

Not sometimes. Not occasionally. Four to eight times.

**The Numbers:**

| Finding | Data | Source |
|---------|------|--------|
| Harvard scientists paid to blame fat, exonerate sugar | $50,000 (1967 dollars), published in *NEJM* | Kearns et al., *JAMA Internal Medicine*, 2016 |
| Industry-funded nutrition studies favoring sponsor | 4-8x more likely than independent studies | Lesser et al., *PLOS Medicine*, 2007 |
| Coca-Cola funded "Global Energy Balance Network" to shift obesity blame from sugar to inactivity | $1.5M to university researchers | O'Connor, *New York Times*, 2015; disclosed funding records |
| Supplement industry global market value | $177 billion (2023) — largely unregulated, most claims untested | Grand View Research; FDA does not require pre-market approval for supplements |
| Percentage of Americans who trust health influencers | 45% trust social media for health advice | IFIC Food & Health Survey, 2023 |

**The Modern Version:**

The laboratory has moved. The mechanism hasn't.

In the 1960s, the sugar industry bought journal papers. Today, the supplement industry buys podcasts. The structure is identical: a trusted voice delivers a narrative that sounds like independence — *they don't want you to know this, the system is broken, here's the real science* — and between the revelations, reads an advertisement for a $79/month electrolyte powder, a greens supplement with 47 ingredients and zero clinical trials at that specific formulation, a "science-backed" sucrose drink that is, chemically, sugar water with salt.

The podcaster is not lying. The podcaster believes. That's what makes it work. The most effective advertising doesn't feel like advertising — it feels like a friend sharing what helped them. The FTC requires disclosure. The disclosure happens. Nobody listens to it. The narrative has already done its work.

This is not cynicism. This is peer-reviewed, documented, reproducible reality. When money enters the room, conclusions tilt toward whoever is paying. Not always. Not every time. But often enough, and systematically enough, that the pattern is no longer debatable.

**What This Means in the Galley:**

Every nutritional claim that reaches you — from a journal, a podcast, a supplier, a guest who "read somewhere that..." — deserves one question before any other: **Who paid for this?**

Not "is this true?" That comes second. First: who funded the research? Who sponsors the show? Who profits if you change your behavior? The answer doesn't automatically invalidate the claim. But it tells you where to apply pressure, where to demand a second source, where to hold the claim against the light and check for fingerprints.

The sugar industry didn't fund bad science. They funded *incomplete* science — studies designed to ask the right question the wrong way, to spotlight one variable while shadowing another. The result looked like knowledge. It was, technically, peer-reviewed. It shaped what the world ate for half a century.

A chef who reads a study funded by the olive oil board saying olive oil is the healthiest fat should not throw the study away. But they should find the study that wasn't funded by the olive oil board and see if it says the same thing. If it does — good. If it doesn't — now you know something.

**What This Does Not Mean:**

Not all industry-funded research is wrong. Some of the best food science comes from companies that make food. The problem isn't funding — it's undisclosed funding, and the systematic tilt that comes with it. Independent research can also be flawed, biased, or poorly designed. The point is not to distrust everything. The point is to check.

And not all free content is trustworthy. Free content funded by advertising has the same structural problem as industry-funded research — the money comes from somewhere, and that somewhere has interests.

**Limitations:**
- The Kearns study examined historical documents; the specific researchers named are deceased and could not respond
- Lesser et al. meta-analysis covers nutrition broadly — not all subcategories show equal bias
- Podcast/influencer research is nascent; most evidence is observational or case-study
- The supplement industry includes legitimate products with genuine evidence alongside unregulated claims — blanket skepticism is as lazy as blanket trust

**Why This Publication Is Free:**

Littoralicious will never run advertisements. Will never accept sponsorship. Will never recommend a product because someone paid for the recommendation. Will never soften a finding because it threatens a revenue stream. There is no revenue stream.

This is not a business model. It's a quality control decision.

The moment money enters, the question changes from "what's true?" to "what's true *that doesn't upset the sponsor?*" — and that second question, however subtly it operates, bends everything it touches. We'd rather publish less, slower, with fewer resources, and be able to look at every sentence and know: nobody bought this.

Every source cited. Every funding conflict flagged. Every claim checked against who profits from it being believed.

Better than power, better than money, better than freedom — give us the truth. We'll do the rest.

**Your Move:**
Next time a podcast tells you something revolutionary about nutrition, pause the episode. Find the study they're citing — if they cite one. Check the funding disclosure. Google the sponsor. Then decide. That's it. Not paranoia. Just literacy.

**Sources:**
- Kearns, C.E., Schmidt, L.A. & Glantz, S.A. (2016). "Sugar Industry and Coronary Heart Disease Research: A Historical Analysis of Internal Industry Documents." *JAMA Internal Medicine*, 176(11), 1680-1685. DOI: 10.1001/jamainternmed.2016.5394
- Lundh, A. et al. (2017). "Industry sponsorship and research outcome." *Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews*, Issue 2. DOI: 10.1002/14651858.MR000033.pub3
- Lesser, L.I. et al. (2007). "Relationship between Funding Source and Conclusion among Nutrition-Related Scientific Articles." *PLOS Medicine*, 4(1), e5. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pmed.0040005
- Ioannidis, J.P.A. (2005). "Why Most Published Research Findings Are False." *PLOS Medicine*, 2(8), e124. DOI: 10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124
- O'Connor, A. (2015). "Coca-Cola Funds Scientists Who Shift Blame for Obesity Away From Bad Diets." *The New York Times*, August 9, 2015.

drafts.title: Breakfast Is the Most Important Meal of the Day' Was Invented by the Men Selling You Breakfast
drafts.excerpt: **The Origin:** You've heard it your entire life. Your mother said it. Your school nurse said it. The cereal box said it. "Breakfast is the most important meal of the day" feels like ancient nutritional wisdom. It is not. It is an advertising slogan, and its authors had names. In 1917, Lenna Frances Cooper published th…
drafts.body_md:
## "Breakfast Is the Most Important Meal of the Day" Has No Scientific Basis — It Was a Marketing Slogan Created by Cereal Manufacturers

**The Origin:**
You've heard it your entire life. Your mother said it. Your school nurse said it. The cereal box said it. "Breakfast is the most important meal of the day" feels like ancient nutritional wisdom. It is not. It is an advertising slogan, and its authors had names.

In 1917, Lenna Frances Cooper published the phrase in *Good Health* magazine — a publication edited by Dr. John Harvey Kellogg, co-founder of the Kellogg Company, which manufactured and sold breakfast cereal. The statement was never tested. Never peer-reviewed. Never compared against evidence. It was written in a magazine owned by a man selling the product being promoted.

Then it got worse.

In the 1920s, the Beechnut Packing Company — America's largest pork processor — hired Edward Bernays to increase bacon sales. Bernays was Sigmund Freud's nephew and widely considered the father of modern public relations. His method: he asked a physician whether a "hearty breakfast" was preferable to a light one. The physician agreed it was. Bernays then sent that statement to 5,000 doctors, asking if they concurred. 4,500 signed. The result was published across newspapers as medical consensus: doctors recommend bacon and eggs for breakfast.

No clinical trial. No control group. No peer review. One loaded question, multiplied by authority.

This is the origin of your breakfast habit.

**The Study:**
In 2019, Sievert et al. published a systematic review and meta-analysis in the *British Medical Journal* — 13 randomized controlled trials examining the effect of eating breakfast on body weight and daily energy intake. This was not observational data. These were controlled experiments.

**Key Finding:**
There was no evidence that eating breakfast promotes weight loss or that skipping breakfast leads to weight gain. Breakfast eaters consumed on average **260 additional calories per day** without any compensatory metabolic benefit.

**The Numbers:**

| Claim | What Industry Said | What the Evidence Shows | Source |
|-------|-------------------|------------------------|--------|
| "Breakfast prevents obesity" | Repeated by Kellogg's-funded studies for decades | No significant effect on weight loss in RCTs | Sievert et al., *BMJ*, 2019 |
| "Skipping breakfast slows metabolism" | Widely promoted in cereal marketing | Resting metabolic rate not significantly different between eaters and skippers | Dhurandhar et al., *AJCN*, 2014 |
| "Breakfast improves cognitive performance" | Basis of school breakfast program advocacy | Majority of supporting studies funded by cereal industry; independent studies show mixed results | Adolphus et al., *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience*, 2013 (Kellogg's funded) |
| Average extra daily calories from breakfast | — | +260 kcal/day with no metabolic offset | Sievert et al., *BMJ*, 2019 |
| "Doctors recommend a hearty breakfast" | Bacon and eggs, 1920s newspaper campaign | PR campaign by Edward Bernays for Beechnut Packing Co., not medical research | Bernays, E. (1928). *Propaganda*. |

**The Pattern You Recognize:**

Notice the architecture. It repeats:

1. A company that sells a product funds a study about that product.
2. The study finds the product is beneficial.
3. The finding enters public health messaging.
4. The messaging becomes "common knowledge."
5. Common knowledge becomes "what your mother told you."
6. Nobody traces it back.

Kellogg's has funded breakfast research for over a century. General Mills, Quaker Oats, Nestlé — the same. A 2016 analysis by Chartres et al. in *PLOS ONE* found that food industry-funded research was more likely to report outcomes favorable to the sponsor than independently funded studies. This isn't Kellogg's commissioning fake data. It's subtler: they fund questions designed to produce useful answers. "Does breakfast improve morning alertness?" (yes, because you ate — this applies to any meal). Not: "Is breakfast more important than lunch or dinner?" (no evidence it is).

The question determines the answer. The funder determines the question.

**What Your Guest "Knows":**

This matters because it walks aboard your yacht every charter.

The guest who wants egg whites and avocado toast at 07:00 because "breakfast is essential." The guest who feels guilty sleeping until 10:00 and asks for a late breakfast "because I know I should eat in the morning." The charter preference sheet that says "healthy breakfast, no skipping."

These beliefs feel personal. They are industrial. A century of cereal marketing, processed through public health messaging, absorbed as identity.
